questions.question: A fintech company is launching a GenAI-powered "Spending Analyzer" feature. The application uses a Large Language Model (LLM) on Amazon Bedrock to explain transaction history to users. The project has two critical governance requirements:Compliance: The model must strictly refuse to answer any questions related to "Tax Advice" or "Legal Liability," as the company is not licensed to provide these services.Agility: The non-technical Product Team needs the ability to update the "Persona" and "Output Format" (e.g., switching from paragraphs to bullet points) of the analyzer weekly without requiring the engineering team to deploy new application code.Which combination of AWS GenAI features should the developer implement to meet these requirements? (Choose two)
options: Create an Amazon Bedrock Guardrail. Configure a "Denied Topics" policy that explicitly defines "Tax Advice" and "Legal Advice" with description examples. Attach this guardrail to the model invocation to intercept and block restricted queries.

Implement Amazon Bedrock Prompt Management. Create a parameterized prompt template that defines the System Prompt (Persona) and output structure. Grant the Product Team permissions to iterate, test, and version the prompt within the Bedrock console.

Use AWS Systems Manager Parameter Store to hold the System Prompt string. Update the backend Lambda function to fetch the latest prompt version on every invocation. Give the Product Team access to the Systems Manager console to edit the string.
Fine-tune the Foundation Model using Amazon SageMaker JumpStart on a dataset of "Refusal" examples. Train the model to recognize tax-related questions and output a standard disclaimer.

Implement a post-processing AWS Lambda function. Use a keyword list (e.g., "tax", "audit", "lawyer") to scan the generated text. If a restricted keyword is found, overwrite the response with a hardcoded error message.
questions.explain: CORRECT: Create an Amazon Bedrock Guardrail. Configure a "Denied Topics" policy that explicitly defines "Tax Advice" and "Legal Advice" with description examples. Attach this guardrail to the model invocation to intercept and block restricted queries. This addresses the Compliance requirement using the "Responsible AI" tool mentioned in the skill. Amazon Bedrock Guardrails allows you to define specific "Denied Topics" (not just PII or toxicity). The guardrail intercepts the input/output independently of the model's training, providing a deterministic safety layer that satisfies the legal requirement better than prompting alone. CORRECT: Implement Amazon Bedrock Prompt Management. Create a parameterized prompt template that defines the System Prompt (Persona) and output structure. Grant the Product Team permissions to iterate, test, and version the prompt within the Bedrock console. This addresses the Agility requirement. Amazon Bedrock Prompt Management is the specific feature designed to decouple prompt engineering from application code. It provides a UI for non-developers (Product Team) to manage, version, and test prompts ("templates," "role definitions"). The application simply calls the API referencing the PromptIdentifier, allowing the logic to change without a code deploy. INCORRECT: Use AWS Systems Manager Parameter Store to hold the System Prompt string. Update the backend Lambda function to fetch the latest prompt version on every invocation. Give the Product Team access to the Systems Manager console to edit the string. While this is a valid "Configuration" pattern, it is not the specialized GenAI tool for the job. Parameter Store lacks the "Prompt Playground" features (testing, model comparison, variable interpolation visualization) that Bedrock Prompt Management provides. For the exam, always prefer the service purpose-built for the task (Prompt Management) over general infrastructure configuration tools. INCORRECT: Fine-tune the Foundation Model using Amazon SageMaker JumpStart on a dataset of "Refusal" examples. Train the model to recognize tax-related questions and output a standard disclaimer. Fine-tuning is expensive, slow, and requires significant data science expertise. It is overkill for simply blocking a topic. Furthermore, fine-tuning does not guarantee refusals (the model can still be jailbroken), whereas Guardrails provide a hard filter. INCORRECT: Implement a post-processing AWS Lambda function. Use a keyword list (e.g., "tax", "audit", "lawyer") to scan the generated text. If a restricted keyword is found, overwrite the response with a hardcoded error message. Keyword filtering is brittle. It leads to false positives (e.g., "I paid my tax bill" is a valid transaction description) and false negatives (synonyms). It is not an "effective model instruction framework" compared to the semantic understanding of Bedrock Guardrails.

questions.question: An insurance company is building a generative AI chatbot to assist customers with filing claims. The business requirement states that if a user submits a vague request (e.g., "I had an accident"), the system must not hallucinate a response or ask generic questions. Instead, it must accurately identify the specific claim type (Auto, Home, or Life) and then enter a guided, multi-turn dialogue to collect missing critical details (Date of Incident, Policy Number) before generating a final confirmation. The architecture must be serverless and maintain the state of the conversation flow to track which details have already been collected.Which architectural design meets these requirements?
options: Use Amazon Comprehend to classify the user's initial intent and extract available entities. Pass the results to an AWS Step Functions state machine that orchestrates a "Slot Filling" workflow. Configure the state machine to branch based on the claim type and loop through "Clarification" states to prompt the user for missing information until the state payload is complete.

Use Amazon Textract to parse the user's text input. Store the raw text in Amazon DynamoDB. Configure a DynamoDB Stream to trigger a Lambda function that passes the entire conversation history to Amazon Bedrock with a system prompt instructing it to "ask for missing details."

Deploy the Foundation Model on Amazon SageMaker with a custom inference script. Inside the script, implement a Python while loop that checks for keywords like "Auto" or "Home." If the keywords are missing, return a hardcoded string asking for clarification. Keep the loop running until the user responds.

Use Amazon Lex to handle the intent recognition. Configure the fulfillment code hook to call Amazon OpenSearch Service to retrieve similar past claims. Pass the most similar claim to Amazon Bedrock to use as a few-shot example for generating the final response.
questions.explain: CORRECT: Use Amazon Comprehend to classify the user's initial intent and extract available entities. Pass the results to an AWS Step Functions state machine that orchestrates a "Slot Filling" workflow. Configure the state machine to branch based on the claim type and loop through "Clarification" states to prompt the user for missing information until the state payload is complete. This answer explicitly combines the tools mentioned in the skill examples for their intended purposes. Amazon Comprehend is used for Intent Recognition (classifying "I had an accident" -> Auto_Claim_Intent). AWS Step Functions is used for Clarification Workflows . Step Functions is the ideal service for "State Management" in a multi-turn conversation. It can maintain the "State Payload" (e.g., {"PolicyNumber": "123", "Date": null}) and execute decision logic (e.g., Choice: If Date is Null -> Go to State "AskDate"). This ensures a deterministic, guided interaction to improve user experience. Disclaimer: This diagram is a conceptual visualization designed to illustrate the specific exam logic. It abstracts certain production details to focus on the correct answer. INCORRECT: Use Amazon Textract to parse the user's text input. Store the raw text in Amazon DynamoDB. Configure a DynamoDB Stream to trigger a Lambda function that passes the entire conversation history to Amazon Bedrock with a system prompt instructing it to "ask for missing details." Amazon Textract is for extracting text from documents/images (OCR), not for parsing raw string inputs from a chat interface. Additionally, while DynamoDB is good for history, relying on a Stream for real-time conversational flow introduces unnecessary latency and complexity compared to a direct orchestration workflow. INCORRECT: Deploy the Foundation Model on Amazon SageMaker with a custom inference script. Inside the script, implement a Python while loop that checks for keywords like "Auto" or "Home." If the keywords are missing, return a hardcoded string asking for clarification. Keep the loop running until the user responds. This is a poor architectural pattern. Keeping a Python loop running in an inference script implies a long-running synchronous process that blocks resources and costs money while waiting for user input. It is not scalable or serverless. INCORRECT: Use Amazon Lex to handle the intent recognition. Configure the fulfillment code hook to call Amazon OpenSearch Service to retrieve similar past claims. Pass the most similar claim to Amazon Bedrock to use as a few-shot example for generating the final response. While Amazon Lex is a valid tool for intent recognition, this solution focuses on retrieval (RAG) rather than the requested Clarification Workflow to collect missing info. Fetching "similar past claims" does not help the system ask the specific question "What is your policy number?" for this current user.

questions.question: A large banking institution is scaling its use of Generative AI across multiple departments (Marketing, Fraud, Customer Support). The Chief Risk Officer (CRO) has identified two critical governance gaps:Inconsistency: Different teams are using slightly different versions of the "Standard Banking Tone" system prompt, leading to brand misalignment.Lack of Oversight: There is no audit trail to determine who modified a prompt or which specific prompt version was used to generate a loan denial explanation on a specific date.Which combination of actions should the GenAI Developer take to implement a robust governance system? (Choose two)
options: Migrate all hardcoded prompts to Amazon Bedrock Prompt Management. Create a single "Banking Standard" prompt template with placeholders (variables) for department-specific context. Enforce the use of specific prompt versions via the API.

Enable Amazon Bedrock Model Invocation Logging and configure the destination as Amazon CloudWatch Logs. Create a metric filter to track the usage of specific prompt identifiers and retention policies to meet audit requirements.
Store all prompts as text files in an Amazon S3 bucket with Versioning enabled. Use Amazon EventBridge to trigger an AWS Lambda function every time a prompt file is modified to run a spell check.

Use AWS Config to monitor the Amazon Bedrock service. Create a custom Config Rule that scans the application code in AWS CodeCommit to detect if any developers are hardcoding prompts instead of fetching them from the database.
Implement Amazon Inspector to scan the prompt text for sensitive data (PII) before it is sent to the model. Generate a finding in AWS Security Hub if the prompt contains account numbers.
questions.explain: CORRECT: Migrate all hardcoded prompts to Amazon Bedrock Prompt Management. Create a single "Banking Standard" prompt template with placeholders (variables) for department-specific context. Enforce the use of specific prompt versions via the API. This addresses the Inconsistency gap. Amazon Bedrock Prompt Management is the managed service designed to centralize prompt assets. By using parameterized templates (variables) and versioning , the organization ensures that all teams use the approved "Standard Banking Tone" while injecting their specific data dynamically. This eliminates prompt drift. Disclaimer: This diagram is a conceptual visualization designed to illustrate the specific exam logic. It abstracts certain production details to focus on the correct answer. CORRECT: Enable Amazon Bedrock Model Invocation Logging and configure the destination as Amazon CloudWatch Logs. Create a metric filter to track the usage of specific prompt identifiers and retention policies to meet audit requirements. This addresses the Lack of Oversight gap regarding "which prompt was used." Model Invocation Logging captures the full request metadata (including the prompt payload and version) and writes it to Amazon CloudWatch Logs (or S3). This provides the forensic audit trail required to prove exactly what input generated the loan denial on a specific date. Disclaimer: This diagram is a conceptual visualization designed to illustrate the specific exam logic. It abstracts certain production details to focus on the correct answer. INCORRECT: Store all prompts as text files in an Amazon S3 bucket with Versioning enabled. Use Amazon EventBridge to trigger an AWS Lambda function every time a prompt file is modified to run a spell check. While S3 versioning is a valid storage mechanism, it lacks the native GenAI lifecycle features of Bedrock Prompt Management (testing, playground integration, variable interpolation). It is a "raw storage" solution rather than a "governance system." Spell checking does not solve the brand consistency or audit issues described. INCORRECT: Use AWS Config to monitor the Amazon Bedrock service. Create a custom Config Rule that scans the application code in AWS CodeCommit to detect if any developers are hardcoding prompts instead of fetching them from the database. AWS Config is for infrastructure resource configuration (e.g., "Is the bucket encrypted?"). It is not designed to scan source code in CodeCommit repositories to analyze logic or text strings. This is the wrong tool for the job. INCORRECT: Implement Amazon Inspector to scan the prompt text for sensitive data (PII) before it is sent to the model. Generate a finding in AWS Security Hub if the prompt contains account numbers. While protecting PII is critical, this solution addresses Security/Privacy , not the specific Governance/Consistency gaps (Tone inconsistency and Version auditing) requested in the scenario.

questions.question: A Machine Learning Engineer has deployed the Meta Llama 3.1 8B Instruct model to a real-time inference endpoint using Amazon SageMaker JumpStart. The engineer is writing a Python client application using boto3to generate text responses from this endpoint.When calling the invoke_endpoint API, which JSON structure must be used in the Bodyparameter to correctly format the inference request?
options: Construct a JSON object with a prompt key containing the input text and a max_tokens_to_sample key for the generation limit. Set the Content-Type header to application/json .

Construct a JSON object with an inputs key containing the input text (or chat template string) and a parameters key containing a dictionary of generation settings like max_new_tokens and temperature . Set the Content-Type header to application/json .
Construct a JSON object with an inputText key for the user query and a textGenerationConfig object for parameters. Set the Content-Type header to application/json .
Construct a CSV-formatted string where the first column is the input prompt and subsequent columns represent generation parameters. Set the Content-Type header to text/csv .
questions.explain: CORRECT: Construct a JSON object with an inputs key containing the input text... and a parameters key... SageMaker JumpStart models, particularly those running on Hugging Face Text Generation Inference (TGI) or Deep Java Library (DJL) containers (like Llama 3.1), typically expect a JSON payload with an inputs string and an optional parameters dictionary. Example Payload: JSON { "inputs": "<|begin_of_text|><|start_header_id|>user<|end_header_id|>...\n\n", "parameters": { "max_new_tokens": 256, "temperature": 0.6, "top_p": 0.9 } } INCORRECT: Construct a JSON object with a prompt key containing the input text and a max_tokens_to_sample key... This schema is characteristic of the legacy Amazon Bedrock API for Anthropic Claude 2.x models (which used prompt and max_tokens_to_sample). It does not match the standard schema used by the Hugging Face/DJL containers that power Llama 3 models on SageMaker JumpStart. INCORRECT: Construct a JSON object with an inputText key for the user query and a textGenerationConfig object... This schema is specific to Amazon Titan models on Amazon Bedrock. SageMaker endpoints do not natively use this schema unless you have deployed a custom container explicitly designed to mimic it. INCORRECT: Construct a CSV-formatted string where the first column is the input prompt... While text/csv is the standard input format for legacy SageMaker built-in algorithms (like XGBoost or Linear Learner) for tabular data, it is not used for Large Language Models (LLMs) on JumpStart, which require JSON payloads to handle complex text inputs and hyperparameters.

questions.question: A fintech enterprise is building a "Financial Concierge" feature using Generative AI. The system is designed as a multi-agent architecture consisting of three specialized autonomous agents: a Mortgage Advisor, an Investment Planner, and a Tax Consultant. Each agent utilizes distinct Action Groups and foundation models optimized for their specific domain.Users frequently switch topics during a conversation (e.g., "Given the mortgage rate you just quoted, how does that affect my investment liquidity?"). Currently, the agents operate in silos, forcing users to repeat information (such as income or risk tolerance) when the conversation routes to a different agent.As a GenAI Developer, how should you implement the architecture to ensure seamless state management and memory retention across these specialized agents?
options: Deploy the agents as a Step Functions State Machine. Use the ResultPath and InputPath parameters to pass the JSON transcript of the conversation sequentially from one agent to the next, enforcing a rigid linear workflow for every user interaction.

Implement a Supervisor Agent using Amazon Bedrock Agents with multi-agent collaboration enabled. Register the Mortgage, Investment, and Tax agents as collaborator agents. Configure the Supervisor to maintain the conversation history and route relevant context to the appropriate collaborator agent based on the user's intent.

Create a single Amazon DynamoDB table to store the conversation state. configure each Agent's Lambda executor to query this table for the entire chat history before generating a response, and update the table with the new turn after execution.

Merge all functionalities into a single monolithic Amazon Bedrock Agent with one large system prompt that includes instructions for all three domains. Use a single Action Group containing all the API definitions for mortgages, investments, and taxes to avoid the need for state sharing.
questions.explain: CORRECT: Implement a Supervisor Agent using Amazon Bedrock Agents with multi-agent collaboration enabled. Register the Mortgage, Investment, and Tax agents as collaborator agents. Configure the Supervisor to maintain the conversation history and route relevant context to the appropriate collaborator agent based on the user's intent. Amazon Bedrock Agents supports multi-agent collaboration (often referred to as agent squads), where a specialized Supervisor Agent routes requests to other Collaborator Agents . The Supervisor retains the session state (memory of the conversation) and passes the relevant context and history to the specialized agents when they are invoked. This allows the Investment Planner to "know" the mortgage rate quoted by the Mortgage Advisor earlier in the session without the user repeating it. This is the native, managed way to handle state in autonomous multi-agent systems on AWS. Disclaimer: This diagram is a conceptual visualization designed to illustrate the specific exam logic. It abstracts certain production details to focus on the correct answer. INCORRECT: Deploy the agents as a Step Functions State Machine. Use the ResultPath and InputPath parameters to pass the JSON transcript of the conversation sequentially from one agent to the next... While Step Functions is excellent for deterministic workflows, it does not provide the autonomous decision-making required for a conversational agent where the user might switch topics unpredictably. A linear state machine forces a rigid order (e.g., Mortgage -> Investment -> Tax), which fails to handle dynamic, user-driven context switching described in the scenario. INCORRECT: Create a single Amazon DynamoDB table to store the conversation state. configure each Agent's Lambda executor to query this table... This is a manual "do-it-yourself" approach to state management. While technically possible, it introduces significant latency and complexity (retrieving and injecting full history into the prompt manually). It ignores the built-in memory and session management capabilities of Amazon Bedrock Agents and does not leverage the native multi-agent routing features. INCORRECT: Merge all functionalities into a single monolithic Amazon Bedrock Agent with one large system prompt... This contradicts the requirement for a "multi-agent architecture" and the use of "specialized agents" mentioned in the scenario. A monolithic agent with a massive system prompt and too many diverse tools is prone to confusion, hallucination, and hitting context window limits. It does not demonstrate the skill of developing multi-agent systems with appropriate memory/state handling.

refs.label: Use multi-agent collaboration with Amazon Bedrock Agents

questions.question: A utility company is developing a "Grid Balancing Assistant" to automate decisions on activating backup gas turbines. To make a safe and accurate Go/No-Go decision, the AI must follow a strict multi-step protocol:Retrieve real-time weather forecasts for the region.Calculate expected solar output based on cloud cover data.Query the SCADA system for current battery storage levels.Compare total projected supply against demand to determine if the turbine is needed.Initial tests using a single prompt reveal that the model frequently hallucinates the solar calculation or ignores the battery storage data entirely.Which architectural implementations should a GenAI Developer use to enforce structured reasoning and ensure the model accurately executes this complex workflow? (Select two)
options: Use AWS Step Functions to orchestrate a Chain of Thought (CoT) workflow. Design a state machine where sequential states prompt the Foundation Model (FM) to perform one specific task at a time (e.g., "Analyze Weather," then "Calculate Solar Output"). Pass the validated output of each state as context into the next, culminating in a final state that makes the Go/No-Go decision based on the aggregated data.

Implement a ReAct (Reasoning and Acting) pattern. Configure a system prompt that instructs the model to generate a "Thought" followed by an "Action" (tool call) for each step of the analysis. Use a compute layer to execute the specific tool (Weather API, Calculation Engine, SCADA DB) and feed the result back to the model as an "Observation," repeating this loop until the model reaches a final conclusion.

Ingest the historical weather logs, solar output tables, and battery manuals into an Amazon Bedrock Knowledge Base. Use the RetrieveAndGenerate API to query this knowledge base with the prompt "Should we activate the turbine?", relying on the semantic search to find similar past scenarios to guide the model's decision.

Use Amazon SageMaker JumpStart to fine-tune a Llama model on a dataset of 10,000 historical operator logs. Deploy this fine-tuned model to a SageMaker endpoint and use a high temperature setting (e.g., 0.9) to encourage creative problem-solving when generating the decision.

Deploy the model behind Amazon API Gateway with a caching policy enabled. Use Amazon Bedrock Guardrails with a "Denied Topics" filter to block the model from generating any response that suggests a power outage, forcing it to choose the "Activate Turbine" option more frequently to ensure grid stability.
questions.explain: CORRECT: Use AWS Step Functions to orchestrate a Chain of Thought (CoT) workflow... This option directly addresses the skill of "breaking down" complex problems. By using AWS Step Functions, the developer can decompose the complex task into discrete, manageable steps. This enforces a "Chain of Thought" structure where the model cannot skip steps (like ignoring battery levels) because the workflow explicitly mandates that state execution before proceeding to the final decision. Disclaimer: This diagram is a conceptual visualization designed to illustrate the specific exam logic. It abstracts certain production details to focus on the correct answer. CORRECT: Implement a ReAct (Reasoning and Acting) pattern... The ReAct pattern (Reasoning + Acting) is specifically designed to solve complex problems by interleaving reasoning (Thoughts) with tool execution (Actions). By implementing this loop (either via code or Step Functions), the system allows the model to dynamically gather the necessary data (Weather, Solar, Battery) and reason about it iteratively, rather than trying to solve everything in one pass. This aligns perfectly with the exam guide's mention of "implement ReAct patterns." Disclaimer: This diagram is a conceptual visualization designed to illustrate the specific exam logic. It abstracts certain production details to focus on the correct answer. INCORRECT: Ingest the historical weather logs... into an Amazon Bedrock Knowledge Base... While RAG (Retrieval Augmented Generation) is useful for fetching static information, it is not the primary solution for reasoning through a live, multi-step decision process that requires real-time calculation and tool execution. RAG would find past documents, but it wouldn't inherently force the model to perform the specific live checks and math required for the current situation. INCORRECT: Use Amazon SageMaker JumpStart to fine-tune... and use a high temperature setting... Fine-tuning helps with domain vocabulary and style, but it does not inherently solve the problem of reasoning through a complex, multi-step logic puzzle with external dependencies. Furthermore, a high temperature (0.9) adds randomness, which is the opposite of what is needed for a strict, deterministic safety protocol like grid balancing. INCORRECT: Deploy the model behind Amazon API Gateway... Use Amazon Bedrock Guardrails... Guardrails are for safety and filtering content (Task 3.1), not for enabling complex problem-solving logic. Blocking "power outage" topics does not help the model perform the correct math or data lookups required to make the decision.

refs.label: Enhance model responses with model reasoning
Orchestrate generative AI workflows with Amazon Bedrock and AWS Step Functions

questions.question: A logistics company has deployed an autonomous GenAI agent responsible for rescheduling delayed shipments. The agent uses tools to query driver availability and update delivery routes in a DynamoDB table.During a recent outage, the agent entered a failure loop: it repeatedly called the "GetDriverAvailability" tool for the same Order ID thousands of times, incurring significant API costs. Additionally, the security team is concerned that the agent currently has the ability to modify routes for "Class-A Restricted" government cargo, which should be strictly off-limits to AI automation.Which combination of mechanisms should the developer implement to safeguard the agent's behavior and enforce these constraints? (Select two)
options: Configure the agent's execution role with an AWS IAM policy that uses condition keys to restrict dynamodb:Update Item actions. Allow updates only on resources where the cargo_type tag is set to standard , effectively blocking access to any items tagged as restricted.

Wrap the agent's orchestration logic in an AWS Step Functions State Machine. Implement a "Circuit Breaker" pattern using a Pass state with a counter or a DynamoDB lock item. Define a specific stopping condition that transitions the workflow to a "Fail" state if the number of tool retries for a single Order ID exceeds a defined threshold (e.g., 5 attempts).

Implement Amazon Bedrock Guardrails with a "Word Filter" configured to block the specific Order IDs that caused the infinite loop. Update the filter dynamically using a Lambda function whenever a new loop is detected.

Increase the timeout setting on the AWS Lambda function that hosts the agent logic to 15 minutes. This ensures that even if the agent loops repeatedly, the function has enough time to eventually resolve the schedule without crashing.

Add a "System Instruction" to the agent's prompt that says: "Do not call the driver API more than once per order and do not touch government cargo." Rely on the Foundation Model's reasoning capabilities to adhere to these instructions during execution.
questions.explain: CORRECT: Wrap the agent's orchestration logic in an AWS Step Functions State Machine. Implement a "Circuit Breaker" pattern... This option directly addresses the "infinite loop" problem using Step Functions to implement stopping conditions . By tracking the number of retries/loops in the state machine and enforcing a hard stop (circuit breaker), the developer prevents runaway API calls and costs. Disclaimer: This diagram is a conceptual visualization designed to illustrate the specific exam logic. It abstracts certain production details to focus on the correct answer. CORRECT: Configure the agent's execution role with an AWS IAM policy that uses condition keys to restrict dynamodb:UpdateItem actions... This option addresses the "unauthorized access" problem by using IAM policies to enforce resource boundaries . By applying condition keys based on resource tags (cargo_type: standard), the developer ensures that the agent physically cannot modify restricted records at the infrastructure level, regardless of what the model "decides" to do. INCORRECT: Implement Amazon Bedrock Guardrails with a "Word Filter"... Guardrails are for content safety (toxicity, PII, specific topics), not for managing execution flow or logic loops. Dynamically updating word filters to block Order IDs is a reactive, unscalable, and incorrect use of the service. INCORRECT: Increase the timeout setting on the AWS Lambda function... Increasing the timeout would likely make the problem worse (generating more costs) by allowing the loop to run longer. Skill 2.1.3 mentions Lambda timeouts as a mechanism to limit execution (fail fast), not extend it. INCORRECT: Add a "System Instruction" to the agent's prompt... While good prompting is helpful, it is not a "safeguarded workflow" mechanism for critical operational controls. Models can hallucinate or ignore instructions. The exam guide distinguishes between "Prompt Engineering" and "Safeguarded AI Workflows". For infinite loops and security boundaries, infrastructure-level controls (Step Functions, IAM) are required.

refs.label: Circuit breaker pattern
Using attribute-based access control with DynamoDB

questions.question: A healthcare startup is building a "Virtual Triage" application. The system performs three distinct functions for every patient interaction:Vitals Extraction: Parses simple numerical values (Blood Pressure, Heart Rate) from nurse notes.Medical History Review: Summarizes over 100 pages of attached PDF medical records to identify pre-existing conditions.Diagnostic Reasoning: Analyzes the current symptoms against a complex medical knowledge base to suggest potential rare diseases.The current prototype uses a single, ultra-large Foundation Model (FM) for all tasks. However, the system is too slow for the simple extraction task and incurs prohibitive costs when processing the large PDF history files.As a GenAI Developer, which model coordination strategy should you implement to optimize performance and cost?
options: Deploy three identical replicas of the ultra-large FM on Amazon SageMaker endpoints. Use an Application Load Balancer (ALB) to distribute the incoming requests using a round-robin algorithm to reduce latency and avoid bottlenecks.

Implement a "Model Router" pattern. Configure a routing layer to send "Vitals Extraction" to a lightweight, low-latency model (e.g., Amazon Titan Text Express). Route "Medical History Review" to a cost-effective model with a large context window (e.g., Anthropic Claude Haiku). Route "Diagnostic Reasoning" to a high-intelligence model (e.g., Anthropic Claude 3.5 Sonnet). Aggregate the results in the application logic.

Use Amazon Bedrock Guardrails to dynamically adjust the inference parameters of the single FM. Set the temperature to 0.0 for Vitals Extraction to speed up generation, and increase top-k for Medical History Review to process more tokens per second.

Fine-tune a single Amazon Titan Image Generator model using SageMaker JumpStart to handle all three modalities. Use the "image-to-text" capability to read the PDFs and the "text-generation" capability for the reasoning, consolidating all tasks into one multi-modal endpoint.
questions.explain: CORRECT: Implement a "Model Router" pattern. Configure a routing layer to send "Vitals Extraction" to a lightweight, low-latency model... This solution effectively uses specialized FMs to perform complex tasks . By implementing a model selection framework (the router), the developer assigns each task to the model best suited for it: a small, fast model for simple extraction (optimization for speed/cost), a high-context model for large documents (optimization for context/cost), and a "smart" model for complex reasoning (optimization for capability). Disclaimer: This diagram is a conceptual visualization designed to illustrate the specific exam logic. It abstracts certain production details to focus on the correct answer. INCORRECT: Deploy three identical replicas of the ultra-large FM on Amazon SageMaker endpoints... This approach addresses concurrency/throughput (Scaling Out), but it does not optimize for cost or capability efficiency. Using an ultra-large model for simple number extraction remains wasteful and slow regardless of how many replicas exist. It fails to utilize "specialized FMs." INCORRECT: Use Amazon Bedrock Guardrails to dynamically adjust the inference parameters... Guardrails are for safety, not parameter tuning. Furthermore, adjusting temperature or top-k does not significantly change the speed or cost of a large model's processing of input tokens, nor does it make a general model better at handling massive context windows compared to switching to a specialized architecture. INCORRECT: Fine-tune a single Amazon Titan Image Generator model... This suggests using an Image Generator model for primarily text-based reasoning tasks, which is architecturally incorrect. Even with multimodal capabilities, trying to force one model to do everything via fine-tuning contradicts the "model coordination" and "specialized FMs" approach required by the scenario.

questions.question: A legal technology firm is building an "AI Associate" to draft complex commercial contracts. Due to the high liability of legal errors, the system requires a "Human-in-the-Loop" workflow where a senior attorney must review and approve every draft before it is finalized. Additionally, the firm wants to capture the specific edits made by attorneys during this review to continuously improve the model's future performance.Which combination of architectural patterns should the GenAI Developer implement to achieve these goals? (Select two)
options: Implement an AWS Step Functions State Machine that generates the initial draft and then transitions to a task state using the .waitForTaskToken callback pattern. This pauses the workflow and sends a notification to the attorney's portal. The workflow resumes only when the attorney submits their approval via an API call that returns the token.

Create a "Feedback" resource on Amazon API Gateway that accepts a payload containing the original AI draft, the attorney's edited version, and a quality rating. Configure an AWS Lambda integration to store these correction pairs in an Amazon S3 bucket to serve as a dataset for future fine-tuning or few-shot prompting examples.

Configure Amazon Bedrock Guardrails with a "PII Redaction" filter. Set the filter to "Block" mode to ensure that no client names are visible to the AI model, and automatically route the request to a human support agent if a blocked entity is detected.

Use Amazon CloudWatch Synthetics to run "Canary" scripts that simulate a human attorney reviewing a document every 5 minutes. Configure an alarm to trigger if the AI model's response time exceeds 2 seconds, ensuring the review interface remains responsive.

Deploy the Foundation Model on Amazon SageMaker Asynchronous Inference. Configure an auto-scaling policy that scales the instance count based on the metric ApproximateNumberOfMessagesVisible in the review queue, ensuring enough compute capacity is available when attorneys are active.
questions.explain: CORRECT: Implement an AWS Step Functions State Machine that generates the initial draft and then transitions to a task state using the .waitForTaskToken callback pattern... This option directly addresses the need for a review and approval process orchestrated by Step Functions. The .waitForTaskToken pattern is the standard AWS mechanism for pausing an automated workflow to wait for an external human action (approval) before proceeding. This creates a collaborative system where the AI starts the work and the human completes the validation. Disclaimer: This diagram is a conceptual visualization designed to illustrate the specific exam logic. It abstracts certain production details to focus on the correct answer. CORRECT: Create a "Feedback" resource on Amazon API Gateway that accepts a payload containing the original AI draft, the attorney's edited version, and a quality rating... This option implements a feedback collection mechanism using API Gateway. By capturing the specific edits (human expertise) and ratings, the developer creates a "flywheel" effect where human knowledge is harvested to enhance the FM's capabilities (via future fine-tuning or RAG updates), which is the core definition of a collaborative AI system. Disclaimer: This diagram is a conceptual visualization designed to illustrate the specific exam logic. It abstracts certain production details to focus on the correct answer. INCORRECT: Configure Amazon Bedrock Guardrails with a "PII Redaction" filter... While Guardrails are crucial for safety, redaction is a protective measure, not a mechanism for "enhancing FM capabilities with human expertise" or orchestrating a review workflow. It blocks data rather than enabling collaboration. INCORRECT: Use Amazon CloudWatch Synthetics to run "Canary" scripts... This is an operational monitoring task (Content Domain 4). Simulating users with scripts checks for system uptime/latency, but it does not facilitate actual human collaboration or approval of legal drafts. INCORRECT: Deploy the Foundation Model on Amazon SageMaker Asynchronous Inference... This is an infrastructure scaling and performance optimization strategy. While scaling is important, it does not define the logic for human interaction, approval gates, or feedback collection required by the scenario.

questions.question: A developer is building a "Corporate IT Support" agent using Amazon Bedrock. The agent uses a custom tool named ResetUserPasswordwhich requires a strictly formatted EmployeeID(e.g., "EMP-1234") and a ReasonCode.During beta testing, the model often invokes the tool with malformed IDs (e.g., "1234" or "employee-1234"), causing the backend Identity Provider to throw unhandled exceptions that terminate the session abruptly.According to AWS best practices for reliable tool operations, how should the developer implement the integration to resolve this issue?
options: Configure an Amazon CloudWatch Alarm to detect the backend exceptions. Use an AWS Lambda function triggered by the alarm to parse the error logs and send an email to the user explaining the correct ID format.

Define a strict OpenAPI specification for the tool's parameters. Implement the tool's logic using an AWS Lambda function that validates the EmployeeID format using Regex before calling the Identity Provider. If validation fails, return a structured error message (e.g., {"error": "InvalidFormat", "message": "ID must start with EMP-"} ) back to the agent to enable self-correction.

Update the agent's System Prompt to include a few-shot example showing the model exactly how to format the EmployeeID . Rely on the model's in-context learning to prevent it from ever sending an invalid format to the tool.

Deploy an Amazon API Gateway in front of the Identity Provider with a standard throttling policy. This ensures that if the agent enters a loop of sending bad requests, the backend system will not be overwhelmed.
questions.explain: CORRECT: Define a strict OpenAPI specification for the tool's parameters. Implement the tool's logic using an AWS Lambda function that validates the EmployeeID format using Regex before calling the Identity Provider. If validation fails, return a structured error message (e.g., {"error": "InvalidFormat", "message": "ID must start with EMP-"} ) back to the agent to enable self-correction. This solution directly implements the skill of using Lambda functions to implement error handling and parameter validation . By intercepting the request in the Lambda function before it hits the fragile backend, the developer ensures reliable operation. Crucially, returning the error message back to the agent (instead of crashing) allows the "intelligent" agent to read the error and try again (e.g., "I apologize, but I need the ID in the format EMP-XXXX. Please provide it again."), which is a core tenet of robust agentic design. Disclaimer: This diagram is a conceptual visualization designed to illustrate the specific exam logic. It abstracts certain production details to focus on the correct answer. INCORRECT: Configure an Amazon CloudWatch Alarm to detect the backend exceptions... This is a reactive monitoring approach (Domain 4), not a tool integration strategy. It creates a disjointed user experience where the chat session crashes, and the user receives an email later, rather than fixing the interaction in real-time. INCORRECT: Update the agent's System Prompt to include a few-shot example... While helpful, relying solely on prompt engineering (Skill 1.6) does not guarantee "reliable tool operations". Models can still make mistakes. Robust tool integration requires deterministic validation code (Lambda) to act as a guardrail for the API. INCORRECT: Deploy an Amazon API Gateway in front of the Identity Provider with a standard throttling policy... Throttling prevents load issues, but it does not fix the logic error of malformed inputs. The tool calls will still fail, just at a controlled rate, without helping the agent understand why they are failing.

questions.question: A game development studio is building a "World Builder" AI assistant using the Model Context Protocol (MCP) to standardize how their Foundation Models interact with internal tools. The developers need to deploy two specific MCP servers to extend the model's capabilities:A "Dice Roller" Tool: A lightweight, stateless utility that generates cryptographically secure random numbers for loot generation. It handles bursty traffic but requires minimal compute resources.A "Physics Simulator" Tool: A complex legacy application that loads a 12 GB physics engine into memory. It takes 3 minutes to initialize and must maintain a persistent state to calculate object trajectories over multiple turn-based interactions.Which architecture choices should the GenAI Developer select to deploy these MCP servers effectively? (Select two)
options: Deploy the "Dice Roller" tool as a stateless MCP server on AWS Lambda. Configure a Function URL to provide a secure endpoint for the MCP client to connect to the tool.

Deploy the "Physics Simulator" tool as a containerized MCP server on Amazon ECS. Configure the service to maintain a persistent connection, allowing the heavy engine to remain loaded in memory between requests.
Deploy the "Physics Simulator" tool on AWS Lambda with Provisioned Concurrency enabled. Set the memory to the maximum limit (10 GB) to ensure the physics engine can load quickly for every request.
Deploy the "Dice Roller" tool on Amazon EC2 behind an Application Load Balancer. Use Auto Scaling Groups to manage the bursty traffic and ensure high availability for the random number generation.
Use AWS App Runner to deploy both tools as a single monolithic MCP server. Configure the service to pause when idle to save costs on the physics engine execution.
questions.explain: CORRECT: Deploy the "Dice Roller" tool as a stateless MCP server on AWS Lambda. Configure a Function URL to provide a secure endpoint for the MCP client to connect to the tool. This aligns with the exam guide's recommendation to use "Lambda functions to implement stateless MCP servers that provide lightweight tool access". Lambda is the ideal compute environment for lightweight, stateless utilities like a random number generator because it scales instantly with bursty traffic and incurs no cost when idle. Disclaimer: This diagram is a conceptual visualization designed to illustrate the specific exam logic. It abstracts certain production details to focus on the correct answer. CORRECT: Deploy the "Physics Simulator" tool as a containerized MCP server on Amazon ECS. Configure the service to maintain a persistent connection, allowing the heavy engine to remain loaded in memory between requests. This aligns with the exam guide's recommendation to use "Amazon ECS to implement MCP servers that provide complex tools". Since the tool requires a massive memory footprint (12 GB), a long initialization time (3 minutes), and persistent state across interactions, ECS is necessary. It allows the container to stay running, keeping the engine loaded in memory, whereas ephemeral compute would struggle with the startup latency and state persistence. Disclaimer: This diagram is a conceptual visualization designed to illustrate the specific exam logic. It abstracts certain production details to focus on the correct answer. INCORRECT: Deploy the "Physics Simulator" tool on AWS Lambda with Provisioned Concurrency... While Lambda supports up to 10 GB of memory, it is not designed for long-running, stateful applications that require 3-minute initialization times. Even with provisioned concurrency, the 15-minute hard timeout and the stateless nature of Lambda make it unsuitable for a tool that needs to maintain a persistent physics session across multiple turns. INCORRECT: Deploy the "Dice Roller" tool on Amazon EC2... Using EC2 for a simple, stateless random number generator is architectural overkill. It introduces unnecessary management overhead (patching, scaling configuration) and cost compared to the serverless efficiency of AWS Lambda. INCORRECT: Use AWS App Runner to deploy both tools as a single monolithic MCP server... Combining a lightweight tool and a heavy legacy tool into a monolith creates scaling inefficiencies (scaling the heavy engine just to roll dice). Furthermore, App Runner's pause/resume functionality might conflict with the requirement to keep the heavy physics engine loaded and ready for interaction.

refs.label: Guidance for Deploying Model Context Protocol Servers on AWS

questions.question: A large retail company is launching two distinct Generative AI features:The "Catalog Updater": An internal tool used by five administrators. Once a week, they upload a CSV of new products, and the AI generates descriptions. The speed of generation is not critical, but the cost should be minimized during the 6 days of idleness.The "Black Friday Assistant": A customer-facing chatbot that will go live during the holiday sale. It is expected to handle 50,000 concurrent users with a strict latency SLA of under 1 second per token to ensure a smooth user experience.Which deployment strategies should the GenAI Developer choose to meet the specific performance and cost requirements for these two workloads? (Select two)
options: Configure Amazon Bedrock Provisioned Throughput for the "Black Friday Assistant." Purchase Model Units (MUs) sufficient to handle the peak concurrent traffic, ensuring guaranteed capacity and consistent latency during the sale event.

Use Amazon Bedrock On-Demand inference triggered by an AWS Lambda function for the "Catalog Updater." This ensures the company pays only for the tokens processed during the weekly updates, with zero costs during idle time.

Deploy the "Black Friday Assistant" using Amazon Bedrock On-Demand inference. Rely on the service's default quotas to handle the traffic spikes to avoid the upfront commitment of Provisioned Throughput.

Deploy the "Catalog Updater" on a Amazon SageMaker Real-time Inference endpoint with a GPU instance. Enable auto-scaling with a minimum instance count of 1 to ensure the model is always loaded in memory for the administrators.

Use Amazon SageMaker Asynchronous Inference for the "Black Friday Assistant." Configure a large queue buffer to manage the high concurrency, accepting that some responses may take several minutes to process during peak load.
questions.explain: CORRECT: Configure Amazon Bedrock Provisioned Throughput for the "Black Friday Assistant." Purchase Model Units (MUs) sufficient to handle the peak concurrent traffic, ensuring guaranteed capacity and consistent latency during the sale event. This aligns with the need for guaranteed capacity and strict latency SLAs. On-demand inference is subject to shared capacity limits and potential throttling during high-traffic events like Black Friday. Provisioned Throughput reserves specific compute capacity (Model Units) for the customer, ensuring consistent performance for high-concurrency workloads. CORRECT: Use Amazon Bedrock On-Demand inference triggered by an AWS Lambda function for the "Catalog Updater." This ensures the company pays only for the tokens processed during the weekly updates, with zero costs during idle time. This aligns with the requirement for cost optimization for periodic, bursty workloads. Since the tool is used only once a week and latency is not critical, the serverless nature of On-Demand inference (often orchestrated via Lambda) is the most cost-effective choice. You pay only for input/output tokens, avoiding the cost of idle infrastructure. INCORRECT: Deploy the "Black Friday Assistant" using Amazon Bedrock On-Demand inference... Relying on On-Demand inference for a massive event like Black Friday creates a high risk of "ThrottlingExceptions" if the shared service capacity is exhausted. It cannot guarantee the "strict latency SLA" required by the scenario. INCORRECT: Deploy the "Catalog Updater" on a Amazon SageMaker Real-time Inference endpoint... Keeping a Real-time endpoint running 24/7 (even with min instance count of 1) for a tool used only once a week is financially wasteful. You would pay for the compute instance for 6 days of idleness, whereas On-Demand/Serverless options cost nothing when idle. INCORRECT: Use Amazon SageMaker Asynchronous Inference for the "Black Friday Assistant"... Asynchronous Inference is designed for large payloads and long processing times (minutes), not for a conversational chatbot that requires "latency under 1 second per token." Queuing responses for minutes would result in a terrible user experience for a live chat.

refs.label: Increase model invocation capacity with Provisioned Throughput in Amazon Bedrock

questions.question: A media company wants to host a 70-billion parameter open-source Foundation Model on Amazon SageMaker to generate creative fiction. In half-precision (FP16), the model weights require approximately 140 GB of GPU memory.The team attempts to deploy the model on an ml.p4d.24xlarge instance, which provides 8 NVIDIA A100 GPUs and ample total VRAM (320 GB). However, when using a standard PyTorch container, the deployment fails with a CUDA OutOfMemory (OOM) error because the default loading script attempts to load the entire model onto cuda:0 (a single GPU with only 40 GB of memory).Which deployment strategy should the GenAI Developer implement to resolve this memory constraint and optimize GPU utilization?
options: Configure the endpoint to use Amazon SageMaker Inference Pipelines. Split the model into eight separate containers, deploying each layer group to a different container in a sequential chain to avoid memory overflow on a single process.

Use an AWS Deep Learning Container (DLC) for Large Model Inference (LMI) (e.g., powered by DJL Serving). Configure the serving.properties file with option.tensor_parallel_degree=8 to automatically shard the model parameters across all 8 GPUs using Tensor Parallelism.

Update the inference script to use Model Offloading to the instance's CPU memory. Configure the PyTorch device_map to store 80% of the weights in system RAM and stream them to GPU 0 only when needed for computation.

Implement Flash Attention v2 in the standard container. This technique compresses the model weights by a factor of 4 during the loading phase, allowing the 140 GB model to fit comfortably within the 40 GB limit of a single A100 GPU.
questions.explain: CORRECT: Use an AWS Deep Learning Container (DLC) for Large Model Inference (LMI) (e.g., powered by DJL Serving). Configure the serving.properties file with option.tensor_parallel_degree=8 to automatically shard the model parameters across all 8 GPUs using Tensor Parallelism. This option correctly identifies a specialized container-based deployment pattern optimized for LLMs. Large models often exceed the memory of a single GPU. Tensor Parallelism (supported by engines like DeepJavaLibrary (DJL) Serving or vLLM in AWS LMI containers) splits the individual tensors of the model across multiple GPUs (sharding), allowing them to process the model in parallel. This is the standard solution for the "unique challenge" of memory/GPU utilization described in the skill. INCORRECT: Configure the endpoint to use Amazon SageMaker Inference Pipelines... Inference Pipelines are designed to chain distinct steps (e.g., Preprocessing -> Inference -> Postprocessing), not to split a single neural network model across containers for memory management. This would introduce massive latency and complexity and is not the correct pattern for model parallelism. INCORRECT: Update the inference script to use Model Offloading to the instance's CPU memory... While technically possible for local debugging, offloading a 70B model to CPU RAM for production inference is extremely slow due to the PCIe bandwidth bottleneck. It contradicts the goal of "optimizing token processing capacity" and fails to utilize the expensive multi-GPU hardware (the other 7 GPUs would sit idle). INCORRECT: Implement Flash Attention v2 in the standard container... Flash Attention is an optimization for the attention mechanism (speed and context memory usage during inference), but it does not compress the static model weights themselves. It cannot make a 140 GB model fit into a 40 GB memory slot.

refs.label: Deploy large models on Amazon SageMaker using DJLServing and DeepSpeed model parallel inference

questions.question: A SaaS company operates a customer support chatbot powered by a high-intelligence, large-parameter Foundation Model (FM) on Amazon Bedrock (e.g., Anthropic Claude 3.5 Sonnet).Analysis reveals that 70% of user queries are routine questions (e.g., "How do I reset my password?" or "Where is the billing page?"), while only 30% require the deep reasoning capabilities of the large model. The current architecture is exceeding the monthly budget, and users are complaining about the latency for simple answers.As a GenAI Developer, which deployment strategy should you implement to balance cost, performance, and response quality?
options: Implement an API-based model cascading architecture. Use a lightweight, low-latency model (such as Amazon Titan Text Express) to analyze the initial user prompt. Configure the logic to answer routine queries immediately using the small model, and only route complex or ambiguous prompts to the large-parameter FM.

Purchase Provisioned Throughput for the large-parameter FM. This guarantees a fixed number of Model Units (MUs) to process the high volume of routine queries with consistent latency, ensuring that the budget remains predictable regardless of traffic spikes.

Fine-tune a small open-source model (e.g., Llama 8B) on the company's entire historical support dataset. Replace the large-parameter FM completely with this fine-tuned model to handle all queries, ensuring the lowest possible cost per token.

Enable Semantic Caching using Amazon ElastiCache for Redis. Store the responses from the large-parameter FM. For every new user query, check the cache first; if a similar question was asked previously, return the cached response to eliminate FM inference costs.
questions.explain: CORRECT: Implement an API-based model cascading architecture... This answer directly applies the concept of model cascading . By distinguishing between "routine" and "complex" tasks, the developer can "balance performance and resource requirements." The small model handles the bulk of the traffic (70%) cheaply and quickly (solving the latency/cost issue), while the expensive model is reserved only for the tasks that actually need it (maintaining quality). Disclaimer: This diagram is a conceptual visualization designed to illustrate the specific exam logic. It abstracts certain production details to focus on the correct answer. INCORRECT: Purchase Provisioned Throughput for the large-parameter FM... While Provisioned Throughput fixes latency consistency and offers predictable billing, it does not reduce the overall resource requirement or cost per query for simple tasks. You would still be paying a premium to use a "genius-level" model to answer "reset password" questions, which is inefficient resource utilization. INCORRECT: Fine-tune a small open-source model... Replace the large-parameter FM completely... Replacing the large model entirely introduces a risk of quality degradation for the 30% of complex queries that require deep reasoning. A small model, even when fine-tuned, may struggle with novel, complex troubleshooting scenarios that the large model handled well. Cascading offers the best of both worlds; total replacement creates a trade-off. INCORRECT: Enable Semantic Caching... Caching is a valid optimization, but it relies on users asking similar questions. It does not solve the cost/latency problem for new or unique routine queries that haven't been cached yet. Cascading is a more robust architectural strategy for the specific problem of "routine vs. complex" routing described in the skill.

refs.label: Amazon Bedrock Intelligent Prompt Routing
Understanding intelligent prompt routing in Amazon Bedrock

questions.question: A manufacturing company uses a legacy on-premises ERP system to track assembly line issues. The company wants to integrate Amazon Bedrock to automatically analyze "Technician Notes" (unstructured text) and categorize the root cause of failures using the Anthropic Claude 4.5 Sonnet model.The legacy ERP system has a strict limitation: it blocks all user operations while waiting for external API responses and times out if a response takes longer than 300 milliseconds. The GenAI analysis typically takes 3–5 seconds to complete.As a GenAI Developer, which integration architecture should you design to incorporate the Foundation Model (FM) capabilities without disrupting the legacy system's operations?
options: Create an Amazon API Gateway REST API with a synchronous integration to an AWS Lambda function. Configure the ERP to call this API with the notes. Update the Lambda function to increase the memory allocation to 10 GB, ensuring the model inference completes within the 300-millisecond window.

Implement an asynchronous, event-driven architecture. Configure the ERP to push the technician notes to an Amazon SQS queue and immediately return success to the user. Use an AWS Lambda function to poll the queue, invoke the Amazon Bedrock model for analysis, and write the classification results back to the ERP database via a separate callback API.

Use AWS AppSync with a GraphQL subscription. configure the ERP system to subscribe to the RootCauseAnalysis mutation. When a note is saved, trigger the mutation to invoke Amazon Bedrock directly. The subscription connection keeps the channel open without blocking the main ERP thread, allowing the response to stream back when ready.

Deploy an AWS PrivateLink connection between the on-premises network and the Amazon Bedrock service endpoint. Configure the ERP system to make direct HTTPS calls to the Bedrock InvokeModel API using the private endpoint, bypassing the public internet to reduce network latency below the 300-millisecond threshold.
questions.explain: CORRECT: Implement an asynchronous, event-driven architecture. Configure the ERP to push the technician notes to an Amazon SQS queue... This solution uses an event-driven architecture to achieve loose coupling . By pushing the message to Amazon SQS, the legacy ERP system can offload the task instantly (well under 300ms) and unblock the user. The heavy processing (GenAI inference) happens asynchronously in the background. Once the analysis is complete, the results are synchronized back to the legacy system via a callback, adhering to the latency constraints while successfully integrating the modern FM capability. Disclaimer: This diagram is a conceptual visualization designed to illustrate the specific exam logic. It abstracts certain production details to focus on the correct answer. INCORRECT: Create an Amazon API Gateway REST API with a synchronous integration... This represents tight coupling . Even with maximum memory, a large Foundation Model like Claude 4.5 Sonnet cannot reliably perform reasoning and generate tokens in under 300 milliseconds. The physics of token generation speed (latency per token) means this request will time out, causing the ERP to freeze or crash. INCORRECT: C. Use AWS AppSync with a GraphQL subscription... While subscriptions are useful for real-time updates, implementing a GraphQL client in a "legacy on-premises ERP" is often technically infeasible or requires significant code changes to the legacy app. Furthermore, maintaining a persistent WebSocket connection (subscriptions) might still block the specific transactional thread depending on the legacy language's concurrency model. The SQS pattern is the standard, robust pattern for legacy-to-cloud offloading. INCORRECT: D. Deploy an AWS PrivateLink connection... Reducing network latency does not solve the inference latency problem. Even if the network packet travel time is 0ms, the model itself still takes seconds to "think" and generate the output. Direct calls from the ERP to Bedrock are synchronous and will violate the 300ms hard timeout rule.

refs.label: Using Lambda with Amazon SQS
Creating event-driven architectures with Lambda

questions.question: A logistics company operates a legacy Transport Management System (TMS) that manages global shipping. The company wants to enhance the customer experience by introducing a "Proactive Empathy" feature. Whenever the TMS detects a shipment exception (such as a customs hold or severe weather delay), it sends an HTTP POST webhook with the shipment details.You need to build a solution that accepts this webhook and uses a Foundation Model (FM) on Amazon Bedrock to generate a personalized, reassuring explanation email for the customer.Constraints:The TMS webhook requires an HTTP 200 OK response within 2 seconds to confirm receipt.The text generation process using Amazon Bedrock and subsequent email delivery takes approximately 8–12 seconds.The solution must be serverless and event-driven.Which integration architecture should you implement to meet these requirements?
options: Create an AWS Lambda function with a Function URL to receive the webhook. Inside the Lambda handler, invoke the Amazon Bedrock model synchronously to generate the email content. Once the email is generated and sent via Amazon SES, return an HTTP 200 response to the TMS.

Configure an Amazon API Gateway REST API with a service proxy integration to Amazon EventBridge. Create an EventBridge rule that matches "Exception" events and targets an asynchronous AWS Lambda function. Configure the Lambda function to invoke Amazon Bedrock for content generation and Amazon SES for delivery.

Use AWS AppSync to create a GraphQL API endpoint for the TMS webhook. Configure a VTL (Velocity Template Language) resolver to directly call the Amazon Bedrock Runtime API. Use the response from Bedrock to populate the mutation return field so the TMS can send the email.

Set up an Amazon Kinesis Data Firehose delivery stream to ingest the webhooks directly into an Amazon S3 bucket. Configure an S3 Event Notification to trigger an AWS Lambda function that processes the files in batches, calls Amazon Bedrock for each exception, and updates a DynamoDB table with the draft emails.
questions.explain: CORRECT: Configure an Amazon API Gateway REST API with a service proxy integration to Amazon EventBridge. Create an EventBridge rule that matches "Exception" events and targets an asynchronous AWS Lambda function. Configure the Lambda function to invoke Amazon Bedrock for content generation and Amazon SES for delivery. This architecture perfectly addresses the requirements and aligns with Skill 2.3.2 (from the official exam guide) regarding developing integrated AI capabilities using API Gateway and EventBridge. Decoupling & Latency: By integrating API Gateway directly with EventBridge, the system can acknowledge the webhook (return HTTP 200) immediately after the event is successfully put onto the event bus, typically in milliseconds. This satisfies the TMS's 2-second timeout requirement. Event-Driven Processing: The actual heavy lifting—calling Amazon Bedrock which takes 8–12 seconds—happens asynchronously via the EventBridge Rule and Lambda. The TMS does not need to wait for this process to complete. Integration: This standard pattern allows legacy applications (TMS) to trigger modern GenAI workflows without refactoring the legacy code beyond configuring a webhook URL. Disclaimer: This diagram is a conceptual visualization designed to illustrate the specific exam logic. It abstracts certain production details to focus on the correct answer. INCORRECT: Create an AWS Lambda function with a Function URL to receive the webhook. Inside the Lambda handler, invoke the Amazon Bedrock model synchronously to generate the email content. Once the email is generated and sent via Amazon SES, return an HTTP 200 response to the TMS. This solution fails the specific constraints. Because the Lambda function waits for Amazon Bedrock to generate text (8–12 seconds) before returning a response, the TMS will time out (limit is 2 seconds). This synchronous coupling is a poor practice for webhook handlers involving long-running GenAI inference. INCORRECT: Use AWS AppSync to create a GraphQL API endpoint for the TMS webhook. Configure a VTL (Velocity Template Language) resolver to directly call the Amazon Bedrock Runtime API. Use the response from Bedrock to populate the mutation return field so the TMS can send the email. Legacy systems often cannot easily interact with GraphQL mutations without code changes. More importantly, this is a synchronous call. AppSync would wait for Bedrock to respond before replying to the TMS. This violates the 2-second timeout constraint and places the burden of sending the email back on the legacy TMS, rather than handling it in the new GenAI application. INCORRECT: Set up an Amazon Kinesis Data Firehose delivery stream to ingest the webhooks directly into an Amazon S3 bucket. Configure an S3 Event Notification to trigger an AWS Lambda function that processes the files in batches, calls Amazon Bedrock for each exception, and updates a DynamoDB table with the draft emails. While this decouples the system, it introduces significant architectural overhead and latency (batching) that may not be necessary for individual webhook processing. Furthermore, Kinesis Data Firehose is generally used for high-throughput streaming data ingestion (logs, clickstreams), not as a standard webhook handler for transactional business events like "Shipment Exception." API Gateway + EventBridge is the standard pattern for this integration type.

refs.label: Amazon API Gateway targets for rules in Amazon EventBridge
What is Amazon API Gateway?
Building a serverless document chat with AWS Lambda and Amazon Bedrock

questions.question: A multinational pharmaceutical company is developing an internal "Drug Discovery Assistant" web application. The application allows researchers to query Amazon Bedrock Knowledge Bases to retrieve proprietary clinical trial data. The company uses Microsoft Entra ID (formerly Azure AD) as its corporate identity provider.To ensure strict data isolation and least privilege, the following access requirements must be enforced:Junior Researchers must only be able to query the "Public_Clinical_Trials" Knowledge Base.Senior Scientists must be able to query both "Public_Clinical_Trials" and "Confidential_Molecule_Data" Knowledge Bases.The application frontend uses the AWS SDK for JavaScript to invoke Amazon Bedrock directly, eliminating the need for an intermediate API layer.Which combination of steps should the developers take to implement this secure access framework? (Select TWO)
options: Configure an Amazon Cognito Identity Pool (Federated Identities) with OIDC federation to Microsoft Entra ID. Set up role-based mapping rules to assign a specific IAM role to the user based on their "department" claim in the identity token.

Create a single IAM role with full Amazon Bedrock access attached to the Cognito User Pool. Configure Amazon Bedrock Guardrails to analyze the semantic intent of the user's prompt and block requests to the "Confidential_Molecule_Data" Knowledge Base if the user is a Junior Researcher.

Define distinct IAM permission policies for the Junior and Senior roles. In each policy, explicitly allow the bedrock:Retrieve and bedrock:RetrieveAndGenerate actions, setting the Resource element to the specific ARN(s) of the Knowledge Bases authorized for that role.

Configure Amazon Cognito User Pools to vend a JSON Web Token (JWT). Pass this JWT in the Authorization header of the Amazon Bedrock API request, relying on the Bedrock Runtime to parse the token claims and enforce the "department" scope dynamically.

Use AWS IAM Identity Center to synchronize all employee directories. Attach a resource-based policy directly to each Amazon Bedrock Knowledge Base resource that lists the specific Microsoft Entra ID Group IDs in the Principal element.
questions.explain: CORRECT: Configure an Amazon Cognito Identity Pool (Federated Identities) with OIDC federation to Microsoft Entra ID. Set up role-based mapping rules to assign a specific IAM role to the user based on their "department" claim in the identity token. This setup correctly implements identity federation between the enterprise system (Entra ID) and AWS services. By using Cognito Identity Pools with role mapping, the application can exchange the corporate identity token for temporary AWS IAM credentials that correspond to a specific role (Junior or Senior). CORRECT: Define distinct IAM permission policies for the Junior and Senior roles. In each policy, explicitly allow the bedrock:Retrieve and bedrock:RetrieveAndGenerate actions, setting the Resource element to the specific ARN(s) of the Knowledge Bases authorized for that role. This implements the principle of least privilege and Role-Based Access Control (RBAC). By scoping the IAM permissions to specific Knowledge Base ARNs, the security controls are enforced at the AWS infrastructure level. Even if a Junior Researcher attempts to query the Confidential KB via the SDK, the API call will be denied by IAM. INCORRECT: Create a single IAM role with full Amazon Bedrock access attached to the Cognito User Pool. Configure Amazon Bedrock Guardrails to analyze the semantic intent of the user's prompt and block requests to the "Confidential_Molecule_Data" Knowledge Base if the user is a Junior Researcher. Amazon Bedrock Guardrails are designed for content safety (blocking toxic language, PII, or specific topics), not for enforcing resource-level access control or authorization logic. Relying on a single role with full access violates the principle of least privilege, as the underlying credentials technically possess permission to access the confidential data. INCORRECT: Configure Amazon Cognito User Pools to vend a JSON Web Token (JWT). Pass this JWT in the Authorization header of the Amazon Bedrock API request, relying on the Bedrock Runtime to parse the token claims and enforce the "department" scope dynamically. The Amazon Bedrock data plane APIs (Runtime) do not natively accept Cognito JWTs or OIDC tokens for authentication. They require requests to be signed using AWS Signature Version 4 (SigV4) with valid IAM credentials (Access Key and Secret Key), which are obtained via the Cognito Identity Pool flow. INCORRECT: Use AWS IAM Identity Center to synchronize all employee directories. Attach a resource-based policy directly to each Amazon Bedrock Knowledge Base resource that lists the specific Microsoft Entra ID Group IDs in the Principal element. Amazon Bedrock Knowledge Bases do not currently support resource-based policies that allow external IDP groups (like Entra ID groups) to be defined directly as Principals. Access must be granted via identity-based policies attached to IAM roles.

refs.label: Cognito - Using role-based access control
Set up permissions for a user or role to create and manage knowledge bases

questions.question: A large financial services enterprise is building a centralized platform to allow internal development teams to consume Foundation Models (FMs) from Amazon Bedrock. To ensure strict compliance and governance, the organization requires a "GenAI Gateway" architecture.The architecture must meet the following requirements:Prevent "Shadow AI" by forcing all internal applications to route requests through a single control point that logs every prompt and response for auditing.Automate the testing of prompt templates. Whenever a prompt engineer updates a standard system prompt, it must be automatically evaluated for toxicity and accuracy before being made available to applications.Ensure that PII is automatically redacted from prompts before they are sent to the model provider.Which combination of actions should you implement to achieve this? (Select TWO)
options: Develop a centralized REST API using Amazon API Gateway and AWS Lambda. Configure the Lambda function to act as an abstraction layer that intercepts requests, uses Amazon Comprehend to detect and redact PII, logs the transaction to Amazon CloudWatch Logs, and then invokes Amazon Bedrock.

Create an AWS CodePipeline for prompt versioning. Include an AWS CodeBuild stage that runs an automated evaluation script (using a library like Ragas or specific Bedrock evaluations) against the new prompt candidate. Configure the pipeline to fail and rollback if the toxicity score exceeds a defined threshold.

Grant all internal application roles direct IAM permissions to bedrock:InvokeModel. Enable AWS CloudTrail data events for Amazon Bedrock to capture the request payloads and response bodies for auditing purposes.
Use Amazon Inspector within the CI/CD pipeline to scan the weights of the Amazon Bedrock base models for vulnerabilities and malware before approving them for use in the production environment.

Implement AWS AppSync with a direct data source to Amazon Bedrock. Use VTL resolvers to hardcode the system prompts within the API definition, ensuring that developers cannot modify them without redeploying the API.
questions.explain: CORRECT: Develop a centralized REST API using Amazon API Gateway and AWS Lambda. Configure the Lambda function to act as an abstraction layer that intercepts requests, uses Amazon Comprehend to detect and redact PII, logs the transaction to Amazon CloudWatch Logs, and then invokes Amazon Bedrock. This action implements the GenAI Gateway pattern and centralized abstraction layer (btw we have skill for that in the exam guide as well) . By forcing traffic through API Gateway + Lambda, the organization gains a control point to enforce PII redaction (compliance) and comprehensive logging (observability) without relying on individual application teams to implement these controls correctly. CORRECT: Create an AWS CodePipeline for prompt versioning. Include an AWS CodeBuild stage that runs an automated evaluation script (using a library like Ragas or specific Bedrock evaluations) against the new prompt candidate. Configure the pipeline to fail and rollback if the toxicity score exceeds a defined threshold. This action addresses the CI/CD pipelines and automated testing frameworks . It ensures that changes to GenAI components (prompts) are rigorously tested for safety (toxicity) and performance (accuracy) in an automated fashion before deployment, providing the required "security scans and rollback support." INCORRECT: Grant all internal application roles direct IAM permissions to bedrock:InvokeModel. Enable AWS CloudTrail data events for Amazon Bedrock to capture the request payloads and response bodies for auditing purposes. While CloudTrail Data Events can log payloads, this approach (Direct Access) fails to provide a centralized abstraction layer for active controls like PII redaction before the model is called. It relies on reactive auditing rather than proactive governance (Gateway pattern). INCORRECT: Use Amazon Inspector within the CI/CD pipeline to scan the weights of the Amazon Bedrock base models for vulnerabilities and malware before approving them for use in the production environment. Amazon Inspector is used to scan EC2 instances, container images, and Lambda functions for vulnerabilities. It does not scan the model weights of managed SaaS services like Amazon Bedrock. The security of the base model weights is managed by AWS (Shared Responsibility Model). INCORRECT: Implement AWS AppSync with a direct data source to Amazon Bedrock. Use VTL resolvers to hardcode the system prompts within the API definition, ensuring that developers cannot modify them without redeploying the API. Hardcoding prompts in VTL makes them difficult to test and iterate on. It does not provide the automated evaluation workflow required by the scenario. Furthermore, while AppSync is an API layer, this specific implementation lacks the logic for dynamic PII redaction and flexible logging described in the Gateway requirement.

refs.label: Building an AI gateway to Amazon Bedrock with Amazon API Gateway
Amazon Comprehend - Detecting PII entities

questions.question: A media company needs to process a high volume of viewer comments using Amazon Bedrock to extract sentiment.
