questions.question: A healthcare company is deploying a Bedrock-powered medical documentation assistant that helps clinicians generate patient reports. The company wants to identify prompts that contain potential Protected Health Information (PHI) or requests for medical diagnoses to analyze usage patterns and improve training materials. However, the assistant must continue processing all requests without blocking any clinician workflows, as blocking could delay critical patient care. The solution must log all detected policy violations for weekly review by the compliance team and trigger immediate alerts only for high-severity violations.Which solution meets ALL these requirements?
options: Create a Bedrock guardrail with PII filters and denied topics for medical diagnoses. Configure the guardrail to BLOCK mode. Set up CloudWatch alarms on the GuardrailBlocked metric to alert the compliance team. Use CloudWatch Logs Insights to query blocked requests weekly.

Create a Bedrock guardrail with PII filters and denied topics for medical diagnoses. Configure the guardrail in ENABLED mode without specifying a blocking behavior. Attach the guardrail to the model invocations. Set up EventBridge rules to capture guardrail evaluation results and route them to an SNS topic for compliance team notifications.

Create a Bedrock guardrail with sensitive information filters for PHI and denied topics for medical diagnoses. Apply the guardrail with intervention action set to "NONE" (detect-only mode). Configure CloudWatch Logs to capture all guardrail evaluations. Create CloudWatch metric filters on guardrail violation severity and set up alarms for high-severity events only.

Create a Lambda function that calls Amazon Comprehend Medical to detect PHI before invoking Bedrock. If PHI is detected, log to CloudWatch but continue with the Bedrock invocation. Use CloudWatch Insights for weekly analysis and SNS for immediate high-severity alerts based on custom severity scoring logic.
questions.explain: CORRECT: Create a Bedrock guardrail with sensitive information filters for PHI and denied topics for medical diagnoses. Apply the guardrail with intervention action set to "NONE" (detect-only mode). Configure CloudWatch Logs to capture all guardrail evaluations. Create CloudWatch metric filters on guardrail violation severity and set up alarms for high-severity events only. Bedrock guardrails support detect-only mode by setting the intervention action to "NONE", which identifies policy violations without blocking requests - this ensures uninterrupted clinician workflows while still capturing violations. All guardrail evaluations are automatically logged to CloudWatch Logs with detailed violation information including severity levels. CloudWatch metric filters can extract severity information from logs and trigger alarms selectively for high-severity violations, meeting the requirement for immediate alerts. The compliance team can use CloudWatch Logs Insights for weekly analysis of all detected violations. This is the native AWS solution with minimal operational overhead. INCORRECT: Create a Bedrock guardrail with PII filters and denied topics for medical diagnoses. Configure the guardrail to BLOCK mode. Set up CloudWatch alarms on the GuardrailBlocked metric to alert the compliance team. Use CloudWatch Logs Insights to query blocked requests weekly. BLOCK mode violates the core requirement that the assistant must continue processing all requests without blocking clinician workflows. When a guardrail is configured in BLOCK mode, any request triggering the guardrail policies will be rejected, and Bedrock will not generate a response. This could delay critical patient care and is explicitly stated as unacceptable in the requirements. The GuardrailBlocked metric would track blocking events, but blocking itself should not occur in this scenario. INCORRECT: Create a Bedrock guardrail with PII filters and denied topics for medical diagnoses. Configure the guardrail in ENABLED mode without specifying a blocking behavior. Attach the guardrail to the model invocations. Set up EventBridge rules to capture guardrail evaluation results and route them to an SNS topic for compliance team notifications. Guardrails in "ENABLED mode" without explicitly setting intervention to "NONE" will default to BLOCK behavior - this means requests triggering guardrail policies would still be blocked, violating the no-blocking requirement. Additionally, Bedrock guardrails do not natively emit events to EventBridge for each evaluation - guardrail results are returned in the API response and logged to CloudWatch Logs. While EventBridge can be used with CloudWatch Logs subscriptions, this answer misrepresents the default guardrail behavior and the native integration pattern. The solution would block workflows and requires additional complexity to work around the incorrect configuration. INCORRECT: Create a Lambda function that calls Amazon Comprehend Medical to detect PHI before invoking Bedrock. If PHI is detected, log to CloudWatch but continue with the Bedrock invocation. Use CloudWatch Insights for weekly analysis and SNS for immediate high-severity alerts based on custom severity scoring logic. This solution adds unnecessary complexity and operational overhead by introducing a Lambda pre-processing layer with Amazon Comprehend Medical. While this approach could technically work, it requires custom code to orchestrate the detection, logging, severity scoring, and alerting - all capabilities that Bedrock guardrails provide natively. The solution also misses the "denied topics for medical diagnoses" requirement, as Comprehend Medical only detects PHI entities but doesn't evaluate content against topic restrictions. Bedrock guardrails are the purpose-built, managed solution for this exact use case, eliminating the need for custom Lambda functions and reducing maintenance burden.

refs.label: Create your guardrail

questions.question: A financial services company is building a Bedrock-powered investment advisory chatbot. The application uses guardrails to detect potentially risky financial advice or regulatory violations. When a guardrail detects a violation, the request should not be immediately blocked. Instead, a human compliance officer must review the request and decide whether to allow or reject it. The solution must pause processing until the human makes a decision, must track all review decisions for audit purposes, and must return the final response to the user within the same session without requiring them to retry.Which solution meets ALL these requirements?
options: Configure the Bedrock guardrail in BLOCK mode. When a request is blocked, trigger an SNS notification to the compliance team. Store the original request in DynamoDB with a unique ID. Instruct users to retry with the same ID after 5 minutes, and use a Lambda function to check if the compliance officer has approved the request in DynamoDB.

Configure the Bedrock guardrail with intervention action set to "NONE" (detect-only mode). Create a Lambda function that invokes Bedrock and checks the guardrail assessment in the response. If violations are detected, invoke a Step Functions workflow that creates an Amazon A2I human review task and waits synchronously for the decision. Based on the human decision, either continue with the Bedrock invocation or return an error to the user.

Configure the Bedrock guardrail in BLOCK mode. Set up an EventBridge rule that triggers on GuardrailBlocked CloudWatch metrics. The EventBridge rule invokes a Step Functions workflow that sends the blocked request to Amazon A2I for human review. Use SNS to notify the user via email when the review is complete and ask them to resubmit their request.

Configure the Bedrock guardrail in ENABLED mode. Use API Gateway to synchronously invoke a Lambda function that calls Bedrock. If the guardrail blocks the request, the Lambda function publishes a message to an SQS queue. A separate Lambda function processes the queue, creates A2I review tasks, and updates the user via WebSocket API when the review is complete.
questions.explain: CORRECT: Configure the Bedrock guardrail with intervention action set to "NONE" (detect-only mode). Create a Lambda function that invokes Bedrock and checks the guardrail assessment in the response. If violations are detected, invoke a Step Functions workflow that creates an Amazon A2I human review task and waits synchronously for the decision. Based on the human decision, either continue with the Bedrock invocation or return an error to the user. This solution correctly implements the human-in-the-loop pattern. Using detect-only mode (intervention = "NONE") allows the Lambda function to programmatically inspect guardrail violations in the API response without automatic blocking. The Lambda invokes Step Functions with a synchronous execution, and Step Functions creates an A2I human review task. Step Functions has native integration with A2I and can wait synchronously until the human makes a decision (approve/reject) without timing out - this is the callback pattern where Step Functions pauses and waits for an external task token to be returned. Based on the human decision, the Lambda either proceeds with the original Bedrock request or returns an appropriate error, keeping the user in the same session. All review decisions are automatically tracked in A2I and Step Functions execution history for audit compliance. INCORRECT: Configure the Bedrock guardrail in BLOCK mode. When a request is blocked, trigger an SNS notification to the compliance team. Store the original request in DynamoDB with a unique ID. Instruct users to retry with the same ID after 5 minutes, and use a Lambda function to check if the compliance officer has approved the request in DynamoDB. BLOCK mode immediately rejects the request, and there's no direct way to trigger SNS from a blocked Bedrock invocation within the application flow. More critically, this solution breaks the user experience by requiring them to manually retry after 5 minutes - this violates the requirement to return the final response within the same session. The user shouldn't need to know that human review is happening behind the scenes. This approach also adds significant complexity with DynamoDB state management and manual retry logic, and doesn't provide audit trail integration with Step Functions and A2I. INCORRECT: Configure the Bedrock guardrail in BLOCK mode. Set up an EventBridge rule that triggers on GuardrailBlocked CloudWatch metrics. The EventBridge rule invokes a Step Functions workflow that sends the blocked request to Amazon A2I for human review. Use SNS to notify the user via email when the review is complete and ask them to resubmit their request. Bedrock guardrails do not emit real-time events to EventBridge for individual blocked requests - CloudWatch metrics are aggregated statistics, not per-request events. Even if this integration existed, BLOCK mode immediately rejects the request, so the original user session is already terminated. Asking users to wait for an email notification and resubmit violates the requirement to handle everything within the same session. This solution fundamentally misunderstands the eventing model and creates a poor user experience with asynchronous email notifications and manual resubmission. INCORRECT: Configure the Bedrock guardrail in ENABLED mode. Use API Gateway to synchronously invoke a Lambda function that calls Bedrock. If the guardrail blocks the request, the Lambda function publishes a message to an SQS queue. A separate Lambda function processes the queue, creates A2I review tasks, and updates the user via WebSocket API when the review is complete. Using "ENABLED mode" without specifying intervention action defaults to BLOCK behavior, which immediately rejects the request. Once blocked, the original Lambda execution cannot continue - publishing to SQS creates an asynchronous pattern where a separate Lambda handles the review. This breaks the synchronous requirement: the original API Gateway request will timeout before the human review completes (API Gateway maximum timeout is 29 seconds, human review takes much longer). While WebSocket API could theoretically update the user, this adds significant architectural complexity and doesn't meet the "same session" requirement - the original HTTP/WebSocket connection would have terminated. Step Functions with callback pattern is the purpose-built solution for long-running synchronous workflows. Key Concepts: Guardrail intervention "NONE" = detect violations without blocking, allowing programmatic handling Step Functions callback pattern = synchronous wait for external task (A2I) completion Amazon A2I = managed service for human review tasks with audit trails Synchronous user experience = user waits in same session for final response

Create your guardrail
Using Amazon Augmented AI for Human Review

questions.question: A research company is building a Bedrock-powered system to automate scientific literature reviews. The system must perform three specialized tasks: search academic databases using an existing search tool (already built as an action group), analyze the retrieved papers to extract key findings, and generate a comprehensive summary report. Each task requires different capabilities and prompting strategies. The solution must coordinate these tasks in sequence, pass data between them efficiently, and allow independent updates to each specialized capability without affecting the others.Which TWO solutions should be combined to meet these requirements?(Choose TWO)
options: Create a single Bedrock Agent with the existing search action group. Configure the agent with a detailed prompt that instructs it to first search, then analyze results, then generate a summary report. Use the agent's built-in reasoning to orchestrate all three tasks sequentially.

Create three separate Bedrock Agents, each specialized for one task: a search agent (with the existing search action group), an analysis agent, and a summary agent. Create a supervisor agent that orchestrates the workflow by invoking each specialized agent in sequence and passing outputs as inputs to the next agent.

Create three separate Bedrock Agents for search, analysis, and summary tasks. Use AWS Step Functions to orchestrate the workflow by invoking each agent sequentially via the InvokeAgent API, passing the output of each agent as input to the next step.

Create a single Bedrock Agent with multiple action groups: attach the existing search action group, and create two additional Lambda-backed action groups for analysis and summary. Configure the agent to orchestrate all three tasks through its reasoning engine.

Use Amazon Bedrock Flows to create a visual workflow that connects three separate Bedrock Agents (search, analysis, summary). Configure data flow between agents using Flows' built-in data passing mechanisms, and deploy the complete workflow as a single managed orchestration.
questions.explain: CORRECT: Create three separate Bedrock Agents, each specialized for one task: a search agent (with the existing search action group), an analysis agent, and a summary agent. Create a supervisor agent that orchestrates the workflow by invoking each specialized agent in sequence and passing outputs as inputs to the next agent. This solution implements the multi-agent orchestration pattern using a supervisor agent architecture. Each specialized agent can have its own optimized system prompt, model selection, and configuration tailored to its specific task. The supervisor agent coordinates the workflow by invoking specialized agents in the correct sequence and managing data flow between them. This architecture allows independent updates to each specialized agent without affecting others - you can improve the analysis agent's prompt or switch its model without touching the search or summary agents. The supervisor pattern is native to Bedrock Agents and doesn't require external orchestration services. CORRECT: Use Amazon Bedrock Flows to create a visual workflow that connects three separate Bedrock Agents (search, analysis, summary). Configure data flow between agents using Flows' built-in data passing mechanisms, and deploy the complete workflow as a single managed orchestration. Bedrock Flows is purpose-built for orchestrating complex multi-agent workflows with visual design and managed execution. Flows provides native integration with Bedrock Agents, handles data passing between steps, supports branching and error handling, and deploys as a managed service. Each agent remains independently configurable and updateable. Flows eliminates the need to build custom orchestration logic while providing enterprise features like monitoring, versioning, and deployment controls. This is AWS's recommended approach for complex agent orchestration that requires visual workflow design and centralized management. INCORRECT: Create a single Bedrock Agent with the existing search action group. Configure the agent with a detailed prompt that instructs it to first search, then analyze results, then generate a summary report. Use the agent's built-in reasoning to orchestrate all three tasks sequentially. While a single agent with detailed prompts can technically perform multiple tasks, this violates the requirement for "independent updates to each specialized capability." Changing the analysis strategy requires modifying the single agent's prompt, which risks breaking the search or summary logic. Additionally, a single agent cannot optimize model selection, temperature, or other parameters differently for each task type. As tasks become more complex, a monolithic prompt becomes difficult to maintain and debug. The "different capabilities and prompting strategies" requirement explicitly calls for separation of concerns that a single agent cannot provide. INCORRECT: Create a single Bedrock Agent with multiple action groups: attach the existing search action group, and create two additional Lambda-backed action groups for analysis and summary. Configure the agent to orchestrate all three tasks through its reasoning engine. This approach conflates task orchestration with tool execution. Action groups are designed for discrete tool invocations (API calls, database queries, external system integrations), not for complex multi-step reasoning tasks like "analyze papers" or "generate comprehensive reports." While you could technically implement analysis and summary logic in Lambda functions, this pushes complex prompt engineering and LLM orchestration into code rather than leveraging Bedrock's native agent reasoning capabilities. It also prevents using different foundation models for different tasks - all action groups would be invoked by the same agent with the same model configuration. INCORRECT: Create three separate Bedrock Agents for search, analysis, and summary tasks. Use AWS Step Functions to orchestrate the workflow by invoking each agent sequentially via the InvokeAgent API, passing the output of each agent as input to the next step. While Step Functions can technically orchestrate agents, this introduces unnecessary complexity and operational overhead. Step Functions requires you to write custom integration code, handle agent invocation API details, manage state transitions, and implement error handling logic that Bedrock's native supervisor agent pattern or Flows already provide. Step Functions also adds latency for state machine transitions and doesn't integrate with Bedrock's agent-specific features like guardrails inheritance or trace propagation. Since the requirement is purely agent-to-agent orchestration without external system integration, using Step Functions violates the principle of using purpose-built AWS services - both supervisor agents and Flows are designed specifically for this use case. Key Concepts: Supervisor agent pattern = centralized orchestration using a coordinator agent Bedrock Flows = visual workflow designer for multi-agent orchestration Specialized agents = separation of concerns for independent updates Action groups = tools for external system integration, not task orchestration

Amazon Bedrock Agents

questions.question: A legal firm uses a Bedrock Knowledge Base connected to an S3 bucket containing case law documents and regulatory updates. Lawyers report that the AI assistant sometimes provides outdated legal precedents, even though the documents in S3 are updated daily by the research team. The firm needs a solution that ensures the Knowledge Base always reflects the most current information with minimal manual intervention and the LEAST operational overhead.Which solution addresses this issue MOST effectively?
options: Configure an S3 Event Notification to trigger a Lambda function whenever new documents are added or updated. The Lambda function calls the StartIngestionJob API to synchronize the Knowledge Base automatically. Set up CloudWatch alarms to monitor ingestion job failures.

Schedule an EventBridge rule to invoke the StartIngestionJob API every hour to keep the Knowledge Base synchronized with S3. Configure the ingestion job to process only modified files by enabling incremental sync. Use SNS to notify the team if ingestion fails.

Enable S3 Versioning and configure the Knowledge Base to automatically detect changes in the S3 bucket. Set the synchronization mode to "Automatic" in the Knowledge Base settings so it continuously monitors S3 for updates without requiring manual triggers.

Create a Step Functions workflow that runs daily to compare S3 object metadata with Knowledge Base ingestion timestamps. If discrepancies are detected, trigger the StartIngestionJob API. Use DynamoDB to track the last successful sync time for each document.
questions.explain: CORRECT: Configure an S3 Event Notification to trigger a Lambda function whenever new documents are added or updated. The Lambda function calls the StartIngestionJob API to synchronize the Knowledge Base automatically. Set up CloudWatch alarms to monitor ingestion job failures. This solution provides event-driven synchronization with minimal operational overhead. S3 Event Notifications trigger immediately when documents are added or modified, ensuring near-real-time updates to the Knowledge Base. The Lambda function is simple - it only needs to call the StartIngestionJob API, which handles all the complexity of chunking, embedding, and vector store updates. CloudWatch alarms provide visibility into failures without requiring active monitoring. This is the AWS-recommended pattern for keeping Knowledge Bases synchronized with S3 data sources and requires the least operational overhead since it's fully automated and event-driven rather than scheduled. INCORRECT: Schedule an EventBridge rule to invoke the StartIngestionJob API every hour to keep the Knowledge Base synchronized with S3. Configure the ingestion job to process only modified files by enabling incremental sync. Use SNS to notify the team if ingestion fails. While scheduled ingestion works, hourly synchronization introduces up to 60 minutes of data staleness - during this window, lawyers could receive outdated information. This violates the "always reflects most current information" requirement. Additionally, running ingestion jobs on a fixed schedule regardless of whether data has changed wastes compute resources and increases costs. While incremental sync reduces processing time by only handling modified files, it doesn't solve the fundamental problem of scheduled delays. Event-driven synchronization (answer 1) is more efficient and provides faster updates. INCORRECT: Enable S3 Versioning and configure the Knowledge Base to automatically detect changes in the S3 bucket. Set the synchronization mode to "Automatic" in the Knowledge Base settings so it continuously monitors S3 for updates without requiring manual triggers. Bedrock Knowledge Bases do not have an "Automatic" synchronization mode that continuously monitors S3 for changes. Synchronization must be explicitly triggered via the StartIngestionJob API - there is no built-in S3 change detection mechanism in Knowledge Bases. While S3 Versioning is useful for data governance, it doesn't enable automatic Knowledge Base synchronization. This answer describes a feature that doesn't exist and would mislead someone into believing they can set-and-forget synchronization without implementing event-driven or scheduled triggers. INCORRECT: Create a Step Functions workflow that runs daily to compare S3 object metadata with Knowledge Base ingestion timestamps. If discrepancies are detected, trigger the StartIngestionJob API. Use DynamoDB to track the last successful sync time for each document. This solution adds significant unnecessary complexity and operational overhead. Building custom logic to compare S3 metadata with ingestion timestamps requires maintaining state in DynamoDB, writing comparison logic, and managing the Step Functions workflow. Daily execution means up to 24 hours of staleness, which is unacceptable for legal precedents where updates may be time-sensitive. The complexity of tracking per-document sync times violates the "LEAST operational overhead" requirement - the simple event-driven approach (answer 1) achieves better results with far less code and infrastructure to maintain. Key Concepts: StartIngestionJob API = triggers Knowledge Base synchronization with data source S3 Event Notifications = event-driven triggers for object changes Event-driven vs scheduled sync = real-time updates vs fixed-interval delays Incremental sync = processes only changed files, not full re-ingestion

questions.question: A customer service company is building a Bedrock-powered support chatbot that handles complex multi-turn conversations. The chatbot must maintain conversation context across multiple exchanges, retrieve relevant knowledge from a 50,000-document product manual, and respond within 500 milliseconds to maintain a natural conversational flow. The solution must support thousands of concurrent users and provide the MOST cost-effective architecture while meeting the performance requirements.Which combination of data stores should be used?
options: Use Amazon OpenSearch Service with vector engine for both conversation history and knowledge base. Configure separate indices for chat sessions and product documentation. Enable cross-cluster replication for high availability.

Use Amazon MemoryDB for Redis to store conversation history with vector embeddings for context retrieval. Use Amazon Bedrock Knowledge Base with Amazon OpenSearch Service as the vector store for the product manual. Implement a hybrid retrieval strategy combining conversation context from MemoryDB and knowledge from OpenSearch.

Use Amazon DynamoDB to store conversation history as JSON objects with TTL for automatic cleanup. Use Amazon Bedrock Knowledge Base with S3 as the vector store for product documentation. Implement caching with Amazon ElastiCache to improve response times.

Use Amazon Aurora PostgreSQL with pgvector extension for both conversation history and knowledge base storage. Create separate schemas for chat sessions and product documentation. Use read replicas to distribute query load across concurrent users.
questions.explain: CORRECT: Use Amazon MemoryDB for Redis to store conversation history with vector embeddings for context retrieval. Use Amazon Bedrock Knowledge Base with Amazon OpenSearch Service as the vector store for the product manual. Implement a hybrid retrieval strategy combining conversation context from MemoryDB and knowledge from OpenSearch. This solution uses the right tool for each job. MemoryDB for Redis provides sub-millisecond latency for conversation history retrieval, which is critical for maintaining natural conversational flow within the 500ms requirement. Its in-memory architecture handles thousands of concurrent sessions efficiently, and Redis's vector similarity search capabilities enable fast context retrieval from recent conversation turns. OpenSearch Service is appropriate for the 50,000-document knowledge base because it scales well for large document collections, provides advanced filtering and hybrid search capabilities, and integrates natively with Bedrock Knowledge Bases. The hybrid approach (conversation context + knowledge base) is the recommended pattern for conversational AI, and using specialized data stores for each workload type provides the most cost-effective solution while meeting performance requirements. INCORRECT: Use Amazon OpenSearch Service with vector engine for both conversation history and knowledge base. Configure separate indices for chat sessions and product documentation. Enable cross-cluster replication for high availability. While OpenSearch can technically store both conversation history and knowledge base content, it's over-engineered and cost-inefficient for session management. OpenSearch is designed for large-scale search and analytics workloads, not for ultra-low-latency session state retrieval. Storing thousands of concurrent chat sessions in OpenSearch creates unnecessary index bloat and increases costs - you're paying for search capabilities you don't need for simple key-value conversation history lookups. MemoryDB provides sub-millisecond access for session data at a fraction of the cost. Cross-cluster replication for chat sessions adds even more expense and complexity without addressing the core performance issue. This violates the "MOST cost-effective" requirement. INCORRECT: Use Amazon DynamoDB to store conversation history as JSON objects with TTL for automatic cleanup. Use Amazon Bedrock Knowledge Base with S3 as the vector store for product documentation. Implement caching with Amazon ElastiCache to improve response times. DynamoDB provides fast key-value access for conversation history, but it doesn't natively support vector similarity search - you would need to retrieve all conversation turns and perform embedding comparisons in application code, adding latency and complexity. More critically, using S3 as the vector store for knowledge base creates unacceptable query latency. S3 is not designed for real-time vector search - queries would take seconds, not milliseconds, violating the 500ms response time requirement. While ElastiCache could cache frequent queries, it doesn't solve the fundamental problem that S3 cannot serve as a performant vector database for real-time retrieval. The architecture fails to meet the performance requirement. INCORRECT: Use Amazon Aurora PostgreSQL with pgvector extension for both conversation history and knowledge base storage. Create separate schemas for chat sessions and product documentation. Use read replicas to distribute query load across concurrent users. Aurora with pgvector can handle vector search, but it's not optimized for the dual workload pattern described. For conversation history, Aurora adds unnecessary complexity and cost compared to MemoryDB's purpose-built session management capabilities - you don't need ACID transactions or relational capabilities for ephemeral chat sessions. For knowledge base queries, pgvector's performance degrades with large vector datasets (50,000 documents) compared to specialized vector databases like OpenSearch. Read replicas help with scaling, but they introduce replication lag that could result in inconsistent conversation context. This solution uses a general-purpose database for specialized workloads, violating the cost-effectiveness requirement and potentially missing the latency target under load. Key Concepts: MemoryDB for Redis = ultra-low latency session storage with vector capabilities OpenSearch Service = scalable vector database for large knowledge bases Hybrid retrieval = conversation context + knowledge base documents Workload-specific data stores = right tool for each job = cost optimization

questions.question: An aerospace engineering company is building a Bedrock Knowledge Base containing thousands of technical maintenance manuals for aircraft components. Each manual has a well-defined hierarchical structure with chapters, sections, subsections, and numbered procedures. Engineers using the AI assistant must be able to verify answers by referencing specific manual sections (e.g., "Section 3.2.4: Hydraulic System Pressure Testing"). The solution must ensure accurate attribution to the original document structure and allow engineers to quickly locate the exact source of information for compliance audits.Which chunking strategy should be used?
options: Use semantic chunking to split documents based on topic boundaries detected through embedding similarity. Configure the chunking algorithm to identify natural topic transitions and create chunks of approximately 300 tokens each with 10% overlap between consecutive chunks.

Use hierarchical chunking to preserve the document structure. Configure parent chunks to represent chapters and sections, with child chunks for subsections and procedures. Enable metadata extraction to capture section numbers, titles, and hierarchical relationships for accurate source attribution.

Use fixed-size chunking with 512-token chunks and 50-token overlap. Extract section headers using regex patterns and store them as metadata tags. Configure the retrieval system to return chunk metadata alongside the content for source attribution.

Use sentence-based chunking to split documents at sentence boundaries. Group sentences into chunks of 5-7 sentences each. Use a sliding window approach with 2-sentence overlap to maintain context across chunk boundaries.
questions.explain: CORRECT: Use hierarchical chunking to preserve the document structure. Configure parent chunks to represent chapters and sections, with child chunks for subsections and procedures. Enable metadata extraction to capture section numbers, titles, and hierarchical relationships for accurate source attribution. Hierarchical chunking is specifically designed for structured documents with clear organizational hierarchies like technical manuals. By preserving the chapter/section/subsection structure, the system can provide precise citations (e.g., "Section 3.2.4") that engineers need for compliance audits. Parent-child chunk relationships maintain document context while allowing granular retrieval - when a child chunk (procedure) is retrieved, the system can reference its parent chunks (section, chapter) for complete attribution. Metadata extraction captures section numbers and titles, enabling the AI to cite sources exactly as they appear in the original manual. This approach directly addresses the requirement for "accurate attribution to original document structure" and supports compliance workflows where engineers must verify information against specific manual sections. INCORRECT: Use semantic chunking to split documents based on topic boundaries detected through embedding similarity. Configure the chunking algorithm to identify natural topic transitions and create chunks of approximately 300 tokens each with 10% overlap between consecutive chunks. Semantic chunking identifies topic boundaries based on meaning, not document structure. While this works well for unstructured text like news articles or emails, it breaks the explicit hierarchical organization of technical manuals. A semantic chunking algorithm might merge content from different sections if they're topically similar (e.g., two hydraulic pressure procedures from different chapters), destroying the section numbering and citation structure that engineers rely on. When an engineer asks "What does Section 3.2.4 say about pressure testing?", semantic chunks cannot reliably map back to specific section numbers because they were created based on topic similarity, not structural boundaries. This violates the accurate attribution requirement. INCORRECT: Use fixed-size chunking with 512-token chunks and 50-token overlap. Extract section headers using regex patterns and store them as metadata tags. Configure the retrieval system to return chunk metadata alongside the content for source attribution. Fixed-size chunking arbitrarily splits text every 512 tokens regardless of content boundaries or document structure. This approach will split procedures mid-sentence, separate section headers from their content, and break numbered steps across multiple chunks. While regex patterns can extract some section headers, fixed-size chunks will frequently span multiple sections, making attribution ambiguous - a chunk might contain the end of Section 3.2.3 and the beginning of Section 3.2.4. Engineers would receive partial procedures without clear indication of which section they came from. This creates compliance risks when engineers cannot definitively cite sources and violates the requirement for accurate structural attribution. INCORRECT: Use sentence-based chunking to split documents at sentence boundaries. Group sentences into chunks of 5-7 sentences each. Use a sliding window approach with 2-sentence overlap to maintain context across chunk boundaries. Sentence-based chunking operates at too granular a level and completely ignores document structure. Technical procedures often span multiple sentences (e.g., a 10-step procedure), and splitting them into 5-7 sentence chunks would fragment procedural logic. Section headers, warnings, and notes would be separated from the procedures they apply to. The sliding window creates chunks that span unrelated sections without preserving hierarchical relationships. When the system retrieves "5 sentences from somewhere in the manual," engineers cannot determine if they're reading a complete procedure or fragments from different sections. This approach loses all structural attribution needed for compliance audits and fails to leverage the manual's inherent organization. Key Concepts: Hierarchical chunking = preserves document structure (chapters, sections, subsections) Parent-child relationships = maintains context and enables precise citations Structured documents = technical manuals, legal contracts, research papers Accurate attribution = ability to cite specific sections/clauses

refs.label: Hierarchical chunking

questions.question: A media company is building a Bedrock Knowledge Base containing thousands of news articles, opinion pieces, and investigative reports. Journalists complain that when they query the AI assistant, the retrieved content often feels fragmented - answers include incomplete thoughts, cut-off explanations, or context that seems to start mid-topic. The articles have minimal structural hierarchy (just headline and body text) and often blend multiple related topics in a flowing narrative style. The solution must ensure that retrieved chunks contain complete, coherent ideas that maintain the natural flow of the original writing.Which chunking strategy should be used?
options: Use hierarchical chunking to split articles based on paragraph structure. Configure parent chunks for article sections and child chunks for individual paragraphs. Enable metadata extraction to preserve paragraph numbering for accurate citations.

Use fixed-size chunking with 256-token chunks and 25% overlap between consecutive chunks. Configure the overlap to ensure that no sentence is split across chunk boundaries, and store article titles as metadata for source attribution.

Use semantic chunking to identify topic boundaries based on meaning and context. Configure the chunking algorithm to detect natural topic transitions using embedding similarity, creating variable-length chunks that preserve complete thoughts and maintain narrative coherence.

Use sentence-based chunking with groups of 8-10 sentences per chunk. Implement a sliding window with 3-sentence overlap to maintain context. Extract the first sentence of each chunk as a summary metadata field for improved retrieval.
questions.explain: CORRECT: Use semantic chunking to identify topic boundaries based on meaning and context. Configure the chunking algorithm to detect natural topic transitions using embedding similarity, creating variable-length chunks that preserve complete thoughts and maintain narrative coherence. Semantic chunking solves the fragmentation problem by splitting content at natural topic boundaries rather than arbitrary structural or length-based divisions. It uses embedding similarity to detect when a narrative transitions from one topic to another, ensuring that each chunk contains a complete, self-contained idea. For news articles that flow naturally without rigid structural hierarchy, semantic chunking creates variable-length chunks that might include an entire discussion about one topic (even if it spans multiple paragraphs) before transitioning to the next topic. This preserves the author's intended narrative flow and prevents mid-thought cutoffs that create incomplete or confusing responses. When the system retrieves a chunk, journalists get coherent, complete explanations rather than fragments that require piecing together context from multiple chunks. INCORRECT: Use hierarchical chunking to split articles based on paragraph structure. Configure parent chunks for article sections and child chunks for individual paragraphs. Enable metadata extraction to preserve paragraph numbering for accurate citations. Hierarchical chunking is designed for documents with explicit structural hierarchy (chapters, sections, subsections). News articles and opinion pieces typically don't have this formal structure - they consist of a headline and flowing body text without nested sections. Even if you artificially impose paragraph-level hierarchy, this doesn't solve the fragmentation problem: a single topic discussion often spans multiple paragraphs, and splitting at paragraph boundaries will create the exact "incomplete thoughts" problem journalists are complaining about. A topic might be introduced in paragraph 3, developed in paragraph 4, and concluded in paragraph 5 - hierarchical chunking would split this narrative into three fragments. This approach doesn't address the core issue of preserving semantic completeness. INCORRECT: Use fixed-size chunking with 256-token chunks and 25% overlap between consecutive chunks. Configure the overlap to ensure that no sentence is split across chunk boundaries, and store article titles as metadata for source attribution. Fixed-size chunking is the source of the fragmentation problem being reported. Regardless of overlap percentage, splitting content every 256 tokens will inevitably cut through the middle of topic discussions. A 256-token limit might end a chunk mid-argument, mid-explanation, or mid-narrative, forcing the reader to mentally bridge the gap or requiring the system to retrieve multiple adjacent chunks to reconstruct the complete thought. While 25% overlap helps maintain some context between chunks, it doesn't solve the fundamental issue: topics and narrative arcs don't align with arbitrary token counts. The "incomplete thoughts" and "cut-off explanations" complaints directly indicate that fixed-size chunking is creating poor user experience. INCORRECT: Use sentence-based chunking with groups of 8-10 sentences per chunk. Implement a sliding window with 3-sentence overlap to maintain context. Extract the first sentence of each chunk as a summary metadata field for improved retrieval. Sentence-based chunking with fixed sentence counts has the same fundamental problem as fixed-size token chunking - topics and complete ideas don't align with sentence counts. A complex topic might require 15 sentences to fully explain, while a simpler point might take only 3 sentences. Forcing chunks to contain exactly 8-10 sentences will fragment longer discussions and unnecessarily split shorter ones. The sliding window overlap creates redundancy but doesn't ensure semantic completeness. Journalists would still receive incomplete explanations when a topic discussion spans 12 sentences but is split into two chunks. Using the first sentence as a summary doesn't address the fragmentation issue - it's a retrieval optimization that doesn't solve the core problem of preserving narrative coherence. Key Concepts: Semantic chunking = splits at meaning/topic boundaries, not structure or length Embedding similarity = detects topic transitions algorithmically Variable-length chunks = adapts to content needs, preserves complete thoughts Unstructured content = news articles, emails, narratives without formal hierarchy

refs.label: Semantic chunking

questions.question: A pharmaceutical company is building a Bedrock Knowledge Base containing research documents from multiple departments (Clinical Trials, Regulatory Affairs, Manufacturing). Scientists need to query the Knowledge Base with department-specific filters to ensure they only receive relevant documents (e.g., "What are the latest stability testing protocols?" filtered to Manufacturing documents only). New documents are uploaded to S3 daily by automated pipelines. The solution must enable efficient metadata-based filtering in Knowledge Base queries with the LEAST operational overhead.Which solution should be implemented?
options: Configure the automated upload pipelines to set S3 object metadata (x-amz-meta-department) when uploading documents to S3. Configure the Bedrock Knowledge Base to extract this metadata during ingestion and enable metadata filtering in queries. Users can filter queries by specifying metadata conditions in the retrieval API.

Create a Step Functions workflow that triggers after S3 uploads. The workflow invokes a Lambda function to analyze document content, determine the department, and store metadata mappings in a DynamoDB table with document ID as the key. Modify the application to query DynamoDB first, then filter Knowledge Base results based on the metadata table.

Configure S3 Event Notifications to trigger a Lambda function when documents are uploaded. The Lambda function reads document content, extracts department information using Amazon Comprehend entity recognition, and writes metadata records to an Amazon RDS database. Query the RDS database before each Knowledge Base call to determine which documents match the filter criteria.

Use S3 Object Tags to categorize documents by department during upload. Create separate S3 prefixes for each department (s3://bucket/clinical-trials/, s3://bucket/regulatory/, etc.). Configure separate Knowledge Bases for each department, and route user queries to the appropriate Knowledge Base based on their department filter.
questions.explain: CORRECT: Configure the automated upload pipelines to set S3 object metadata (x-amz-meta-department) when uploading documents to S3. Configure the Bedrock Knowledge Base to extract this metadata during ingestion and enable metadata filtering in queries. Users can filter queries by specifying metadata conditions in the retrieval API. This solution uses Bedrock Knowledge Base's native metadata filtering capabilities with minimal operational overhead. By setting S3 object metadata during upload, the metadata is automatically extracted and indexed when the Knowledge Base ingests documents - no additional processing pipeline is needed. Knowledge Base queries support metadata filtering through the retrievalConfiguration parameter, allowing scientists to specify filters like {"equals": {"key": "department", "value": "Manufacturing"}} directly in the API call. The filtering happens at the vector search level, ensuring efficient retrieval of only relevant documents. Since metadata is set during upload by existing pipelines, there's no additional infrastructure to maintain, no separate databases to manage, and no complex orchestration workflows. This is the AWS-recommended approach for metadata-based filtering in Knowledge Bases. INCORRECT: Create a Step Functions workflow that triggers after S3 uploads. The workflow invokes a Lambda function to analyze document content, determine the department, and store metadata mappings in a DynamoDB table with document ID as the key. Modify the application to query DynamoDB first, then filter Knowledge Base results based on the metadata table. This solution adds significant unnecessary operational overhead. It requires building and maintaining a Step Functions workflow, Lambda functions for content analysis, and a DynamoDB table - all to replicate functionality that Bedrock Knowledge Bases provide natively. The application logic becomes more complex: query DynamoDB for eligible document IDs, pass those IDs to Knowledge Base, then post-filter results. This introduces multiple points of failure and latency (DynamoDB query + Knowledge Base query + filtering logic). Additionally, keeping DynamoDB in sync with Knowledge Base ingestion requires careful state management - if documents are re-ingested or deleted, the DynamoDB table must be updated separately. This violates the "LEAST operational overhead" requirement. INCORRECT: Configure S3 Event Notifications to trigger a Lambda function when documents are uploaded. The Lambda function reads document content, extracts department information using Amazon Comprehend entity recognition, and writes metadata records to an Amazon RDS database. Query the RDS database before each Knowledge Base call to determine which documents match the filter criteria. This solution is even more operationally complex than the previous one. It requires Lambda functions for content processing, Amazon Comprehend for entity extraction (adding cost for every document), and an RDS database with associated maintenance (backups, scaling, patching). Querying RDS before each Knowledge Base call doubles the query latency and creates a dependency on database availability. Using Comprehend to extract department information from content is unreliable - entity recognition might misidentify departments or miss them entirely, especially if documents don't explicitly mention department names in the text. The requirement states that documents are uploaded by automated pipelines that already know the department, so this content analysis step is completely unnecessary. This approach maximizes operational overhead rather than minimizing it. INCORRECT: Use S3 Object Tags to categorize documents by department during upload. Create separate S3 prefixes for each department (s3://bucket/clinical-trials/, s3://bucket/regulatory/, etc.). Configure separate Knowledge Bases for each department, and route user queries to the appropriate Knowledge Base based on their department filter. While S3 Object Tags could work for categorization, creating separate Knowledge Bases per department creates significant operational overhead. You must manage multiple Knowledge Base instances, each with separate ingestion jobs, vector stores, and monitoring. When a scientist wants to search across multiple departments (e.g., "Show me all GMP protocols" spanning both Manufacturing and Regulatory), the application must query multiple Knowledge Bases and merge results, adding complexity. Adding a new department requires provisioning an entirely new Knowledge Base. Costs multiply with each Knowledge Base instance. This architecture also doesn't leverage Knowledge Base's native metadata filtering - it works around the feature rather than using it. A single Knowledge Base with metadata filtering is far simpler and more maintainable. Key Concepts: S3 object metadata (x-amz-meta-*) = user-defined metadata extracted by Knowledge Base Metadata filtering = native Knowledge Base capability for query-time filtering RetrievalConfiguration = API parameter for specifying metadata filter conditions Operational overhead = infrastructure, maintenance, and complexity to manage

refs.label: Amazon Bedrock Knowledge Bases now supports metadata filtering to improve retrieval accuracy

questions.question: A media company uses an Amazon Bedrock model to automatically generate summaries for breaking news articles. The engineering team frequently updates the system prompt to improve the tone and brevity of the summaries. However, a recent prompt update caused a "regression" where the model started failing to capture the "Location" entity in 30% of the summaries, a critical requirement. The team needs an automated Quality Assurance (QA) workflow that runs every time a new prompt version is proposed. This workflow must test the new prompt against a "Golden Dataset" of 100 historical articles and block the deployment if the "Location Accuracy" score drops below 95%.Which architecture provides the most robust and automated solution for this testing pipeline?
options: Create an AWS Step Functions state machine with a Map state to iterate through the Golden Dataset. Inside the Map state, use a Lambda function to invoke the model with the new prompt, and a second "Evaluator" Lambda function to compare the output against the ground truth location. Aggregate the results and publish a custom metric "LocationAccuracy" to Amazon CloudWatch. Configure the CI/CD pipeline to fail if the metric is < 95.

Use Amazon SageMaker Model Monitor to schedule a daily job that captures the inference traffic from the production endpoint. Configure a "Data Quality" monitor to analyze the capture logs for missing location entities and send an Amazon SNS notification to the developer if the drift exceeds 5%.

Implement an Amazon CloudWatch Synthetics Canary that runs once every minute. Configure the canary script to send a sample news article to the Amazon Bedrock model and assert that the response contains a specific location string. If the canary fails, trigger a rollback of the prompt.

Deploy the new prompt to a "Shadow" variant on an Amazon SageMaker Endpoint. Route 10% of the live traffic to this shadow variant. Use Amazon QuickSight to visualize the difference in response length between the production and shadow variants manually before promoting the new prompt.
questions.explain: CORRECT: Create an AWS Step Functions state machine with a Map state to iterate through the Golden Dataset. Inside the Map state, use a Lambda function to invoke the model with the new prompt, and a second "Evaluator" Lambda function to compare the output against the ground truth location. Aggregate the results and publish a custom metric "LocationAccuracy" to Amazon CloudWatch. Configure the CI/CD pipeline to fail if the metric is < 95. This solution describes a classic "Offline Evaluation" or "Regression Testing" pipeline, which is the standard best practice for prompt engineering. Step Functions orchestrates the complex workflow (batch processing). Map State allows for parallel processing of the 100 test cases (Golden Dataset). Lambda "Evaluator" implements the specific "Business Logic" (did it find the location?) by comparing the generated text to the known correct answer (Ground Truth). CloudWatch acts as the "Gatekeeper," allowing the CI/CD pipeline to make a deterministic Go/No-Go decision based on the aggregated score. Disclaimer: This diagram is a simplified conceptual model intended for exam preparation. Some implementation details are intentionally omitted or abstracted to highlight the core logic relevant to the exam. INCORRECT: Use Amazon SageMaker Model Monitor to schedule a daily job that captures the inference traffic from the production endpoint. Configure a "Data Quality" monitor to analyze the capture logs for missing location entities and send an Amazon SNS notification to the developer if the drift exceeds 5%. This is "Online Monitoring" (post-deployment), not "Pre-deployment QA." By the time Model Monitor catches the issue, the bad prompt has already been live in production for up to 24 hours (daily job), affecting real users. The goal is to block the deployment. INCORRECT: Implement an Amazon CloudWatch Synthetics Canary that runs once every minute. Configure the canary script to send a sample news article to the Amazon Bedrock model and assert that the response contains a specific location string. If the canary fails, trigger a rollback of the prompt. Canaries are for "Availability/Health Checking" (is the endpoint up?), not for deep semantic evaluation of a dataset. Testing a single sample article once a minute is not a statistically significant regression test for a 100-item dataset. INCORRECT: Deploy the new prompt to a "Shadow" variant on an Amazon SageMaker Endpoint. Route 10% of the live traffic to this shadow variant. Use Amazon QuickSight to visualize the difference in response length between the production and shadow variants manually before promoting the new prompt. Shadow testing is a valid strategy, but this implementation is manual ("visualize... manually") and checks the wrong metric ("response length" instead of "location accuracy"). It does not meet the requirement for an automated system to block deployment based on accuracy .

refs.label: Map workflow state

questions.question: A logistics company is using a Foundation Model on Amazon Bedrock to generate delivery schedules for its drivers. The prompt provides a list of 15 complex business rules (e.g., "Refrigerated trucks cannot idle for > 30 mins," "Driver A cannot work past 5 PM," "Zone B is closed on Tuesdays"). Currently, the prompt asks: "Given these rules and this list of orders, create a schedule for Driver A." The model frequently generates schedules that look plausible but violate one or two subtle constraints (e.g., scheduling a Zone B delivery on a Tuesday).Which prompt engineering technique should the GenAI Developer implement to improve the model's logical reasoning and adherence to these constraints?
options: Implement a Chain-of-Thought (CoT) instruction pattern. Update the system prompt to say: "Think step-by-step. First, list all the constraints that apply to Driver A and Zone B. Second, check each order against these constraints. Third, build the schedule. Finally, verify the schedule against the constraints before outputting the result. "

Implement the ReAct (Reasoning and Acting) pattern. Configure the model with a tool definition that allows it to call an external "Calendar API" to check the current date and time before generating the schedule.
Use a "Few-Shot" prompting technique. Update the prompt to include 50 examples of valid schedules and 50 examples of invalid schedules to train the model on the correct pattern.
Enforce a structured output format. Modify the prompt to require the response to be a valid JSON object schema containing keys for time, location, and truck_id.
questions.explain: CORRECT: Implement a Chain-of-Thought (CoT) instruction pattern. Update the system prompt to say: "Think step-by-step. First, list all the constraints..." The core issue is that the model is jumping to the solution ("the schedule") without performing the intermediate reasoning steps required to handle 15 complex, conflicting rules. Chain-of-Thought (CoT) is the specific technique designed to solve this. By forcing the model to externalize its reasoning ("First list the constraints..."), you significantly reduce logic errors and hallucinations in complex reasoning tasks. This aligns directly with the skill example "chain-of-thought instruction patterns." INCORRECT: Implement the ReAct (Reasoning and Acting) pattern. Configure the model with a tool definition that allows it to call an external "Calendar API"... While ReAct is powerful, the issue here isn't a lack of external data (like "what day is it?"); the prompt likely provides the necessary context. The issue is the logic of applying the business rules to the orders. Calling a Calendar API doesn't help the model figure out that "Zone B is closed." INCORRECT: Use a "Few-Shot" prompting technique. Update the prompt to include 50 examples of valid schedules and 50 examples of invalid schedules... While Few-Shot helps, adding 100 examples (50 valid + 50 invalid) to the context window is inefficient (high token cost) and potentially confusing (context pollution). CoT is generally more effective and efficient for "logical constraint satisfaction" problems than massive few-shotting. INCORRECT: Enforce a structured output format. Modify the prompt to require the response to be a valid JSON object schema... Structured output (JSON) ensures the format is machine-readable, but it does nothing to ensure the content is logically correct. The model will just generate a perfectly formatted JSON schedule that still violates the "Tuesday" rule.

refs.label: Chain-of-thought prompting
Enhance model responses with model reasoning

questions.question: A marketing agency is building an automated content generator for social media posts. The workflow requires a sophisticated chain of prompts:Generate Draft: Take a topic and generate a draft post using a "Creative" model (e.g., Anthropic Claude 3.5 Sonnet).Safety Check: Pass the draft to a lightweight model (e.g., Amazon Titan Text) to classify if the content is "Safe" or "Risky".Conditional Logic:If "Safe": Pass the draft to a "Polishing" prompt to add emojis and hashtags.If "Risky": Pass the draft to a "Sanitization" prompt to remove controversial language. The team wants to build, visualize, and deploy this logic using a low-code, drag-and-drop interface directly within the Amazon Bedrock console.Which steps should the GenAI Developer take to implement this architecture? (Choose two)
options: Create an Amazon Bedrock Prompt Flow. Add three distinct "Prompt" nodes configured with the specific model IDs and inference parameters (Temperature) required for the Drafting, Polishing, and Sanitization steps.

Add a "Condition" node to the Prompt Flow. Define a conditional rule that evaluates the string output of the Safety Check node (e.g., Equals("Safe")) and routes the flow to the corresponding downstream Prompt node.

Create an Amazon Bedrock Agent. Define an Action Group containing the "Polishing" and "Sanitization" prompts as distinct API operations. Configure the agent to autonomously decide which action to call.
Use AWS Step Functions to create a state machine. Define a Choice state to evaluate the safety classification. Use the Task state to invoke the Bedrock:InvokeModel API for each prompt step.

Implement the logic in a single large Prompt Template using Chain-of-Thought (CoT). Instruct the model to generate the draft, check itself for safety, and then output the final polished or sanitized version in one response.
questions.explain: CORRECT: Create an Amazon Bedrock Prompt Flow. Add three distinct "Prompt" nodes configured with the specific model IDs and inference parameters (Temperature) required for the Drafting, Polishing, and Sanitization steps. Amazon Bedrock Prompt Flows is the specific feature designed for "sequential prompt chains" and visual orchestration of prompt logic. It allows developers to chain multiple prompts (nodes) together, where the output of one becomes the input of another, fulfilling the workflow requirement without writing external code. CORRECT: Add a "Condition" node to the Prompt Flow. Define a conditional rule that evaluates the string output of the Safety Check node (e.g., Equals("Safe")) and routes the flow to the corresponding downstream Prompt node. This addresses the "conditional branching based on model responses" requirement. Prompt Flows includes native Condition nodes that allow you to fork the logic path based on the data flowing through the graph (e.g., the output of the "Safety Check" model). This creates the "Safe" vs. "Risky" routing logic visually. INCORRECT: Create an Amazon Bedrock Agent. Define an Action Group containing the "Polishing" and "Sanitization" prompts as distinct API operations. Configure the agent to autonomously decide which action to call. Agents are for autonomous task execution and tool use (e.g., "Look up a flight"). They are less suitable for a strict, deterministic processing pipeline like this. Agents introduce variability/non-determinism. The requirement is for a fixed workflow (Draft -> Check -> Branch), which is the exact use case for Prompt Flows (Workflows), not Agents. INCORRECT: Use AWS Step Functions to create a state machine. Define a Choice state to evaluate the safety classification. Use the Task state to invoke the Bedrock:InvokeModel API for each prompt step. While Step Functions can do this, the question specifically asks to use the interface "directly within the Amazon Bedrock console." Prompt Flows is the native Bedrock solution for prompt chaining. Step Functions is a broader AWS application orchestration service. INCORRECT: Implement the logic in a single large Prompt Template using Chain-of-Thought (CoT). Instruct the model to generate the draft, check itself for safety, and then output the final polished or sanitized version in one response. While feasible for simple tasks, doing complex multi-step generation, self-correction, and branching in a single inference call is prone to errors (hallucination, instruction drift). It also prevents using different models for different steps (e.g., using a cheap model for safety checking and an expensive one for drafting), which is a key advantage of the Prompt Flow architecture.

How Amazon Bedrock Flows works
Use a template to create an Amazon Bedrock flow

questions.question: A financial services firm is preparing to ingest 50 TB of historical PDF loan applications into an Amazon Bedrock Knowledge Base for a RAG application. Before the ingestion pipeline runs, the compliance team requires a comprehensive audit of the source Amazon S3 bucket to identify any files containing unencrypted Credit Card Numbers (CCN) or Social Security Numbers (SSN).Which solution provides the most operationally efficient way to discover and report this sensitive data at scale?
options: Enable Amazon Macie for the account. Configure a sensitive data discovery job to scan the S3 bucket using the managed data identifiers for Financial Information and Personal Health Information.
Configure Amazon Inspector to scan the S3 bucket. Enable the "Deep Inspection" mode to analyze the object content for PII patterns and generate a finding report in AWS Security Hub.
Create an AWS Glue ETL job to crawl the S3 bucket. Add a "Find Sensitive Data" transform to the visual workflow to detect PII, and write the non-compliant file names to a DynamoDB table.
Enable Amazon GuardDuty S3 Protection. Configure a custom threat list containing regex patterns for CCNs and SSNs to alert whenever a sensitive file is accessed by the ingestion role.
questions.explain: CORRECT: Enable Amazon Macie for the account. Configure a sensitive data discovery job to scan the S3 bucket using the managed data identifiers for Financial Information and Personal Health Information. Amazon Macie is a data security service that uses machine learning and pattern matching to discover and protect sensitive data in AWS. It is specifically designed to scan Amazon S3 buckets for PII (like CCNs and SSNs) at scale. Using a "sensitive data discovery job" is the standard, managed way to audit a large dataset (50 TB) before processing it for GenAI use, satisfying the "operationally efficient" requirement. INCORRECT: Configure Amazon Inspector to scan the S3 bucket... Well, in my opinion that was a strong distractor answer… :) Amazon Inspector is used for vulnerability management (scanning EC2 instances, ECR images, and Lambda functions for software vulnerabilities and unintended network exposure). It does not scan S3 object content for data privacy (PII) findings; that is the specific domain of Amazon Macie. INCORRECT: Create an AWS Glue ETL job to crawl the S3 bucket... While AWS Glue can detect PII using the "Sensitive Data Detection" transform, setting up a full ETL job for a 50 TB one-time security audit is significantly more complex and "operationally heavy" than using Macie, which is a purpose-built security service. Glue is better suited for processing (masking/redacting) the data during movement, not just discovery/auditing. INCORRECT: Enable Amazon GuardDuty S3 Protection... Amazon GuardDuty S3 Protection detects potential security threats related to access patterns (e.g., unusual API calls, compromised credentials accessing the bucket). It does not inspect the content of the files to find PII. It monitors "who is touching the data," not "what is inside the data."

refs.label: Amazon Macie
What is Amazon Macie?
Running sensitive data discovery jobs

questions.question: A healthcare organization is building a generative AI application on Amazon EC2 instances located in a private subnet. The application processes highly sensitive patient data and uses Amazon Bedrock models for summarization. The compliance team enforces the following strict network security requirements:All traffic between the EC2 instances and Amazon Bedrock must remain entirely within the AWS global network and must never traverse the public internet.The connectivity solution must strictly enforce that only the specific IAM role associated with the EC2 application is permitted to invoke models through this network path.Which architecture implementation meets these requirements?
options: Create a Gateway VPC Endpoint for Amazon Bedrock. Update the route table of the private subnet to point traffic destined for the Amazon Bedrock service to the gateway endpoint. Attach a resource-based policy to the endpoint to restrict access to the EC2 IAM role.

Deploy a NAT Gateway in the public subnet and update the private subnet's route table to route internet-bound traffic through the NAT Gateway. Configure AWS Network Firewall to inspect the outbound traffic and whitelist the Amazon Bedrock public API endpoints.

Provision an Interface VPC Endpoint (AWS PrivateLink) for bedrock-runtime . Attach a VPC Endpoint Policy to this endpoint that explicitly allows the bedrock:InvokeModel action only for the Principal of the EC2 instance profile's IAM role.

Establish an AWS Direct Connect connection between the VPC and the AWS region. configure a Public Virtual Interface (VIF) to access Amazon Bedrock public endpoints privately. Use IAM policies on the EC2 instances to restrict access.
questions.explain: CORRECT: Provision an Interface VPC Endpoint (AWS PrivateLink) for bedrock-runtime. Attach a VPC Endpoint Policy to this endpoint that explicitly allows the bedrock:InvokeModel action only for the Principal of the EC2 instance profile's IAM role. This solution addresses the "protected AI environment" requirement by using VPC Endpoints to isolate the network. Amazon Bedrock uses Interface VPC Endpoints (powered by AWS PrivateLink), not Gateway Endpoints, to allow private connectivity from a VPC. By attaching a VPC Endpoint Policy , the developer can enforce granular access controls at the network boundary, ensuring that only the specific IAM principal (the EC2 role) can perform inference (bedrock:InvokeModel) through that endpoint. Disclaimer: This diagram is a simplified conceptual model intended for exam preparation. Some implementation details are intentionally omitted or abstracted to highlight the core logic relevant to the exam. INCORRECT: Create a Gateway VPC Endpoint for Amazon Bedrock... Gateway VPC Endpoints are only supported for Amazon S3 and Amazon DynamoDB. Amazon Bedrock requires Interface VPC Endpoints. INCORRECT: Deploy a NAT Gateway in the public subnet... A NAT Gateway routes traffic to the public internet (even if the source IP is masked). This violates the requirement that traffic "must never traverse the public internet." INCORRECT: Establish an AWS Direct Connect connection... AWS Direct Connect is primarily used for connecting on-premises data centers to AWS. Since the application is already running on Amazon EC2 within the VPC, Direct Connect is not the correct mechanism for VPC-to-AWS Service communication. Furthermore, a Public VIF connects to public endpoints, which does not provide the same level of network isolation as a VPC Endpoint within the VPC.

refs.label: Use interface VPC endpoints (AWS PrivateLink)
Gateway endpoints

questions.question: An aerospace engineering firm is developing a RAG (Retrieval Augmented Generation) application that allows mechanics to query thousands of technical maintenance manuals. To meet strict aviation safety regulations, the application requires full traceability for every answer generated:Source Attribution: Every response generated by the model must explicitly cite the specific manual ID and version number it originated from.Data Governance: All valid maintenance datasets must be centrally registered and version-controlled.Auditability: Access to the underlying data files during the retrieval process must be logged for compliance audits.Which implementation strategy satisfies these requirements?
options: Register the maintenance manuals in the AWS Glue Data Catalog. During the ingestion process, attach metadata tags (Manual ID, Version) to the document chunks stored in the vector database and configure the application to return these tags with the response. Enable AWS CloudTrail to log the data access API calls.

Use Amazon S3 Object Tagging to label each PDF with its Manual ID. Configure the Amazon Bedrock model to read these tags directly from S3 during inference. Use Amazon S3 Server Access Logs to track the requests made by the model.

Ingest the manuals into Amazon Kendra and enable the "Document relevance tuning" feature to prioritize recent versions. Use Amazon CloudWatch Logs to capture the full text of every query and response for the audit team to manually verify the sources later.

Store the manuals in Amazon DynamoDB with a Global Secondary Index (GSI) for the Manual ID. Use Amazon Macie to discover and classify the technical data. Configure the GenAI application to query DynamoDB Streams to retrieve the source lineage.
questions.explain: CORRECT: Register the maintenance manuals in the AWS Glue Data Catalog. During the ingestion process, attach metadata tags (Manual ID, Version) to the document chunks stored in the vector database and configure the application to return these tags with the response. Enable AWS CloudTrail to log the data access API calls. This solution directly implements the components listed in the exam guide for data source tracking: AWS Glue Data Catalog is used to "register data sources," providing a governed, central repository of what data exists and its schema. Metadata tagging on the vector embeddings is the standard mechanism for "source attribution in FM-generated content". When the RAG system retrieves a chunk, it pulls the metadata (Manual ID) along with it, allowing the UI to display the citation. AWS CloudTrail is the correct service for "audit logging" of API calls and data access events. INCORRECT: Use Amazon S3 Object Tagging to label each PDF... While S3 tags exist, Foundation Models cannot "read tags directly from S3" during inference. The context must be retrieved via a search/RAG process. S3 Server Access Logs are less readable and harder to audit than CloudTrail. INCORRECT: Ingest the manuals into Amazon Kendra and enable "Document relevance tuning"... Kendra is a valid search service, but "relevance tuning" does not guarantee source attribution visibility. Furthermore, relying on manual verification via CloudWatch Logs does not meet the requirement for systematic traceability in the application response. INCORRECT: Store the manuals in Amazon DynamoDB... DynamoDB is generally not the primary store for large PDF manuals (S3 is better). Amazon Macie is for sensitive data discovery (PII/Security), not for tracking data lineage or source attribution for technical manuals.

refs.label: Data discovery and cataloging in AWS Glue

questions.question: A pharmaceutical company is fine-tuning Foundation Models (FMs) on Amazon SageMaker to assist with clinical trial documentation. To comply with strict regulatory audit requirements, the MLOps team must implement a traceability system that allows auditors to traverse the entire history of a deployed model.Specifically, the system must allow auditors to query a relationship graph to identify exactly which version of the raw training data in Amazon S3 was used by a specific training job to produce a specific model version currently running on a SageMaker Endpoint.Which solution meets these governance requirements using AWS native tools?
options: Enable AWS CloudTrail data events for the training data S3 bucket. Use Amazon Athena to query the logs for GetObject API calls made by the SageMaker execution role and correlate the event timestamps with the model creation time.

Use Amazon SageMaker ML Lineage Tracking. Configure the training jobs to register the S3 data location as an Artifact and create an Association between the data artifact, the training job, and the resulting model package in the Model Registry.

Configure AWS Glue crawlers to catalog the training data in Amazon S3. Enable the AWS Glue Data Lineage feature to automatically map the dependency between the S3 objects and the Amazon SageMaker inference endpoint.

Implement a tagging strategy using the AWS CLI. During the data preprocessing step, tag the S3 objects with the ModelVersionID . Use the Amazon Resource Groups Tagging API to search for all S3 objects that share a tag with the deployed model.
questions.explain: CORRECT: Use Amazon SageMaker ML Lineage Tracking. Configure the training jobs to register the S3 data location as an Artifact and create an Association between the data artifact, the training job, and the resulting model package in the Model Registry. Amazon SageMaker ML Lineage Tracking is the specific capability designed to create and store information about the steps of a machine learning workflow. It creates a graph structure that links Entities (like S3 data, Models, and Endpoints) via Associations (relationships like "Produced" or "ContributedTo"). This allows for the precise queryability required by the auditors (e.g., "Show me the artifacts associated with this endpoint"). It provides the necessary traceability from the raw data through the training job to the final model artifact. Disclaimer: This diagram uses simplified icons for educational clarity. Official AWS architecture icons may differ. The concepts, services, and relationships depicted are based on AWS SageMaker documentation. INCORRECT: Enable AWS CloudTrail data events for the training data S3 bucket. Use Amazon Athena to query the logs for GetObject API calls... While CloudTrail logs who accessed the data, it does not create a structured lineage graph. Correlating GetObject events by timestamp is an unreliable heuristic (inference vs. training access) and does not formally prove that specific data produced a specific model artifact. It is an audit log of access , not provenance . INCORRECT: Configure AWS Glue crawlers to catalog the training data in Amazon S3. Enable the AWS Glue Data Lineage feature... AWS Glue Data Lineage is designed to visualize the flow of data through ETL (Extract, Transform, Load) jobs within Glue. It does not automatically extend to tracking Amazon SageMaker training jobs or the artifacts (model.tar.gz) produced by them. It solves the data engineering lineage, not the ML model lineage. INCORRECT: Implement a tagging strategy using the AWS CLI... Tagging is useful for cost allocation and basic organization, but it is static and manual. S3 object tags do not inherently support the complex many-to-many relationships or the historical versioning required to trace a model's lifecycle graph (e.g., one dataset used in many trials). It does not meet the requirement for a "relationship graph."

refs.label: Amazon SageMaker ML Lineage Tracking
Lineage Tracking Entities
