The startup wants to process this dataset with the lowest possible compute cost while offloading the complexity of job scheduling, retries, and infrastructure provisioning to AWS.Which combination of configuration steps should the developer implement?(Choose two)
options: Create an AWS Batch Compute Environment configured to use Spot Instances with an allocation strategy of price-capacity-optimized.
Define an AWS Batch Job Definition that specifies the Docker image URI, GPU resource requirements, and the command to process a single data object. Submit the work as an Array Job to the Job Queue.
Deploy the container to an Amazon SageMaker Real-time Inference endpoint. Write a Python script to iterate through the S3 bucket and send synchronous requests to the endpoint.
Use Amazon Bedrock Batch Inference. create a CreateModelInvocationJob request pointing to the S3 bucket containing the protein data.
Configure an Amazon S3 Event Notification to trigger an AWS Lambda function for every new object. Configure the Lambda function to download the container image and run the inference code.
questions.explain: CORRECT: Create an AWS Batch Compute Environment configured to use Spot Instances with an allocation strategy of price-capacity-optimized. AWS Batch is the ideal service for planning, scheduling, and running containerized batch ML workloads at scale. By selecting Spot Instances , the developer can achieve the "lowest possible compute cost" (often up to 90% savings) for fault-tolerant workloads. The price-capacity-optimized strategy balances cost savings with the likelihood of interruption. CORRECT: Define an AWS Batch Job Definition that specifies the Docker image URI, GPU resource requirements, and the command to process a single data object. Submit the work as an Array Job to the Job Queue. A Job Definition acts as a blueprint for the batch job, specifying the container image (the custom GenAI model) and resources (GPUs). Array Jobs are specifically designed to process large collections of similar items (like 10 million files) efficiently without submitting 10 million individual API calls. INCORRECT: Deploy the container to an Amazon SageMaker Real-time Inference endpoint. Write a Python script to iterate through the S3 bucket and send synchronous requests to the endpoint. Real-time endpoints are designed for low-latency, always-on inference. Using them for a massive batch job is cost-inefficient (paying for uptime rather than just processing) and requires maintaining a client-side script to manage the iteration, retries, and error handling, which AWS Batch handles natively. INCORRECT: Use Amazon Bedrock Batch Inference. create a CreateModelInvocationJob request pointing to the S3 bucket containing the protein data. Amazon Bedrock Batch Inference is only available for Foundation Models provided by Amazon Bedrock. It cannot run custom containerized models (like the proprietary protein folding model described in the scenario). INCORRECT: Configure an Amazon S3 Event Notification to trigger an AWS Lambda function for every new object... AWS Lambda has strict timeouts (15 minutes) and limited storage/GPU options compared to full EC2 instances. It is generally unsuitable for loading heavy, custom GenAI containers for computationally intensive simulations, and triggering 10 million individual functions would likely hit concurrency limits.

refs.label: Instance type allocation strategies for AWS Batch
Array jobs

questions.question: A social media platform allows users to generate custom profile avatars using a Foundation Model hosted on Amazon Bedrock. Marketing campaigns frequently cause massive traffic spikes, resulting in thousands of simultaneous image generation requests. The current architecture uses Amazon API Gateway triggering an AWS Lambda function synchronously. During these spikes, the application fails with ThrottlingException errors because the workload exceeds the Amazon Bedrock provisioned throughput quota. The developer needs to implement an event-driven solution that buffers requests and processes them at a controlled rate to prevent throttling, without dropping any user requests.Which combination of architectural changes should the developer implement?(Choose two)
options: Update the Amazon API Gateway integration type to "AWS Service" and configure it to send the incoming requests directly to an Amazon SQS queue.

Configure an AWS Lambda function to poll the Amazon SQS queue. Set the reserved concurrency on the Lambda function to a value that ensures the aggregate request rate remains below the Amazon Bedrock throughput quota.
Enable Provisioned Concurrency on the existing AWS Lambda function to ensure that enough execution environments are pre-initialized to handle the peak traffic volume immediately.
Replace the Amazon API Gateway with an Application Load Balancer (ALB). Configure the ALB to route traffic to the AWS Lambda function using a weighted target group to distribute the load.
Implement an Amazon EventBridge rule to capture the API Gateway events and route them to an Amazon Kinesis Data Firehose stream for batch processing.
questions.explain: CORRECT: Update the Amazon API Gateway integration type to "AWS Service" and configure it to send the incoming requests directly to an Amazon SQS queue. This establishes the decoupling required for an event-driven architecture. By sending requests to SQS instead of invoking Lambda synchronously, the system "buffers" the traffic spike. The API Gateway can immediately acknowledge receipt (e.g., HTTP 200 OK) to the user, ensuring no requests are dropped due to downstream pressure. CORRECT: Configure an AWS Lambda function to poll the Amazon SQS queue. Set the reserved concurrency on the Lambda function to a value that ensures the aggregate request rate remains below the Amazon Bedrock throughput quota. This implements the "controlled rate" processing mechanism. By setting reserved concurrency, the developer strictly limits the number of concurrent Lambda instances processing messages from the queue. This acts as a throttle valve, ensuring that the number of simultaneous calls to Amazon Bedrock never exceeds the quota, effectively resolving the ThrottlingException errors while working through the backlog in the queue. INCORRECT: Enable Provisioned Concurrency on the existing AWS Lambda function to ensure that enough execution environments are pre-initialized to handle the peak traffic volume immediately. Provisioned Concurrency helps with cold starts (latency), but it does not solve the throughput quota issue. In fact, scaling up Lambda concurrency to meet the spike immediately is exactly what causes the Bedrock throttling. INCORRECT: Replace the Amazon API Gateway with an Application Load Balancer (ALB). Configure the ALB to route traffic to the AWS Lambda function using a weighted target group to distribute the load. An ALB is a synchronous trigger. It would still invoke the Lambda function immediately upon request arrival, passing the traffic spike directly to the downstream service and failing to solve the throttling issue. INCORRECT: Implement an Amazon EventBridge rule to capture the API Gateway events and route them to an Amazon Kinesis Data Firehose stream for batch processing. While Kinesis Data Firehose buffers data, it is primarily designed for loading data into destinations like S3 or Redshift for analytics, not for acting as a work queue for transactional processing (image generation) with granular concurrency control.

refs.label: Configuring scaling behavior for SQS event source mappings

questions.question: A financial services firm is building an internal "Analyst Assistant" using a Generative AI agent. The agent uses Amazon Athena to query a data lake stored in Amazon S3 and registered in the AWS Glue Data Catalog. The dataset includes a Transaction_ID, Merchant, Date, and a highly sensitive Customer_PAN (Primary Account Number) column. The security team requires that the IAM role assumed by the agent must be technically blocked from retrieving any data from the Customer_PAN column, while maintaining full access to the rest of the dataset for analysis.Which solution should the developer implement to enforce this column-level restriction?
options: Switch the Glue Data Catalog to AWS Lake Formation permission model. Create a Data filter for the table that explicitly excludes the Customer_PAN column. Grant SELECT permissions to the agent's IAM role with this data filter applied.

In AWS Lake Formation, define a Row-level security filter with the expression Customer_PAN IS NULL. Grant SELECT permissions to the agent's IAM role using this filter to ensure no sensitive numbers are exposed.

Create a dedicated LF-Tag named Sensitive in AWS Lake Formation. Assign this tag to the Customer_PAN column. Update the agent's IAM role permissions in Lake Formation to Deny access to resources tagged with Sensitive.

Enable Hybrid Access Mode in AWS Lake Formation for the database. Configure an IAM policy for the agent's role that allows glue:GetTable on the table resource but includes an explicit Deny statement for the glue:GetColumn action targeting the Customer_PAN column.
questions.explain: CORRECT: Switch the Glue Data Catalog to AWS Lake Formation permission model. Create a Data filter for the table that explicitly excludes the Customer_PAN column. Grant SELECT permissions to the agent's IAM role with this data filter applied. This describes the correct usage of AWS Lake Formation Data Filters (specifically Column-level security). By creating a filter that excludes specific columns and attaching it to the grant for the IAM principal, Lake Formation dynamically filters the schema presented to Athena. The agent sees the table and all other columns, but the sensitive column is effectively removed from the projection, satisfying the security requirement without breaking the dataset. INCORRECT: In AWS Lake Formation, define a Row-level security filter with the expression Customer_PAN IS NULL. Grant SELECT permissions to the agent's IAM role using this filter to ensure no sensitive numbers are exposed. This is a Row-level filter, not a column-level filter. This configuration would hide entire rows where the PAN is present. Since most transaction records presumably have a PAN, this would result in the agent receiving an empty or severely truncated dataset, violating the requirement to "maintain full access to the rest of the dataset." INCORRECT: Create a dedicated LF-Tag named Sensitive in AWS Lake Formation. Assign this tag to the Customer_PAN column. Update the agent's IAM role permissions in Lake Formation to Deny access to resources tagged with Sensitive. While LF-Tags (Tag-based access control) are a valid Lake Formation feature, the permission model is generally additive (Allow). Attempting to "Deny" a single column via tags while "Allowing" the table often results in complex permission evaluation failures or requires explicitly granting the table without the column tag. The Data Filter method (the correct answer) is the standard, direct mechanism for column exclusion. INCORRECT: Enable Hybrid Access Mode in AWS Lake Formation for the database. Configure an IAM policy for the agent's role that allows glue:GetTable on the table resource but includes an explicit Deny statement for the glue:GetColumn action targeting the Customer_PAN column. Standard IAM policies for AWS Glue do not support granular Deny actions on specific columns for Athena query execution. Athena uses the Glue catalog for metadata, but the enforcement of column-level visibility during a query is a specific capability of Lake Formation , not standard IAM Glue policies. Hybrid mode is for migration, not for achieving granular column security that IAM cannot provide.

refs.label: What is AWS Lake Formation?
Column-level security

questions.question: A financial services institution is building a proprietary 175-billion parameter Foundation Model (FM) to power a real-time fraud detection assistant. The project lifecycle involves two critical phases with distinct infrastructure requirements:Training Phase: The model must be pre-trained on petabytes of encrypted transaction logs. This requires synchronizing gradients across thousands of GPUs with minimal network latency to accelerate convergence.Deployment Phase: The fraud detection assistant must analyze transactions interactively with sub-millisecond latency. The traffic is highly variable, requiring the infrastructure to scale out automatically during market hours and scale in at night.Which combination of architectural strategies should the GenAI Developer implement to meet these requirements?(Choose two)
options: Launch the training job on Amazon EC2 UltraClusters (e.g., p4d instances) within Amazon SageMaker. Enable the SageMaker Distributed Data Parallel (SMDDP) library to maximize throughput and efficiently synchronize gradients across the high-speed Elastic Fabric Adapter (EFA) network.

Deploy the trained model to an Amazon SageMaker Real-time inference endpoint. Configure Target Tracking scaling policies to verify the SageMakerVariantInvocationsPerInstance metric and automatically adjust the number of instances (data parallelism) to handle the fluctuating traffic.

Deploy the model to Amazon SageMaker Asynchronous Inference. Configure the endpoint to buffer the incoming transactions in an internal queue, allowing the model to process them in large batches to maximize throughput.

Use Amazon SageMaker Serverless Inference for the training phase. Configure the MaxConcurrency parameter to parallelize the data processing across thousands of ephemeral Lambda-based compute environments to reduce costs.

Implement SageMaker Model Parallelism (SMP) on a fleet of Amazon EC2 Spot Instances for the inference deployment. Use the lowest-price allocation strategy to ensure the real-time endpoint remains cost-effective during peak market hours.
questions.explain: CORRECT: Launch the training job on Amazon EC2 UltraClusters (e.g., p4d instances) within Amazon SageMaker. Enable the SageMaker Distributed Data Parallel (SMDDP) library to maximize throughput and efficiently synchronize gradients across the high-speed Elastic Fabric Adapter (EFA) network. Amazon EC2 UltraClusters provide the massive scale and high-speed networking (EFA) required for training LLMs. SageMaker Distributed Data Parallel (SMDDP) is the specific library designed to optimize inter-node communication for data-parallel training, ensuring efficient gradient synchronization at this scale. CORRECT: Deploy the trained model to an Amazon SageMaker Real-time inference endpoint. Configure Target Tracking scaling policies to verify the SageMakerVariantInvocationsPerInstance metric and automatically adjust the number of instances (data parallelism) to handle the fluctuating traffic. This option addresses the Deployment Phase requirements. SageMaker Real-time inference is the ideal choice for workloads requiring interactive, low-latency responses (like real-time fraud detection). Enabling autoscaling implements data parallelism at the inference layer—adding more model replicas (instances) to handle parallel requests from different users as traffic increases. INCORRECT: Deploy the model to Amazon SageMaker Asynchronous Inference... Asynchronous Inference is designed for large payloads and long processing times (minutes), not for "interactive, sub-millisecond latency." It introduces a queue delay that would violate the real-time requirement. INCORRECT: Use Amazon SageMaker Serverless Inference for the training phase... SageMaker Serverless Inference is for inference , not training. Furthermore, serverless compute does not support the massive GPU requirements (clusters of P4d/P5 instances) needed to train a 175B parameter model. INCORRECT: Implement SageMaker Model Parallelism (SMP) on a fleet of Amazon EC2 Spot Instances for the inference deployment... Using Spot Instances for a real-time production endpoint is risky because instances can be reclaimed with only a 2-minute warning, potentially causing downtime or dropped transactions during fraud analysis. Spot is better suited for fault-tolerant batch workloads or training with checkpointing.

refs.label: Introduction to the SageMaker AI distributed data parallelism library
Amazon EC2 UltraClusters

questions.question: A financial services firm is designing a generative AI application to analyze proprietary market data. The application requires access to a specific proprietary Foundation Model (FM) available only on Amazon Bedrock.The business has outlined the following critical requirements:Performance: The application must handle a steady stream of 500 requests per minute with guaranteed capacity and predictable, low latency during market hours.Security: All network traffic between the application VPC and the AI model must remain entirely within the AWS network and never traverse the public internet.Which architectural design meets these requirements?
options: Configure the Amazon Bedrock model in On-Demand mode to optimize costs during off-peak hours. Set up a NAT Gateway in the application VPC to route API calls securely to the public Amazon Bedrock service endpoints.

Purchase Provisioned Throughput for the specific Foundation Model in Amazon Bedrock to reserve model units. Configure an interface VPC endpoint (AWS PrivateLink) for Amazon Bedrock and update the route table to direct model traffic to the endpoint.

Deploy the proprietary FM to an Amazon SageMaker real-time inference endpoint using a custom container. Use AWS Direct Connect to establish a dedicated private connection between the application VPC and the SageMaker service.

Use Amazon Bedrock with a Batch Inference job configuration to process the requests in groups. Store the input data in an Amazon S3 bucket with a bucket policy that restricts access to the application’s IAM role.
questions.explain: CORRECT: Purchase Provisioned Throughput for the specific Foundation Model in Amazon Bedrock to reserve model units. Configure an interface VPC endpoint (AWS PrivateLink) for Amazon Bedrock and update the route table to direct model traffic to the endpoint - Provisioned Throughput allows the customer to purchase "Model Units" that provide guaranteed processing capacity, meeting the steady stream/predictable latency requirement. AWS PrivateLink (interface VPC endpoint) enables private connectivity to AWS services, ensuring traffic stays within the AWS network backbone without exposing it to the public internet. This combination satisfies both the performance and security constraints. Disclaimer: This diagram is a conceptual visualization designed to illustrate the specific exam logic. It abstracts certain production details to focus on the correct answer. INCORRECT: Configure the Amazon Bedrock model in On-Demand mode to optimize costs during off-peak hours. Set up a NAT Gateway in the application VPC to route API calls securely to the public Amazon Bedrock service endpoints - On-Demand mode relies on shared infrastructure and is subject to throttling, which fails to meet the requirement for "guaranteed capacity" and "predictable latency" at high volumes. Furthermore, a NAT Gateway routes traffic out to the public internet (even if it doesn't leave the AWS edge network), which violates the strict requirement to never traverse the public internet. INCORRECT: Deploy the proprietary FM to an Amazon SageMaker real-time inference endpoint using a custom container. Use AWS Direct Connect to establish a dedicated private connection between the application VPC and the SageMaker service - Most proprietary FMs available on Bedrock (e.g., Anthropic Claude, Amazon Titan) are managed services and cannot be exported to run in a custom container on SageMaker. Additionally, AWS Direct Connect is primarily used for linking on-premises data centers to AWS, not for connecting an AWS VPC to an AWS Service (SageMaker). INCORRECT: Use Amazon Bedrock with a Batch Inference job configuration to process the requests in groups. Store the input data in an Amazon S3 bucket with a bucket policy that restricts access to the application’s IAM role - Batch inference is designed for asynchronous, large-scale processing where immediate responses are not required. It does not meet the requirement for a "steady stream" of requests with "low latency" needed for real-time market analysis.

refs.label: Use interface VPC endpoints (AWS PrivateLink)

questions.question: A legal technology company is building a generative AI application to draft contract clauses. The application must utilize a repository of 200,000 proprietary legal documents that is updated with new case files every night.The Chief Legal Officer has set the following strict requirements:Attribution: Every generated clause must include a direct citation to the specific case file it was derived from.Freshness: The model must incorporate data from documents added to the repository within the last 24 hours.Accuracy: The solution must minimize hallucinations and strictly adhere to the facts present in the proprietary documents.Which architectural strategy should the GenAI engineer implement to meet these requirements?
options: Use Amazon Bedrock Custom Models to fine-tune a base Large Language Model (LLM) on the proprietary documents. Configure an automated pipeline using Amazon SageMaker Pipelines to re-train and deploy a new model version every night.

Implement a Retrieval Augmented Generation (RAG) architecture using Amazon Bedrock Knowledge Bases. Configure the data source to sync with the document repository daily and set the generation configuration to return source citations.

Use Amazon SageMaker JumpStart to deploy an open-source model to a real-time inference endpoint. Implement a LangChain workflow that loads the entire document dataset into the model's context window for each inference request.

Perform Continued Pre-training on an Amazon Titan model using the proprietary documents to adapt the model to the legal domain. Use the resulting custom model to generate clauses based on its internal knowledge of the dataset.
questions.explain: CORRECT: Implement a Retrieval Augmented Generation (RAG) architecture using Amazon Bedrock Knowledge Bases. Configure the data source to sync with the document repository daily and set the generation configuration to return source citations. RAG is the standard architectural pattern for requirements involving frequent data updates and strict attribution. By keeping the knowledge external to the model, the system can easily ingest new data (freshness) by syncing the Knowledge Base. Bedrock Knowledge Bases natively support retrieving source chunks and providing citations, which directly addresses the attribution and accuracy requirements. Disclaimer: This diagram is a conceptual visualization designed to illustrate the specific exam logic. It abstracts certain production details to focus on the correct answer. INCORRECT: Use Amazon Bedrock Custom Models to fine-tune a base Large Language Model (LLM) on the proprietary documents. Configure an automated pipeline using Amazon SageMaker Pipelines to re-train and deploy a new model version every night. While fine-tuning adapts a model's style, it is not suitable for data that updates frequently (daily) due to the high cost and time required for training. Furthermore, fine-tuned models store information in their parameters, which makes extracting exact citations (attribution) difficult and prone to hallucination compared to retrieval-based methods. INCORRECT: Use Amazon SageMaker JumpStart to deploy an open-source model to a real-time inference endpoint. Implement a LangChain workflow that loads the entire document dataset into the model's context window for each inference request. Loading 200,000 documents into a context window for every request is technically unfeasible (exceeding token limits of almost all models) and cost-prohibitive. This "context stuffing" approach does not scale for large corpora. INCORRECT:Perform Continued Pre-training on an Amazon Titan model using the proprietary documents to adapt the model to the legal domain. Use the resulting custom model to generate clauses based on its internal knowledge of the dataset. Continued Pre-training is efficient for teaching a model a new language or domain vocabulary (e.g., "Legalese"), but like fine-tuning, it embeds knowledge into static weights. It does not solve the "daily update" requirement efficiently, nor does it provide a reliable mechanism for exact source citations.

questions.question: A mobile application developer is using an Amazon Bedrock base model (in On-Demand mode) to power a real-time conversational assistant. During marketing campaigns, traffic spikes unpredictably, causing the application to frequently receive ThrottlingException errors from the Bedrock API because the workload exceeds the service quotas in the primary AWS Region.The developer needs a solution to maximize throughput and reliability during these spikes without managing complex failover logic or predicting exact capacity requirements.Which approach should the developer implement?
options: Implement a retrying mechanism with exponential backoff using the AWS SDK within the application code. If the retries are exhausted, fallback to a smaller, less capable model hosted on Amazon SageMaker JumpStart in the same region.
Configure an Amazon Bedrock Cross-Region Inference profile. Update the application to direct inference requests to the profile's ARN instead of a specific region-based model ID.

Purchase Provisioned Throughput for the model in the primary region. Configure AWS Application Auto Scaling to dynamically adjust the number of Provisioned Model Units based on the InvocationThrottles CloudWatch metric.

Decouple the application from the model using Amazon SQS and AWS Lambda. Configure the Lambda function to process requests from the queue and send them to Amazon Bedrock, ensuring that the concurrency limit of the Lambda function matches the Bedrock transaction per minute (TPM) quota.
questions.explain: CORRECT: Configure an Amazon Bedrock Cross-Region Inference profile. Update the application to direct inference requests to the profile's ARN instead of a specific region-based model ID. Amazon Bedrock Cross-Region Inference allows you to route traffic across multiple AWS Regions dynamically. By using an inference profile, Amazon Bedrock automatically routes requests to a region where capacity is available, smoothing out spikes and increasing effective throughput without the developer needing to manually manage load balancing or replicate infrastructure across regions. This is the most efficient way to handle unpredictable bursts and avoid throttling. Disclaimer: This diagram is a conceptual visualization designed to illustrate the specific exam logic. It abstracts certain production details to focus on the correct answer. INCORRECT: Implement a retrying mechanism with exponential backoff using the AWS SDK within the application code. If the retries are exhausted, fallback to a smaller, less capable model hosted on Amazon SageMaker JumpStart in the same region. Exponential backoff is a standard error-handling strategy, but it does not solve the root cause of hitting regional capacity limits during high traffic. It simply delays the requests, which increases latency for a real-time application. Furthermore, falling back to a different model in the same region does not address regional service disruptions or capacity shortages. INCORRECT: Purchase Provisioned Throughput for the model in the primary region. Configure AWS Application Auto Scaling to dynamically adjust the number of Provisioned Model Units based on the InvocationThrottles CloudWatch metric. While Provisioned Throughput guarantees capacity, it is generally intended for steady-state workloads with predictable patterns. More importantly, Amazon Bedrock Provisioned Throughput does not currently support AWS Application Auto Scaling to add/remove Model Units dynamically in real-time based on throttling metrics. Model Units are a fixed commitment resource. INCORRECT: Decouple the application from the model using Amazon SQS and AWS Lambda. Configure the Lambda function to process requests from the queue and send them to Amazon Bedrock, ensuring that the concurrency limit of the Lambda function matches the Bedrock transaction per minute (TPM) quota. This architecture converts a synchronous, real-time conversational flow into an asynchronous one. While this prevents the API from being overwhelmed, it introduces significant latency (queuing time) that degrades the user experience for a conversational assistant. It essentially "smooths" traffic by making users wait, rather than finding more capacity.

questions.question: An e-commerce company uses an AWS Step Functions workflow to orchestrate a customer support chatbot. One state in the workflow invokes an Amazon Bedrock model to generate return instructions. Recently, the workflow has failed intermittently due to timeouts during peak traffic, causing the chatbot to hang and leaving customers without a response.Which combination of actions should the GenAI Developer take to improve the resilience of this workflow and ensure a responsive user experience during disruptions? (Choose two)
options: Add a Catch field to the Step Functions task that invokes the Amazon Bedrock model. Configure a fallback state to invoke a different, lightweight model in a secondary AWS Region if the primary task fails.
Configure Amazon Route 53 with a weighted routing policy to distribute the model inference API calls across Bedrock endpoints in two different Regions.

Define a TimeoutSeconds value for the Bedrock task state. Create a fallback path that returns a pre-defined, static set of general return instructions to the user if the model fails to respond within the limit.

Replace the synchronous Step Functions workflow with an Amazon SQS queue and a Lambda function. Configure a Dead Letter Queue (DLQ) to automatically re-drive failed requests to a model in a different region.
Enable Amazon CloudFront in front of the Amazon Bedrock service endpoint to cache successful model responses and serve them during service outages.
questions.explain: CORRECT: Add a Catch field to the Step Functions task that invokes the Amazon Bedrock model. Configure a fallback state to invoke a different, lightweight model in a secondary AWS Region if the primary task fails. This implements the "Circuit Breaker" pattern explicitly mentioned in the exam guide. By catching errors (such as TaskFailed or Timeout), the workflow can dynamically switch to a backup resource (cross-Region fallback) instead of failing completely. Disclaimer: This diagram is a conceptual visualization designed to illustrate the specific exam logic. It abstracts certain production details to focus on the correct answer. CORRECT: Define a TimeoutSeconds value for the Bedrock task state. Create a fallback path that returns a pre-defined, static set of general return instructions to the user if the model fails to respond within the limit. This is an example of a "graceful degradation strategy". If the advanced AI capability is unavailable or too slow, the system "degrades" to a simpler, reliable method (static text) to ensure the user still receives helpful information rather than an error or a hang. Disclaimer: This diagram is a conceptual visualization designed to illustrate the specific exam logic. It abstracts certain production details to focus on the correct answer. INCORRECT: Configure Amazon Route 53 with a weighted routing policy to distribute the model inference API calls across Bedrock endpoints in two different Regions. Amazon Route 53 is used for routing traffic to application endpoints (like EC2 or API Gateway), not for directly load-balancing AWS Service API calls (like bedrock-runtime.invokeModel) between regions. The application logic or SDK configuration controls which regional endpoint is called. INCORRECT: Replace the synchronous Step Functions workflow with an Amazon SQS queue and a Lambda function. Configure a Dead Letter Queue (DLQ) to automatically re-drive failed requests to a model in a different region. A Dead Letter Queue (DLQ) is designed to store messages that failed processing so they can be analyzed later; it does not automatically "re-drive" logic to a different region or fix real-time outages. This would strictly isolate the failure but not provide a real-time resolution for the waiting user. INCORRECT: Enable Amazon CloudFront in front of the Amazon Bedrock service endpoint to cache successful model responses and serve them during service outages. You cannot place Amazon CloudFront directly in front of the public Amazon Bedrock service API. CloudFront is for content delivery of your own web applications. Additionally, caching does not solve the issue for new, unique user queries that have not been asked before.

questions.question: A large enterprise is scaling its adoption of generative AI. An internal audit reveals that development teams are implementing inconsistent observability standards: some teams are logging raw prompts containing PII to plain text logs, while others have disabled logging entirely. These practices violate the "Security" and "Operational Excellence" pillars of the AWS Well-Architected Framework Generative AI Lens.As a Lead GenAI Developer, how should you ensure consistent, compliant implementation of these standards across all future projects with minimal developer friction?
options: Use the AWS Well-Architected Tool to define a Custom Lens that includes a mandatory checklist for PII redaction and logging. Configure the CI/CD pipeline to pause deployment until a Cloud Architect manually approves the workload review in the tool.

Create a standardized library of AWS Cloud Development Kit (AWS CDK) constructs that instantiate Amazon Bedrock clients with pre-configured Guardrails and appropriate CloudWatch logging filters. Publish this library to AWS CodeArtifact for teams to import into their application code.

Configure AWS Config rules to monitor the bedrock:InvokeModel API calls. Set up an auto-remediation action that automatically terminates any IAM session that attempts to call the model without the correct logging parameters.

Deploy a central Amazon API Gateway as a proxy in a shared services VPC. Hardcode the logging and guardrail configurations in the API Gateway and force all development teams to change their application endpoints to route through this proxy instead of calling Bedrock directly.
questions.explain: CORRECT: Create a standardized library of AWS Cloud Development Kit (AWS CDK) constructs that instantiate Amazon Bedrock clients with pre-configured Guardrails and appropriate CloudWatch logging filters. Publish this library to AWS CodeArtifact for teams to import into their application code. This approach directly addresses the skill of creating "standardized technical components." By encapsulating the complex, compliant configuration (logging, PII protection via Guardrails) into reusable CDK constructs or CloudFormation modules, you ensure that any team using these components inherits the best practices defined in the Generative AI Lens by default. This "Paved Road" approach enforces consistency programmatically. Disclaimer: This diagram is a conceptual visualization designed to illustrate the specific exam logic. It abstracts certain production details to focus on the correct answer. INCORRECT: Use the AWS Well-Architected Tool to define a Custom Lens that includes a mandatory checklist for PII redaction and logging. Configure the CI/CD pipeline to pause deployment until a Cloud Architect manually approves the workload review in the tool. While the Well-Architected Tool helps assess compliance, relying on manual checklists and human approval creates a bottleneck. It does not provide the "technical components" (code/infrastructure) to help developers implement the solution correctly; it only checks if they claimed to have done so. INCORRECT: Configure AWS Config rules to monitor the bedrock:InvokeModel API calls. Set up an auto-remediation action that automatically terminates any IAM session that attempts to call the model without the correct logging parameters. AWS Config monitors resource configuration (infrastructure), not real-time API data plane calls like InvokeModel. Additionally, terminating IAM sessions is an aggressive, destructive action that disrupts development rather than enabling consistent implementation. INCORRECT: Deploy a central Amazon API Gateway as a proxy in a shared services VPC. Hardcode the logging and guardrail configurations in the API Gateway and force all development teams to change their application endpoints to route through this proxy instead of calling Bedrock directly. While a gateway pattern is valid, routing all internal GenAI traffic through a single proxy creates a massive bottleneck and a single point of failure. It also prevents teams from using advanced Bedrock features (like Agents or streaming) that might not be fully supported by a rigid proxy implementation. The CDK approach (Client-side standardization) is generally preferred for flexibility and scalability in this context.

refs.label: Generative AI Lens - AWS Well-Architected Framework
Best practices for scaling AWS CDK adoption within your organization

questions.question: A legal technology firm is building an automated contract review system. The system must ingest complete merger and acquisition agreements, which average 75 pages (approximately 45,000 tokens) in length. The application requires the model to perform deep reasoning to cross-reference clauses across the entire document and identify subtle liability risks with the highest possible accuracy.Latency is not a concern, as the reviews are performed asynchronously overnight.Which Foundation Model on Amazon Bedrock is the most appropriate choice for this use case?
options: Amazon Titan Text Express
Anthropic Claude 3 Opus
Anthropic Claude 3 Haiku
AI21 Labs Jurassic-2 Ultra
questions.explain: CORRECT: Anthropic Claude 3 Opus: While you won’t get a direct question like this about models, getting familiar with models that you can access via Amazon Bedrock is beneficial. :) Now, here is the explanation.This use case prioritizes high accuracy and deep reasoning over speed or cost. Claude 3 Opus is optimized for the most complex tasks and demonstrates top-tier performance on reasoning benchmarks. Furthermore, its 200,000-token context window easily accommodates the 45,000-token input requirement in a single prompt, ensuring the model can "see" the entire contract to cross-reference clauses effectively. INCORRECT: Amazon Titan Text Express: While Titan Text Express is a cost-effective, general-purpose model, it has a maximum context window of 8,192 tokens. It cannot ingest a 45,000-token document in a single pass, making it technically unsuitable for this specific requirement without complex (and potentially accuracy-degrading) chunking strategies. INCORRECT: Anthropic Claude 3 Haiku: Claude 3 Haiku is optimized for speed and cost efficiency (low latency). While it supports the necessary context window (200k), the business requirement explicitly prioritizes "highest possible accuracy" and "deep reasoning" over latency. Haiku is a lighter model and would likely miss subtle risks that the larger, more powerful Opus model would catch. INCORRECT: AI21 Labs Jurassic-2 Ultra: Similar to Titan Text Express, the Jurassic-2 Ultra model has a context window limitation (8,192 tokens) that prevents it from processing the entire 75-page contract in a single context. It fails the technical constraint regarding input size.

refs.label: Anthropic's Claude models on Amazon Bedrock
Supported foundation models in Amazon Bedrock

questions.question: A SaaS company provides a generative AI writing assistant. The company wants to implement a tiered subscription model where "Free Tier" users are routed to a smaller, faster model (e.g., Amazon Titan Text), while "Premium Tier" users are routed to a more capable model (e.g., Anthropic Claude 3 Opus). The operations team needs the ability to change which specific model IDs are assigned to each tier instantly, without redeploying the application code or restarting the backend Lambda functions.Which architectural approaches allow the team to achieve this dynamic selection without code modifications? (Choose two)
options: Store the model-to-tier mapping configuration in an AWS AppConfig hosted configuration profile. Update the backend AWS Lambda function to retrieve the configuration using the AWS AppConfig Lambda extension.
Use AWS CloudFormation parameters to define the model IDs. Trigger a stack update with new parameter values whenever the model mapping needs to change.
Define the model IDs as Stage Variables in Amazon API Gateway. configure the backend Lambda function to read the model ID from the event object context passed by the API Gateway.
Hardcode the model IDs into the AWS Lambda environment variables. Update the function configuration via the AWS CLI whenever a model switch is required.
Create separate Amazon Bedrock Provisioned Throughput units for each model. Use an Application Load Balancer (ALB) to route traffic to the specific Provisioned Throughput ARN based on the URL path.
questions.explain: CORRECT: Store the model-to-tier mapping configuration in an AWS AppConfig hosted configuration profile. Update the backend AWS Lambda function to retrieve the configuration using the AWS AppConfig Lambda extension. AWS AppConfig is explicitly designed for this use case. It allows you to store configuration data (like { "free": "titan-v1", "premium": "claude-v3" }) separate from the code. The Lambda extension retrieves and caches this config. When the operations team updates the profile in AppConfig, the Lambda function picks up the change immediately at runtime without a deployment or restart. Disclaimer: This diagram is a conceptual visualization designed to illustrate the specific exam logic. It abstracts certain production details to focus on the correct answer. CORRECT: Define the model IDs as Stage Variables in Amazon API Gateway. configure the backend Lambda function to read the model ID from the event object context passed by the API Gateway. Stage Variables act as configuration attributes associated with a specific API Gateway deployment stage. By mapping the model ID to a stage variable (e.g., modelId = anthropic.claude-v2), you can pass this value dynamically to the backend Lambda function. Changing the stage variable value updates the model selection without touching the backend code. INCORRECT: Use AWS CloudFormation parameters to define the model IDs. Trigger a stack update with new parameter values whenever the model mapping needs to change. While this avoids changing source code , it requires a CloudFormation stack update , which is a deployment event. This process is slower and riskier than runtime configuration changes and does not meet the requirement for "instant" updates without redeployment. INCORRECT: Hardcode the model IDs into the AWS Lambda environment variables. Update the function configuration via the AWS CLI whenever a model switch is required. Updating Lambda environment variables triggers a constrained update of the function (a new function version or forcing a cold start to reload variables). While better than changing code, it is still considered a deployment/configuration change to the compute resource, whereas AppConfig and API Gateway allow changes external to the compute resource. INCORRECT: Create separate Amazon Bedrock Provisioned Throughput units for each model. Use an Application Load Balancer (ALB) to route traffic to the specific Provisioned Throughput ARN based on the URL path. Application Load Balancers cannot route traffic directly to Amazon Bedrock ARNs. Bedrock is an AWS API service, not a target group resource like EC2 or Lambda. The routing logic must exist in the application layer (Lambda/API Gateway), not the network load balancer.

refs.label: AWS AppConfig Lambda extension
API Gateway stage variables

questions.question: A media company uses a generative AI application to summarize news articles. The application runs on AWS Lambda and currently uses Amazon Titan Text Express. The product team wants to perform an A/B test by routing 10% of the production traffic to Anthropic Claude 3 Haiku to evaluate if the summary quality justifies the cost difference. They require the ability to adjust this percentage (e.g., increase to 20% or roll back to 0%) immediately without triggering a new CI/CD deployment or modifying the Lambda function code.Which solution meets these requirements?
options: Use AWS Systems Manager Parameter Store to hold the model ID. Update the Lambda function to poll the parameter store on every invocation. To implement the 10% routing, update the parameter value to a JSON string containing both model IDs and the split ratio.

Implement an AWS AppConfig feature flag. Define a split traffic strategy in AppConfig that routes 10% of the sessions to the "variation" (Claude 3 Haiku) and 90% to the "default" (Titan Text). Configure the Lambda function to use the AWS AppConfig Lambda extension to retrieve the flag status.

Deploy a second Lambda function configured to use Claude 3 Haiku. Create a Canary deployment in Amazon API Gateway. Set the percentTraffic on the canary stage to 10.0 to route requests to the new Lambda function.

Use Amazon CloudWatch Evidently to create a project. Define a feature with two variations (Titan and Claude). Insert the EvaluateFeature SDK code into the Lambda function. Use the CloudWatch console to adjust the traffic distribution.
questions.explain: CORRECT: Implement an AWS AppConfig feature flag. Define a split traffic strategy in AppConfig that routes 10% of the sessions to the "variation" (Claude 3 Haiku) and 90% to the "default" (Titan Text). Configure the Lambda function to use the AWS AppConfig Lambda extension to retrieve the flag status. AWS AppConfig is the specific service built for this use case. It allows for "Feature Flags" with native traffic splitting capabilities (e.g., "roll out to 10%"). The Lambda extension handles the polling and caching efficiently (preventing latency issues). The operations team can adjust the percentage sliders in the AppConfig console, and the change is reflected instantly in the application without touching the code or deployment pipelines. Disclaimer: This diagram is a conceptual visualization designed to illustrate the specific exam logic. It abstracts certain production details to focus on the correct answer. INCORRECT: Use AWS Systems Manager Parameter Store to hold the model ID. Update the Lambda function to poll the parameter store on every invocation. To implement the 10% routing, update the parameter value to a JSON string containing both model IDs and the split ratio. While Parameter Store can hold configuration, polling it on every invocation introduces significant latency and API cost (throttling risk). More importantly, Parameter Store does not natively support "traffic splitting" or feature flag logic. The developer would have to write custom logic inside the Lambda code to parse the JSON and calculate the random 10% split, which technically violates the spirit of "no code modifications" to logic (you are effectively moving the logic into a config string). INCORRECT: Deploy a second Lambda function configured to use Claude 3 Haiku. Create a Canary deployment in Amazon API Gateway. Set the percentTraffic on the canary stage to 10.0 to route requests to the new Lambda function. API Gateway Canary deployments are designed for testing new API versions (deployment safety), not for feature toggling model providers. This approach requires maintaining two separate Lambda functions (infrastructure duplication). While it achieves traffic splitting, it is a deployment-heavy strategy (Stage variables/Deployment history) rather than a configuration-based strategy. INCORRECT: Use Amazon CloudWatch Evidently to create a project. Define a feature with two variations (Titan and Claude). Insert the EvaluateFeature SDK code into the Lambda function. Use the CloudWatch console to adjust the traffic distribution. Well, I confess :) - this is a very strong distractor because CloudWatch Evidently is designed for A/B testing. However, keep in mind that - AWS AppConfig is the primary service highlighted in the Exam Guide for "dynamic configuration" and "provider switching." AppConfig is generally preferred for operational feature flagging (configuration management), while Evidently is used for deep experimental analytics. In the context of the exam guide's (which I spent a lot of time reading…) explicit mention of AppConfig for Skill 1.2.2, AppConfig is the standard answer for architectural configuration patterns.

refs.label: Understanding multi-variant feature flag rules
Using AWS AppConfig Feature Flags

questions.question: A financial analytics firm uses a 70-billion parameter open-source Foundation Model (FM) to categorize complex investment documents. The data science team retrains the model weekly to adapt to new market terminologies. Currently, the team performs full fine-tuning and saves the entire model (approx. 140 GB) to Amazon S3 for every version. This approach has led to unsustainable storage costs and slow deployment times, making it difficult to roll back quickly if a new version degrades performance.Which lifecycle management strategy should the GenAI Developer implement to resolve these issues while maintaining model performance?
options: Use Amazon Bedrock Continued Pre-training to update the model. Save the output to Amazon S3 Standard-Infrequent Access (S3 Standard-IA) to reduce storage costs. Update the application to point to the new custom model ARN after training completes.

Adopt Low-Rank Adaptation (LoRA) for the fine-tuning process. Register the lightweight adapter artifacts in the Amazon SageMaker Model Registry. Deploy the adapters to a SageMaker endpoint that hosts the frozen base model, allowing for rapid version switching.

Switch to a Retrieval Augmented Generation (RAG) architecture using Amazon OpenSearch Serverless. Stop fine-tuning the model and instead index the new market terminologies into the vector database to provide context dynamically.

Implement a Blue/Green deployment strategy using AWS CodeDeploy. Configure the pipeline to perform full fine-tuning, compress the resulting model using quantization, and deploy it to a new fleet of Amazon EC2 instances to reduce the memory footprint.
questions.explain: CORRECT: Adopt Low-Rank Adaptation (LoRA) for the fine-tuning process. Register the lightweight adapter artifacts in the Amazon SageMaker Model Registry. Deploy the adapters to a SageMaker endpoint that hosts the frozen base model, allowing for rapid version switching. This addresses all constraints. LoRA (Low-Rank Adaptation) is a Parameter-Efficient Fine-Tuning (PEFT) technique that creates very small adapter files (MBs instead of GBs) rather than a full model copy, solving the storage issue. SageMaker Model Registry provides the required versioning and lifecycle tracking (Approved/Rejected status). Deploying adapters to a base model allows for rapid updates and rollbacks because the heavy base model does not need to be reloaded; only the small adapter is swapped. Disclaimer: This diagram is a conceptual visualization designed to illustrate the specific exam logic. It abstracts certain production details to focus on the correct answer. INCORRECT: Use Amazon Bedrock Continued Pre-training to update the model. Save the output to Amazon S3 Standard-Infrequent Access (S3 Standard-IA) to reduce storage costs. Update the application to point to the new custom model ARN after training completes. Continued Pre-training is a heavy, expensive process typically used for teaching a model a completely new language or domain, not for weekly terminology updates. It also produces full model weights, which does not solve the storage or deployment speed "lifecycle" issues effectively. INCORRECT: Switch to a Retrieval Augmented Generation (RAG) architecture using Amazon OpenSearch Serverless. Stop fine-tuning the model and instead index the new market terminologies into the vector database to provide context dynamically. While RAG is a valid pattern for data freshness, this question specifically asks for a solution to the fine-tuning lifecycle management problem ("retrains the model weekly"). Switching architectures entirely avoids the problem rather than solving the specific deployment and lifecycle challenge posed (optimizing the customization process). INCORRECT: Implement a Blue/Green deployment strategy using AWS CodeDeploy. Configure the pipeline to perform full fine-tuning, compress the resulting model using quantization, and deploy it to a new fleet of Amazon EC2 instances to reduce the memory footprint. While quantization reduces size, performing full fine-tuning weekly is still inefficient. Managing EC2 fleets manually for model serving adds operational overhead compared to SageMaker. This solution does not leverage the Model Registry for version control, making rollback and governance harder.

refs.label: Easily deploy and manage hundreds of LoRA adapters with SageMaker efficient multi-adapter inference
Fine-tune models with adapter inference components
Model Registration Deployment with Model Registry

questions.question: An insurance company is building a RAG application using Amazon Bedrock Knowledge Bases. The source data consists of thousands of PDF claim forms containing dense tables with rows for medical codes and costs.During testing, the developer notices that the application frequently answers questions about specific line items incorrectly. An analysis reveals that the default ingestion process is flattening the tables into unstructured text, causing the embedding model to lose the relationship between the columns.Which configuration change should the developer allows the Knowledge Base to interpret these tables correctly with the LEAST development effort?
options: Configure an AWS Lambda function to pre-process the PDFs using Amazon Textract. Convert the table outputs into Markdown format before uploading the files to the ingestion bucket.

Enable the Advanced parsing strategy in the Amazon Bedrock Knowledge Base data source configuration. Select a Foundation Model (e.g., Anthropic Claude 4.5 Sonnet) to interpret the document structure during ingestion.
Change the chunking strategy from "Fixed-size chunking" to "Semantic chunking." Configure the model to identify breakpoints based on the visual layout of the PDF pages.
Implement a "Parent-Child" chunking strategy. Index the individual table cells as child chunks and the table header as the parent chunk to preserve the column context.
questions.explain: CORRECT: Enable the Advanced parsing strategy in the Amazon Bedrock Knowledge Base data source configuration. Select a Foundation Model (e.g., Anthropic Claude 4.5 Sonnet) to interpret the document structure during ingestion. Advanced parsing is a managed feature within Amazon Bedrock Knowledge Bases specifically designed to handle complex non-standard document structures like tables and charts. By enabling this feature (and selecting an FM to power it), Bedrock automatically parses the tables and maintains their structural context during the chunking/embedding process. This is the "LEAST development effort" because it is a configuration toggle, unlike writing custom extraction scripts. INCORRECT: (Textract + Lambda): While valid, this requires writing and maintaining custom Lambda code to orchestrate Textract and format the output (e.g., to Markdown). This is significantly higher development effort than the managed "Advanced parsing" feature. (Semantic Chunking): Semantic chunking breaks text based on meaning (sentences/paragraphs), not visual layout or table structure. It would likely still fragment the table rows incorrectly. (Parent-Child Chunking): While effective for context, "Parent-Child" chunking is a retrieval strategy implementation detail. Bedrock Knowledge Bases natively supports Hierarchical chunking, but manually defining table cells as children requires complex custom pre-processing, whereas Advanced Parsing handles the table understanding automatically.

refs.label: Parsing options for your data source
Amazon Bedrock Knowledge Bases now supports advanced parsing, chunking

questions.question: A digital marketing agency wants to use a Foundation Model on Amazon Bedrock to generate social media captions for a youth-focused fashion brand. The brand uses a highly specific, quirky "Gen Z" tone of voice that standard models fail to replicate, often sounding too formal. The agency has curated a high-quality dataset of 1,000 previous successful captions that perfectly capture this style.The team needs to adapt a model to consistently generate text in this specific style with the lowest computational cost and development effort.Which customization strategy should the GenAI Developer choose?
options: Perform Continued Pre-training on an Amazon Titan Text model using the caption dataset. This will update the model's weights to understand the new domain vocabulary inherent in the quirky language.

Use Amazon Bedrock Fine-tuning to train an Amazon Titan Text Express model. Provide the dataset in JSONL format with "input" (product description) and "output" (quirky caption) pairs to adapt the model's style and instruction-following behavior.

Implement a Retrieval Augmented Generation (RAG) architecture. Index the 1,000 captions in an Amazon Bedrock Knowledge Base and configure the model to retrieve five similar captions as context for every new generation request.
Train a Low-Rank Adaptation (LoRA) adapter using Amazon SageMaker JumpStart. Deploy the adapter to a dedicated SageMaker endpoint and invoke it using the SageMaker Runtime API.
questions.explain: CORRECT: Use Amazon Bedrock Fine-tuning to train an Amazon Titan Text Express model. Provide the dataset in JSONL format with "input" (product description) and "output" (quirky caption) pairs to adapt the model's style and instruction-following behavior. Fine-tuning is the correct technique for adapting a model's style, tone, or instruction-following behavior (like "speak like a Gen Z fashion brand") using a relatively small labeled dataset (1,000 examples is sufficient for style transfer). Amazon Bedrock provides a managed experience for this. INCORRECT: "Perform Continued Pre-training on an Amazon Titan Text model..." Continued Pre-training is used to teach a model new domain knowledge (e.g., medical textbooks, a new language) using massive amounts of unlabeled data (billions of tokens). It is overkill and less effective for simple style adaptation compared to instruction fine-tuning. INCORRECT: "Implement a Retrieval Augmented Generation (RAG) architecture..." RAG is for retrieving facts and information, not for enforcing a specific writing style. While providing few-shot examples via RAG can help, fine-tuning burns the style into the model weights, providing higher consistency and lower latency/cost per request (no retrieval step needed) for style tasks. INCORRECT: "Train a Low-Rank Adaptation (LoRA) adapter using Amazon SageMaker JumpStart..." While SageMaker JumpStart supports LoRA, the requirement is for the "lowest development effort." Amazon Bedrock provides a fully managed serverless customization workflow that avoids managing instances, endpoints, or container infrastructure, making it the preferred "AWS Native" choice for this scenario.

refs.label: Customize your model to improve its performance for your use case
Customize models in Amazon Bedrock with your own data using fine-tuning

questions.question: An e-commerce platform maintains a catalog of 50 million product images, each with a text description. The product team wants to build a "Universal Search" feature that allows users to search the catalog using either a text query (e.g., "red running shoes") or by uploading an image (e.g., a photo of a sneaker).The developer needs to generate vector embeddings that place both the product images and the text descriptions into the same vector space to enable this cross-modal search capability.Which Amazon Bedrock model should the developer select for this task?
options: Amazon Titan Image Generator G1. Use the encoding component of the model to convert input images into latent space vectors and store them in the vector database.

Amazon Titan Multimodal Embeddings G1. Pass both the product images and the text descriptions to the model to generate comparable vectors. Store these embeddings in an Amazon OpenSearch Service index.

Amazon Titan Text Embeddings v2. Use an optical character recognition (OCR) tool to extract text from the images, then generate embeddings for the extracted text and the product descriptions using the cohere.embed-english-v3 model.
Anthropic Claude 3 Sonnet. Use the multimodal capabilities of the Claude model to describe the input image in text, then use Amazon Titan Text Embeddings G1 to convert that description into a vector.
questions.explain: CORRECT: Amazon Titan Multimodal Embeddings G1 . Pass both the product images and the text descriptions to the model to generate comparable vectors. Store these embeddings in an Amazon OpenSearch Service index. Amazon Titan Multimodal Embeddings G1 is specifically designed to convert images and text into the same semantic vector space. This allows for "search by image," "search by text," or "image-to-text" matching, which meets the "Universal Search" requirement. INCORRECT: "Amazon Titan Image Generator G1. Use the encoding component..." Titan Image Generator is for creating or editing images. While it uses internal representations, it does not expose a public API for generating searchable vector embeddings for a database. INCORRECT: "Amazon Titan Text Embeddings v2. Use an optical character recognition (OCR) tool..." This approach relies on text extracted from the image. If the image is a shoe (with no text on it), OCR will fail, and the search will not work. It loses the visual semantic meaning (color, shape, style). INCORRECT: "Anthropic Claude 3 Sonnet. Use the multimodal capabilities..." This is a "multi-hop" workaround (Image -> Text -> Vector). It is slow, expensive, and less accurate than using a purpose-built multimodal embedding model that natively understands visual features.

refs.label: Amazon Titan Multimodal Embeddings G1 model
Build a reverse image search engine with Amazon Titan Multimodal Embeddings

questions.question: A global news agency uses Amazon Bedrock to summarize breaking news in real-time. The application processes high volumes of traffic that can spike unpredictably during major world events. Recently, the application experienced downtime because the throughput in the us-east-1 region was exhausted, resulting in ThrottlingException errors.The architecture team requires a solution that automatically routes inference traffic across multiple AWS Regions to maximize throughput and resilience without requiring the application code to manage complex failover logic.Which feature should the GenAI Developer implement?
options: Amazon Bedrock Provisioned Throughput. Purchase model units in us-east-1 and us-west-2. Use Amazon Route 53 latency-based routing to direct client traffic to the region with the lowest latency.

Amazon Bedrock Cross-Region Inference. Update the application to invoke the model using an inference profile ID (e.g., us.anthropic.claude-3-5-sonnet-20240620-v1:0) instead of a specific regional model ARN.

Amazon SageMaker Endpoints. Deploy the model to endpoints in three regions. Place an Amazon API Gateway in front of the endpoints and use a Network Load Balancer to distribute traffic using a round-robin algorithm.
Amazon EventBridge. Create a rule that detects ThrottlingException events. Configure the rule to trigger a Lambda function that updates the application configuration to point to a backup region.
questions.explain: CORRECT: Amazon Bedrock Cross-Region Inference. Update the application to invoke the model using an inference profile ID (e.g., us.anthropic.claude-3-5-sonnet-20240620-v1:0) instead of a specific regional model ARN. Amazon Bedrock Cross-Region Inference allows you to use an inference profile ID (such as us.anthropic.claude... or eu.anthropic.claude...). When you call this profile, Amazon Bedrock automatically routes the request to any region within that geo-location (e.g., N. Virginia, Oregon, Ohio) where capacity is available. This provides seamless burst capacity and resilience against single-region throttling without client-side failover code. INCORRECT: "Amazon Bedrock Provisioned Throughput... Use Amazon Route 53..." Route 53 routes network traffic, but it doesn't know if a Bedrock model is throttled. Provisioned Throughput guarantees capacity but is expensive and region-bound. Cross-Region Inference is a more dynamic, cost-effective solution for unpredictable bursts across regions. INCORRECT: "Amazon SageMaker Endpoints... Deploy the model to endpoints in three regions..." This requires managing infrastructure (SageMaker endpoints) and load balancers, significantly increasing operational overhead compared to using the managed Bedrock feature. INCORRECT: "Amazon EventBridge. Create a rule that detects ThrottlingException..." This is a reactive "failover" pattern. It is slower (requests fail before switching) and more complex to maintain than the active-active routing provided by Cross-Region Inference profiles.

refs.label: Increase throughput with cross-Region inference
Getting started with cross-region inference in Amazon Bedrock

questions.question: A healthcare software provider is building a "Clinical Note Summarizer." Due to regulatory requirements, every change to the system prompt must be versioned, tested, and approved by a compliance officer before it can be used in the production application.The development team wants to avoid storing prompts in Git repositories or databases where the compliance team cannot easily test them. They need a centralized, native AWS solution to manage the prompt lifecycle.Which workflow meets these governance requirements?
options: Use Amazon Bedrock Prompt Management. Create a prompt template and save drafts as new versions. Use the "Release" feature to create an immutable version. The application refers to the prompt by its ARN and version alias (e.g., PROD).

Store the prompts in AWS Systems Manager Parameter Store. Use parameter versions to track changes. Enable "Advanced" parameters to enforce an approval workflow using AWS Change Manager before a parameter value can be updated.

Use Amazon S3 Versioning. Upload prompts as text files to a versioned S3 bucket. Use S3 Object Lambda to inject the prompt into the application request. Use S3 Object Lock to prevent modification of approved prompts.

Create an Amazon Bedrock Agent. Define the system prompt in the agent's instruction set. To version the prompt, create a new Agent Alias for each iteration and route production traffic to the approved alias ID.
questions.explain: CORRECT: Use Amazon Bedrock Prompt Management. Create a prompt template and save drafts as new versions. Use the "Release" feature to create an immutable version. The application refers to the prompt by its ARN and version alias (e.g., PROD). Amazon Bedrock Prompt Management is the dedicated service for this use case. It allows developers to create, test, and version prompts within the AWS console. It supports: Versioning: Creating immutable snapshots of prompts. Aliasing: Pointing PROD to specific versions. Testing: The compliance officer can use the "Test" window in the console to verify the prompt output before approving a version release. INCORRECT: "Store the prompts in AWS Systems Manager Parameter Store..." While Parameter Store handles versioning, it lacks the specific GenAI capabilities (like a "Playground" to test the prompt with a model) that the compliance officer needs. It is a configuration tool, not a prompt engineering tool. INCORRECT: "Use Amazon S3 Versioning..." Similar to Parameter Store, S3 stores the file but provides no interface for testing or evaluating the prompt against a model, making the approval process difficult for non-developers. INCORRECT: "Create an Amazon Bedrock Agent..." Agents are for complex, multi-step tasks with tool usage. Using an Agent just to manage a single prompt version is architecturally "heavy" and incorrect. Prompt Management is the decoupled resource specifically for managing prompt assets.

refs.label: Construct and store reusable prompts with Prompt management
Create a prompt using Prompt management

questions.question: A financial technology company is aggregating customer chat logs to fine-tune a model. The source data resides in an Amazon S3 bucket. Before the data can be used for training, all Personally Identifiable Information (PII)—specifically names, credit card numbers, and SSNs—must be redacted.The data engineering team wants to integrate this redaction step into their existing ETL pipeline with minimal custom code.Which solution should the developer implement?
options: Create an AWS Glue ETL job. Use the built-in "Sensitive Data Detection" transform to identify the PII entities. Configure the transform to replace the detected text with a redaction string (e.g., [REDACTED]) and write the clean dataset to a new S3 bucket.
Use Amazon Macie to scan the S3 bucket. Configure a "Data Discovery Job" to tag objects containing PII. Use an S3 Bucket Policy to deny the training role access to any object tagged as "Sensitive."

Configure Amazon Bedrock Guardrails. Apply a guardrail with a "Sensitive Information" filter to the fine-tuning job configuration. This will automatically mask PII in the training data during the training process.
Use Amazon Comprehend. Write a Python script using the DetectPiiEntities API. Iterate through every object in the S3 bucket, find the PII offsets, replace the text string, and re-upload the object.
questions.explain: CORRECT: Create an AWS Glue ETL job. Use the built-in "Sensitive Data Detection" transform to identify the PII entities. Configure the transform to replace the detected text with a redaction string (e.g., [REDACTED]) and write the clean dataset to a new S3 bucket. AWS Glue provides a native, no-code/low-code visual transform called "Sensitive Data Detection". It can scan the dataset row-by-row during the ETL process, identify PII entities (like SSN, Credit Cards), and redact or hash them in the output. This prepares a clean dataset ready for fine-tuning. INCORRECT: "Use Amazon Macie to scan the S3 bucket..." Macie is for discovery and alerting, not for ETL/transformation. It tags the file as "containing PII" but does not remove the PII from the file content. Blocking the file would mean losing the training data entirely, rather than cleaning it. INCORRECT: "Configure Amazon Bedrock Guardrails... Apply a guardrail... to the fine-tuning job..." Guardrails are applied during inference (chatting with the model), not during fine-tuning (training the model). You must clean the data before feeding it into a training job. INCORRECT: "Use Amazon Comprehend... Write a Python script..." While this works, it requires writing and maintaining significant custom code (iterating S3, managing API calls, handling retries). The requirement was "minimal custom code," which AWS Glue satisfies better as a managed ETL service.

refs.label: AWS Glue PySpark transforms reference
What is AWS Glue?

questions.question: A wealth management firm is launching a "Portfolio Summarizer" RAG application using Amazon Bedrock. The application retrieves private investment documents from a Knowledge Base to generate client updates.During testing, the compliance team discovered that when a client asked about a stock not present in their portfolio, the model "hallucinated" a generic market summary based on its pre-training data, rather than stating the information was missing.The firm requires a strict safety control that blocks any response not factually supported by the retrieved source documents.Which Amazon Bedrock Guardrails configuration should the developer implement to address this requirement?
options: Enable the "relevance" metric in an Amazon Bedrock Model Evaluation job to continuously score the semantic similarity between the generated summary and the retrieved chunks.

Configure a "Contextual Grounding Check" filter within the Amazon Bedrock Guardrail. Set the threshold for "Grounding" and "Relevance" to high to block responses that are not based on the source data.
Implement a "Denied Topics" filter in the Guardrail configuration. Add a list of all stock tickers that are not currently held in the client's portfolio to the blocklist.
Use the Amazon Bedrock "Prompt Attack" filter to detect if the user is attempting to override the system instructions to force the model to use its internal knowledge.
questions.explain: CORRECT: Configure a "Contextual Grounding Check" filter within the Amazon Bedrock Guardrail. Set the threshold for "Grounding" and "Relevance" to high to block responses that are not based on the source data. The Contextual Grounding Check is a specific capability of Amazon Bedrock Guardrails designed to detect hallucinations in RAG applications. It evaluates if the generated response is factually supported by the source (Grounding) and if it answers the user's query (Relevance). If the model generates info not in the source (hallucination), this filter blocks the response. INCORRECT: "Enable the 'relevance' metric in an Amazon Bedrock Model Evaluation job..." Model Evaluation is an offline or batch process for testing performance. It provides a report after the fact. It does not act as a runtime guardrail to block specific responses during a live user interaction. INCORRECT: "Implement a 'Denied Topics' filter... Add a list of all stock tickers..." This is unscalable and ineffective. You cannot maintain a dynamic blocklist of every stock not in a portfolio. Denied topics are for broad categories (e.g., "Investment Advice"), not dynamic data validation. INCORRECT: "Use the Amazon Bedrock 'Prompt Attack' filter..." Prompt Attack filters detect jailbreaks (e.g., "Ignore previous instructions"). The scenario describes a hallucination/grounding issue, not a malicious adversarial attack by the user.

refs.label: Contextual grounding check – A new policy type to detect hallucinations

questions.question: A government agency is building a classified document analysis tool on Amazon EC2 instances hosted in a private subnet. The application uses Amazon Bedrock for inference.To prevent data exfiltration, the security policy mandates that the EC2 instances must only be able to invoke models owned by the agency's specific AWS account. They must be technically prevented from sending data to Amazon Bedrock models in any other AWS account (e.g., a rogue developer's personal account).Which networking and IAM combination enforces this control?
options: Configure the EC2 instance Security Group to allow outbound HTTPS traffic only to the specific IP addresses of the agency's provisioned model endpoints.
Create an Amazon VPC Interface Endpoint for Amazon Bedrock. Attach a VPC Endpoint Policy that denies all actions unless the aws:PrincipalAccount matches the agency's AWS Account ID.
Use AWS Network Firewall to inspect the payload of the InvokeModel API calls. Create a rule to block any JSON payload that contains a modelId corresponding to a public base model.
Update the IAM role attached to the EC2 instances to include a Condition element restricting the bedrock:InvokeModel action to specific Resource ARNs (the agency's models).
questions.explain: CORRECT: Create an Amazon VPC Interface Endpoint for Amazon Bedrock. Attach a VPC Endpoint Policy that denies all actions unless the aws:PrincipalAccount matches the agency's AWS Account ID. This is the standard pattern for preventing data exfiltration to unauthorized accounts (Data Perimeter). By attaching a policy to the VPC Endpoint itself, you enforce that any traffic passing through that network exit point must belong to the trusted organization/account. This prevents a user from using valid keys from a personal account to send data out via the corporate network. INCORRECT: "Configure the EC2 instance Security Group..." AWS Service endpoints (like Bedrock) use public IP ranges or dynamic internal IPs. You cannot filter access to specific "accounts" via Security Group IP rules. INCORRECT: "Use AWS Network Firewall to inspect the payload..." While Network Firewall can inspect SNI, inspecting encrypted HTTPS payloads for specific JSON parameters (modelId) is complex (requires TLS termination) and less robust than IAM/VPC Endpoint policies for account-level restriction. INCORRECT: "Update the IAM role attached to the EC2 instances..." This restricts who the EC2 role can call, but it doesn't prevent exfiltration if a malicious insider swaps the credentials on the EC2 instance (e.g., using aws configure with personal keys). The VPC Endpoint policy blocks the network path regardless of the credentials used.

refs.label: Use interface VPC endpoints

questions.question: An insurance company uses an Amazon Bedrock Agent to automate claim approvals. The Agent autonomously gathers evidence, checks policy limits, and makes a final "Approve" or "Reject" decision.For regulatory compliance, the company must provide a detailed "Reasoning Log" to the auditor for every decision, showing exactly which steps the Agent took, which documents it read, and the "Chain of Thought" logic it used to reach the conclusion.How should the developer capture this information programmatically?
options: Enable AWS CloudTrail Data Events for the bedrock:InvokeAgent API. configure CloudTrail to deliver the logs to CloudWatch Logs and parse the responseElements field for the decision logic.

Use the InvokeAgent API with the enableTrace parameter set to true. Capture the trace object from the API response stream, which contains the orchestration steps, model "thoughts" (CoT), and tool invocations.
Configure the Agent to use a custom Lambda parser. Inside the Lambda, log the input prompt and final output to Amazon S3. Use Amazon Athena to query the logs during the audit.
Implement Amazon Bedrock Guardrails with a "Transparency" filter. This forces the model to append a citation footer to the final response, which serves as the reasoning log.
questions.explain: CORRECT : Use the InvokeAgent API with the enableTrace parameter set to true. Capture the trace object from the API response stream, which contains the orchestration steps, model "thoughts" (CoT), and tool invocations. The trace capability in Amazon Bedrock Agents is designed specifically for transparency. When enabled, the agent streams back trace events that reveal its internal reasoning (Chain of Thought), the tools it decided to call, and the inputs/outputs of those tools. This provides the exact audit trail required. INCORRECT: "Enable AWS CloudTrail Data Events..." CloudTrail logs that an API call happened and who made it. It does not log the internal "Chain of Thought" or the step-by-step reasoning logic of the model during the agent execution loop. INCORRECT: "Configure the Agent to use a custom Lambda parser..."
