questions.question: A software company has 50 developers using Amazon Q Developer to accelerate coding. The engineering team wants to enforce company-wide coding standards, including specific naming conventions for AWS resources, required security patterns (e.g., always encrypt S3 buckets, use IAM roles instead of access keys), and architectural guidelines (e.g., prefer Lambda over EC2 for event-driven tasks). Currently, developers inconsistently apply these standards, leading to code reviews that repeatedly flag the same issues. The solution must ensure that Amazon Q Developer automatically suggests code that follows company standards without requiring developers to manually reference documentation or remember all rules.Which solution should be implemented?
options: Create a detailed coding standards document and store it in a GitHub repository. Add a link to the repository in the company wiki and Slack channel. Require developers to review the standards before using Amazon Q Developer. During code reviews, reference the GitHub document when standards violations are found.

Use Amazon Q Developer customizations to create an organization-level customization that encodes the company's coding standards, security patterns, and architectural guidelines. Configure Q Developer to automatically apply these customizations for all developers in the organization. The customizations will influence Q Developer's code suggestions to align with company standards without manual intervention.

Create a configuration file (.qdev-standards.yaml) that lists all coding standards and architectural rules. Store this file in each repository's root directory. Train developers to read this file before starting work and manually incorporate the rules when prompting Amazon Q Developer. Use pre-commit hooks to validate that generated code follows the standards.

Build a custom VS Code extension that intercepts Amazon Q Developer suggestions and post-processes them to enforce coding standards using regex patterns and AST transformations. Deploy the extension to all developer workstations via company software management tools. Maintain a rule engine that validates suggestions against the standards document stored in S3.
questions.explain: CORRECT: Use Amazon Q Developer customizations to create an organization-level customization that encodes the company's coding standards, security patterns, and architectural guidelines. Configure Q Developer to automatically apply these customizations for all developers in the organization. The customizations will influence Q Developer's code suggestions to align with company standards without manual intervention. Amazon Q Developer customizations is the purpose-built feature for this exact use case. Administrators can create organization-level customizations that encode company-specific coding standards, architectural patterns, and security guidelines by providing documentation, code examples, and rule descriptions. Once configured, these customizations are automatically applied to all developers in the organization through their Q Developer IDE integration - no manual setup per developer required. When developers use Q Developer, the AI model considers the organization's customizations alongside general coding knowledge, generating suggestions that already incorporate company standards (e.g., suggesting encrypted S3 buckets by default, proposing IAM roles instead of access keys). This eliminates the manual burden on developers to remember and apply rules, and reduces code review friction by preventing standards violations before code is written. Customizations are managed centrally, so when standards evolve, administrators update the customization once and all developers immediately benefit. This is the native AWS solution with zero operational overhead for developers and minimal overhead for administrators. INCORRECT: Create a detailed coding standards document and store it in a GitHub repository. Add a link to the repository in the company wiki and Slack channel. Require developers to review the standards before using Amazon Q Developer. During code reviews, reference the GitHub document when standards violations are found. This approach relies entirely on manual developer discipline and doesn't integrate with Amazon Q Developer at all. Developers must remember to check the GitHub document, manually interpret the rules, and explicitly prompt Q Developer to follow them - a process prone to human error and inconsistency, especially under deadline pressure. The GitHub document is passive reference material; it doesn't influence Q Developer's behavior. Standards violations are only caught during code reviews (after code is written), wasting development time and creating friction between developers and reviewers who repeatedly flag the same issues. This solution doesn't leverage any AI or automation capabilities and doesn't solve the core problem: preventing standards violations during the coding phase. It's the least effective approach and maximizes operational overhead. INCORRECT: Create a configuration file (.qdev-standards.yaml) that lists all coding standards and architectural rules. Store this file in each repository's root directory. Train developers to read this file before starting work and manually incorporate the rules when prompting Amazon Q Developer. Use pre-commit hooks to validate that generated code follows the standards. While configuration files per repository can work for repository-specific rules, this approach doesn't scale for company-wide standards applied across 50 developers and multiple repositories. Maintaining identical .qdev-standards.yaml files across all repos creates synchronization challenges - when standards change, every repository must be updated, leading to drift and inconsistency. More critically, Q Developer doesn't natively read or apply rules from custom .qdev-standards.yaml files - developers must still manually reference the file and explicitly prompt Q Developer to follow rules, which is error-prone. Pre-commit hooks catch violations after code generation, not during, so developers still experience the friction of writing code that fails validation. This solution adds repository maintenance overhead without providing the proactive guidance that Q Developer customizations deliver natively. INCORRECT: Build a custom VS Code extension that intercepts Amazon Q Developer suggestions and post-processes them to enforce coding standards using regex patterns and AST transformations. Deploy the extension to all developer workstations via company software management tools. Maintain a rule engine that validates suggestions against the standards document stored in S3. This solution is massively over-engineered and creates significant technical debt. Building a custom VS Code extension requires substantial development effort (JavaScript/TypeScript extension development, Q Developer API integration, AST parsing libraries, regex rule engines), ongoing maintenance (VS Code API changes, Q Developer updates, rule engine bugs), and operational overhead (extension distribution, version management, developer support). Regex patterns and AST transformations are brittle - they catch syntactic patterns but miss semantic issues (e.g., detecting "should use IAM role instead of access key" requires understanding intent, not just syntax). Post-processing suggestions after Q Developer generates them is reactive, not proactive - it's like autocorrect after typing instead of smart suggestions during typing. When AWS releases new Q Developer features, the custom extension may break, requiring emergency fixes. This approach ignores the native customizations feature that AWS built specifically to solve this problem, choosing instead to maintain a complex custom solution with high operational cost and lower effectiveness. Key Concepts: Q Developer customizations = organization-level coding standards enforcement Proactive guidance = influence suggestions during generation, not validation after Central management = update once, apply to all developers Native integration = no custom tooling or developer training required

refs.label: What is Amazon Q Developer?

questions.question: A legal discovery firm processes thousands of scanned legal documents daily for e-discovery cases. Each document (5-50 pages, 2-10MB PDFs) must be analyzed using a custom-trained SageMaker model that extracts key legal entities, clauses, and metadata. Documents are uploaded to S3 throughout the day in batches. Processing can take 30-90 seconds per document depending on length. Legal teams review results the next business day, so immediate processing is not required. The firm experiences variable document volumes (200-2000 documents per day) and wants to minimize infrastructure costs while ensuring all documents are eventually processed.Which solution provides the MOST cost-effective architecture?
options: Deploy the SageMaker model to a real-time endpoint with auto-scaling enabled. Configure S3 Event Notifications to trigger a Lambda function for each uploaded document. The Lambda function downloads the document, invokes the real-time endpoint synchronously, and stores results in DynamoDB. Scale the endpoint from 1-10 instances based on CloudWatch metrics.

Deploy the SageMaker model using SageMaker Asynchronous Inference. Configure S3 Event Notifications to trigger a Lambda function that submits asynchronous inference requests with S3 input/output locations. SageMaker automatically queues requests, scales compute resources as needed, and writes results back to S3. Configure SNS notifications for inference completion and failures.

Deploy the SageMaker model to a real-time endpoint with a single instance that stays always on. Create an SQS queue to buffer document processing requests. Use a Lambda function to poll the queue and invoke the real-time endpoint sequentially for each document. Implement exponential backoff when the endpoint is busy.

Use SageMaker Batch Transform to process documents. Schedule an EventBridge rule to run every hour that triggers a Lambda function to collect all new documents uploaded since the last run, create a batch transform job, and process all documents together. Store results in S3 and notify legal teams via SNS when each batch completes.
questions.explain: CORRECT: Deploy the SageMaker model using SageMaker Asynchronous Inference. Configure S3 Event Notifications to trigger a Lambda function that submits asynchronous inference requests with S3 input/output locations. SageMaker automatically queues requests, scales compute resources as needed, and writes results back to S3. Configure SNS notifications for inference completion and failures. SageMaker Asynchronous Inference is purpose-built for this exact use case. It handles large payloads (2-10MB PDFs) natively by using S3 input/output locations rather than passing data through API payloads, supporting files up to 1GB. Since legal teams don't need immediate results (next-day review is acceptable), the asynchronous pattern is appropriate - requests are queued and processed as compute becomes available. Async inference automatically scales compute resources based on queue depth, spinning up instances when the queue grows and scaling down to zero when idle, which is ideal for variable document volumes (200-2000 per day). You only pay for actual inference time, not idle capacity, making this significantly more cost-effective than keeping real-time endpoints running. The built-in request queue eliminates the need for custom queueing logic with SQS. SNS notifications provide completion tracking for downstream workflows. For long-running inference (30-90 seconds per document), async inference handles timeout limits better than Lambda-invoked real-time endpoints (Lambda 15-min timeout could be an issue for large batches if synchronous). INCORRECT: Deploy the SageMaker model to a real-time endpoint with auto-scaling enabled. Configure S3 Event Notifications to trigger a Lambda function for each uploaded document. The Lambda function downloads the document, invokes the real-time endpoint synchronously, and stores results in DynamoDB. Scale the endpoint from 1-10 instances based on CloudWatch metrics. Real-time endpoints with auto-scaling are designed for low-latency, interactive applications where users need immediate responses. For this use case, where results aren't needed until the next day, paying for real-time infrastructure is wasteful. Real-time endpoints have minimum instance counts (typically 1+) that run continuously even when idle, incurring costs during low-traffic periods. Auto-scaling has warm-up delays (minutes to provision new instances), so during traffic spikes (batch uploads of 2000 documents), the endpoint may throttle requests while scaling, causing Lambda retries and delays. Passing 2-10MB documents through Lambda (which must download from S3, then send to endpoint) adds latency and Lambda costs. Real-time endpoints charge per hour of instance runtime, whereas async inference charges only per inference request, making async inference far more cost-effective for variable, non-urgent workloads. This solution over-provisions infrastructure for requirements that don't demand real-time performance. INCORRECT: Deploy the SageMaker model to a real-time endpoint with a single instance that stays always on. Create an SQS queue to buffer document processing requests. Use a Lambda function to poll the queue and invoke the real-time endpoint sequentially for each document. Implement exponential backoff when the endpoint is busy. A single always-on instance is the least cost-effective approach. During low-traffic periods (e.g., weekends, nights, slow case periods with only 200 documents), the endpoint incurs costs despite being idle. With variable volumes (200-2000 docs/day), a single instance creates a bottleneck - at 30-90 seconds per document, processing 2000 documents sequentially could take 16-50 hours, which doesn't meet next-day requirements. While SQS buffering helps manage spikes, it doesn't solve the capacity problem - the queue simply grows while the single instance slowly processes documents. Adding exponential backoff when the endpoint is busy further delays processing. Scaling to multiple instances defeats the "single instance" cost optimization, and manual scaling management adds operational complexity. This architecture is neither cost-effective (always-on costs) nor performant (single-instance bottleneck), and it requires custom queueing logic that SageMaker Async Inference provides natively. INCORRECT: Use SageMaker Batch Transform to process documents. Schedule an EventBridge rule to run every hour that triggers a Lambda function to collect all new documents uploaded since the last run, create a batch transform job, and process all documents together. Store results in S3 and notify legal teams via SNS when each batch completes. Batch Transform is designed for offline, scheduled batch processing where you process a large dataset all at once (e.g., nightly model inference on millions of records). Hourly batch jobs introduce processing delays - a document uploaded at 10:01 AM won't be processed until the 11:00 AM batch job runs, adding up to 59 minutes of unnecessary latency. If a batch has only a few documents (low-volume day), you still incur the overhead of provisioning a Batch Transform cluster, running it for a short time, and tearing it down - this start/stop overhead is inefficient for small batches. Batch Transform jobs require manual triggering (via EventBridge in this case), whereas Async Inference responds immediately to requests without scheduling delays. For variable document arrival patterns (continuous uploads throughout the day), event-driven async inference provides better resource utilization and faster overall throughput than hourly batch processing. Async Inference is essentially "micro-batching" that handles each document as it arrives while still optimizing costs through auto-scaling. Key Concepts: SageMaker Asynchronous Inference = large payloads, no real-time requirement, auto-scaling, pay-per-request Real-time endpoints = low latency, always-on costs, synchronous responses Batch Transform = scheduled offline processing, not event-driven Cost optimization = scale-to-zero when idle, pay only for inference time

questions.question: A research institution manages a digital archive containing 500,000+ mixed-format documents: scanned research papers (image-based PDFs), handwritten lab notes (JPEG/PNG images), typed reports (text PDFs), and data tables (embedded in PDFs and images). Researchers need to query this archive using a Bedrock-powered Knowledge Base that can retrieve information regardless of the original format. The solution must extract text from scanned documents, recognize handwritten content, extract structured data from tables, and prepare all content for semantic search. The institution requires an automated pipeline that processes new uploads with minimal manual intervention and handles the variety of formats without custom code for each type.Which solution should be implemented?
options: Enable Amazon Bedrock Data Automation on the S3 bucket containing the archive. Configure Data Automation to automatically detect document types (scanned PDFs, images, text PDFs) and apply appropriate processing (OCR for scanned content, table extraction, handwriting recognition). Connect Data Automation output directly to the Bedrock Knowledge Base data source, which will ingest the processed, text-extracted content for semantic search.

Create a Lambda function that uses Amazon Bedrock Data Automation APIs to process documents based on file type. Configure S3 Event Notifications to trigger the Lambda for each upload. The Lambda determines document type (scanned PDF vs text PDF vs image), calls the appropriate Bedrock Data Automation processing method, and writes extracted text to a separate S3 prefix that feeds the Knowledge Base ingestion pipeline.

Deploy an Amazon Bedrock Data Automation workflow using Step Functions. When documents are uploaded to S3, trigger the workflow which orchestrates: (1) Bedrock Data Automation for format detection, (2) parallel processing branches for different document types (OCR for scanned, table extraction for structured data, text extraction for typed docs), (3) aggregation of results, (4) writing to Knowledge Base input location.

Configure Amazon Bedrock Data Automation as a preprocessing layer in the Knowledge Base settings. During Knowledge Base ingestion, Data Automation automatically intercepts documents, analyzes format types, applies necessary transformations (OCR, table parsing, handwriting recognition), and passes processed content to the chunking and embedding pipeline without requiring separate storage or processing steps.
questions.explain: CORRECT: Configure Amazon Bedrock Data Automation as a preprocessing layer in the Knowledge Base settings. During Knowledge Base ingestion, Data Automation automatically intercepts documents, analyzes format types, applies necessary transformations (OCR, table parsing, handwriting recognition), and passes processed content to the chunking and embedding pipeline without requiring separate storage or processing steps. Bedrock Data Automation is designed to integrate directly with Knowledge Bases as a native preprocessing capability. When enabled in Knowledge Base configuration, Data Automation automatically handles multi-modal document processing during the ingestion phase - it detects document formats (scanned vs text PDFs, images, tables), applies appropriate processing (OCR for scanned documents, handwriting recognition, table structure extraction), and outputs standardized text that flows directly into the Knowledge Base's chunking and embedding pipeline. This is a fully managed, zero-code solution: researchers simply upload documents to S3, and the Knowledge Base with Data Automation handles format detection, content extraction, and semantic indexing automatically. There's no need for intermediate storage, custom Lambda functions, or orchestration workflows. Data Automation supports the variety of formats in the archive (scanned papers, handwritten notes, typed reports, embedded tables) through its multi-modal processing capabilities, making it ideal for heterogeneous document collections. This architecture provides minimal operational overhead and seamlessly handles new uploads without format-specific logic. INCORRECT: Enable Amazon Bedrock Data Automation on the S3 bucket containing the archive. Configure Data Automation to automatically detect document types (scanned PDFs, images, text PDFs) and apply appropriate processing (OCR for scanned content, table extraction, handwriting recognition). Connect Data Automation output directly to the Bedrock Knowledge Base data source, which will ingest the processed, text-extracted content for semantic search. This answer describes an appealing architecture but misrepresents how Data Automation works. Bedrock Data Automation is not configured "on an S3 bucket" as a standalone service that monitors and processes files independently - it's a capability that's activated within specific workflows (like Knowledge Base ingestion or through API calls). You cannot "enable Data Automation on S3" and have it automatically process files and write outputs to another S3 location without additional orchestration. While the concept of connecting Data Automation output to Knowledge Base is correct, the implementation described (bucket-level enablement with automatic processing and output) doesn't match the actual Data Automation integration model. The correct pattern is to enable Data Automation within the Knowledge Base configuration (answer 4), not as a separate bucket-level service. INCORRECT: Create a Lambda function that uses Amazon Bedrock Data Automation APIs to process documents based on file type. Configure S3 Event Notifications to trigger the Lambda for each upload. The Lambda determines document type (scanned PDF vs text PDF vs image), calls the appropriate Bedrock Data Automation processing method, and writes extracted text to a separate S3 prefix that feeds the Knowledge Base ingestion pipeline. This solution adds unnecessary custom orchestration code when Data Automation can be integrated natively with Knowledge Bases. Writing a Lambda function to determine file types and call Data Automation APIs introduces operational overhead: custom code to maintain, file type detection logic to implement, error handling for various document formats, and management of the intermediate S3 prefix for processed content. The Lambda approach requires developers to understand Data Automation APIs and implement format-specific branching logic ("if scanned PDF, call OCR API; if image, call image extraction API"), when Data Automation's multi-modal processing handles format detection automatically. Additionally, this architecture creates a two-stage pipeline (Lambda preprocessing → Knowledge Base ingestion) that's more complex than the single-stage native integration. While functionally this could work, it violates the "minimal manual intervention" and operational simplicity requirements. INCORRECT: Deploy an Amazon Bedrock Data Automation workflow using Step Functions. When documents are uploaded to S3, trigger the workflow which orchestrates: (1) Bedrock Data Automation for format detection, (2) parallel processing branches for different document types (OCR for scanned, table extraction for structured data, text extraction for typed docs), (3) aggregation of results, (4) writing to Knowledge Base input location. This solution massively over-engineers the problem by introducing Step Functions orchestration when Data Automation's native multi-modal processing already handles format detection and appropriate processing automatically. Building parallel processing branches for different document types in Step Functions requires significant development effort: workflow definition, state machine logic, parallel execution coordination, result aggregation code, and error handling across multiple states. Data Automation is designed to be multi-modal by default - it doesn't require explicit branching for "if scanned, do OCR; if table, do extraction" because it intelligently applies the right processing based on content analysis. The Step Functions approach also adds cost (per state transition), latency (state machine overhead), and operational complexity (managing workflow definitions, monitoring executions) without providing benefits beyond what native Knowledge Base + Data Automation integration offers. This architecture is appropriate for complex multi-service workflows with conditional logic, not for straightforward document processing that Data Automation handles natively. Key Concepts: Bedrock Data Automation = multi-modal preprocessing (OCR, handwriting, table extraction) Native Knowledge Base integration = Data Automation as preprocessing layer during ingestion Format detection = automatic, no custom logic needed Zero-code pipeline = upload → automatic processing → semantic indexing

questions.question: A customer support system uses Bedrock to answer product questions through multi-turn conversations. The workflow must: (1) retrieve relevant product documentation from a Knowledge Base, (2) generate an initial response using Claude, (3) if the response mentions out-of-stock items, query an inventory API to get current availability, (4) generate a final response incorporating inventory data. The solution must maintain conversation context across turns and handle the conditional inventory check without requiring the application to orchestrate multiple API calls manually.Which solution should be implemented?
options: Use Amazon Bedrock Prompt Flow to design a workflow with nodes: Input node → Knowledge Base node (retrieve docs) → Prompt node (generate response) → Condition node (check if out-of-stock mentioned) → Lambda node (call inventory API if needed) → Prompt node (final response with inventory) → Output node. Deploy the flow and invoke it via a single API call with conversation history.

Create a Lambda function that orchestrates the workflow: call RetrieveAndGenerate API for Knowledge Base retrieval and initial response, parse the response for out-of-stock mentions using regex, call inventory API if needed, construct a new prompt with inventory data, call InvokeModel again for final response. Store conversation context in DynamoDB between turns.

Use AWS Step Functions to orchestrate a workflow with states: invoke Knowledge Base retrieval, call Bedrock model, use Choice state to check response content, conditionally invoke Lambda for inventory API, call Bedrock again with updated context. Pass conversation history through Step Functions execution input.

Implement the logic in the application code: maintain conversation history in memory, call Bedrock Knowledge Base for retrieval, invoke Claude model, check response text for keywords, call inventory API if needed, invoke Claude again with enriched context. Handle all orchestration within the application runtime.
questions.explain: CORRECT: Use Amazon Bedrock Prompt Flow to design a workflow with nodes: Input node → Knowledge Base node (retrieve docs) → Prompt node (generate response) → Condition node (check if out-of-stock mentioned) → Lambda node (call inventory API if needed) → Prompt node (final response with inventory) → Output node. Deploy the flow and invoke it via a single API call with conversation history. Bedrock Prompt Flow is purpose-built for complex, multi-step AI workflows like this. The visual flow designer lets you create the exact workflow described: Knowledge Base node retrieves documentation, first Prompt node generates initial response, Condition node evaluates whether out-of-stock items are mentioned (using LLM-based conditional logic or pattern matching), Lambda node calls the inventory API only when needed, second Prompt node generates the final response incorporating inventory data. Prompt Flows natively handle conversation context across nodes - you pass conversation history in the API call and it flows through the workflow automatically. The entire multi-step process executes via a single InvokeFlow API call from the application, hiding orchestration complexity. Flows are deployed as managed endpoints with monitoring, versioning, and no infrastructure to maintain. This is the AWS-recommended approach for complex conversational AI workflows requiring conditional logic and external API integration. INCORRECT: Create a Lambda function that orchestrates the workflow: call RetrieveAndGenerate API for Knowledge Base retrieval and initial response, parse the response for out-of-stock mentions using regex, call inventory API if needed, construct a new prompt with inventory data, call InvokeModel again for final response. Store conversation context in DynamoDB between turns. This solution requires writing and maintaining significant custom orchestration code in Lambda. You must implement: API calls to Bedrock, response parsing logic (regex is brittle for detecting "out-of-stock mentions"), conditional branching for inventory API calls, prompt construction with context management, error handling for multiple API calls, and DynamoDB read/write for conversation state. Every time the workflow changes (e.g., add another conditional check), you modify Lambda code, test, and redeploy. Prompt Flows provide visual workflow design where changes are configuration, not code. The Lambda approach also couples workflow logic to code, making it harder for non-developers (product managers, prompt engineers) to understand or modify the workflow. This violates the principle of using purpose-built services - Prompt Flows exist specifically to eliminate this custom orchestration code. INCORRECT: Use AWS Step Functions to orchestrate a workflow with states: invoke Knowledge Base retrieval, call Bedrock model, use Choice state to check response content, conditionally invoke Lambda for inventory API, call Bedrock again with updated context. Pass conversation history through Step Functions execution input. Step Functions can technically orchestrate this workflow, but it's not optimized for conversational AI patterns. Step Functions state machines require defining states in JSON/YAML, which is less intuitive than Prompt Flow's visual designer for AI workflows. More critically, Step Functions charge per state transition and have higher latency due to state machine overhead - for a conversational AI workflow that might execute in real-time during a customer support chat, these delays and costs add up. Step Functions are designed for long-running business process workflows (hours/days), not sub-second conversational turns. Prompt Flows understand AI-specific patterns like prompt chaining, context management, and Knowledge Base integration natively, whereas Step Functions requires custom Lambda tasks for these operations. Additionally, managing conversation context through Step Functions execution state is more complex than Prompt Flow's native context handling. INCORRECT: Implement the logic in the application code: maintain conversation history in memory, call Bedrock Knowledge Base for retrieval, invoke Claude model, check response text for keywords, call inventory API if needed, invoke Claude again with enriched context. Handle all orchestration within the application runtime. Implementing workflow logic directly in application code creates the tightest coupling and most maintenance burden. The orchestration logic is scattered across the application, making it difficult to visualize, debug, or modify the workflow. Every workflow change requires application code changes, testing, and deployment - even non-code changes like adjusting conditional logic or adding a workflow step. Maintaining conversation history "in memory" is problematic for distributed applications (multiple instances, load balancers) - you'd need session affinity or external state storage anyway. This approach also duplicates orchestration logic if multiple applications use similar workflows. Prompt Flows centralize workflow logic in a managed service where it can be version-controlled, tested independently, and reused across applications. The application simply calls InvokeFlow with inputs and receives outputs, decoupling workflow complexity from business logic. This solution maximizes technical debt and operational overhead. Key Concepts: Prompt Flow = visual workflow designer for multi-step AI logic Knowledge Base node = retrieve documents within flow Condition node = branching logic without custom code Lambda node = integrate external APIs/custom logic Single API call = entire workflow executes via InvokeFlow

questions.question: A financial services company is deploying a SageMaker model for loan application risk assessment. Regulatory compliance requires that the model be evaluated for bias against protected groups (gender, age, ethnicity) before production deployment. The company has a Step Functions workflow that orchestrates: (1) model training, (2) model evaluation, (3) conditional deployment to production if quality metrics pass. The bias detection step must analyze the model's predictions on a holdout dataset, generate a detailed bias report with metrics for each protected attribute, fail the deployment if bias exceeds acceptable thresholds, and store the bias report in S3 for audit purposes. The solution must integrate into the existing Step Functions workflow with minimal custom code.Which solution should be implemented?
options: Add a Step Functions task that invokes a Lambda function to perform bias analysis. The Lambda loads the holdout dataset, calls the SageMaker endpoint with test data, calculates bias metrics using custom Python code (comparing prediction rates across demographic groups), writes the report to S3, and returns pass/fail status to Step Functions for conditional deployment logic.

Add a Step Functions task that creates a SageMaker Clarify processing job for bias detection. Configure the Clarify job with the trained model, holdout dataset location in S3, protected attribute columns (gender, age, ethnicity), and bias metric thresholds. Clarify generates a comprehensive bias report in S3, and Step Functions uses the job completion status to conditionally proceed with deployment or halt the pipeline.

Modify the SageMaker training script to calculate bias metrics during the training process by analyzing prediction distributions across protected groups. Output bias metrics to CloudWatch Metrics. Configure Step Functions to query CloudWatch after training completes, evaluate metrics against thresholds, and conditionally deploy based on metric values.

Deploy the model to a SageMaker endpoint with Model Monitor enabled for bias drift detection. Configure Step Functions to wait 24 hours after deployment while Model Monitor collects baseline data and performs initial bias analysis. If bias violations are detected, Step Functions triggers an automated rollback. Store Model Monitor reports in S3 for compliance.
questions.explain: CORRECT: Add a Step Functions task that creates a SageMaker Clarify processing job for bias detection. Configure the Clarify job with the trained model, holdout dataset location in S3, protected attribute columns (gender, age, ethnicity), and bias metric thresholds. Clarify generates a comprehensive bias report in S3, and Step Functions uses the job completion status to conditionally proceed with deployment or halt the pipeline. SageMaker Clarify is purpose-built for bias detection and model explainability in ML pipelines. It provides pre-built algorithms for calculating industry-standard bias metrics (Disparate Impact, Demographic Parity, Equalized Odds) across protected attributes without custom code. The Clarify processing job integrates naturally into Step Functions workflows as a CreateProcessingJob task - Step Functions launches the job, Clarify analyzes the model's predictions on the holdout dataset, generates a detailed JSON report with bias metrics for each protected group, and saves it to S3. If bias metrics exceed configured thresholds, the Clarify job fails, and Step Functions' error handling can halt deployment and trigger alerts. The bias report includes statistical significance tests, visual charts, and detailed breakdowns required for regulatory audits. This is the AWS-recommended approach for bias detection in governed ML pipelines, providing comprehensive analysis with zero custom statistical code. Clarify handles the complexity of loading data, invoking models, computing metrics, and generating audit-ready reports. INCORRECT: Add a Step Functions task that invokes a Lambda function to perform bias analysis. The Lambda loads the holdout dataset, calls the SageMaker endpoint with test data, calculates bias metrics using custom Python code (comparing prediction rates across demographic groups), writes the report to S3, and returns pass/fail status to Step Functions for conditional deployment logic. Building custom bias detection in Lambda requires implementing complex statistical calculations (Disparate Impact ratios, conditional demographic parity, equalized odds calculations) that SageMaker Clarify provides as managed functionality. Custom bias code is error-prone - subtle bugs in metric calculations can lead to incorrect bias assessments, creating compliance risks. Lambda has memory and timeout constraints that may be problematic for large holdout datasets - Clarify uses distributed processing to handle datasets of any size. The custom approach also requires ongoing maintenance as regulatory requirements evolve (new bias metrics, different fairness definitions) - you must update Lambda code, whereas Clarify is continuously updated by AWS with latest fairness research. Custom bias reports lack the standardization and comprehensiveness of Clarify's output, which includes confidence intervals, visualizations, and multiple metric perspectives. This solution adds technical debt and compliance risk when a purpose-built managed service exists. INCORRECT: Modify the SageMaker training script to calculate bias metrics during the training process by analyzing prediction distributions across protected groups. Output bias metrics to CloudWatch Metrics. Configure Step Functions to query CloudWatch after training completes, evaluate metrics against thresholds, and conditionally deploy based on metric values. Calculating bias during training has fundamental problems. Bias should be evaluated on held-out test data, not training data - analyzing training data can give misleading results due to overfitting. Training scripts focus on optimizing model accuracy, not performing comprehensive fairness analysis - mixing these concerns violates separation of responsibilities. Computing bias metrics during training slows the training process and consumes GPU/CPU resources meant for model optimization. CloudWatch Metrics are designed for operational monitoring (throughput, latency), not storing complex bias analysis results with breakdowns by demographic groups - you lose the detailed audit trail required for compliance. This approach also doesn't generate the comprehensive bias report with statistical significance tests and visualizations that regulators expect. Bias detection should be a separate, dedicated step using specialized tools like Clarify, not bolted onto the training process. INCORRECT: Deploy the model to a SageMaker endpoint with Model Monitor enabled for bias drift detection. Configure Step Functions to wait 24 hours after deployment while Model Monitor collects baseline data and performs initial bias analysis. If bias violations are detected, Step Functions triggers an automated rollback. Store Model Monitor reports in S3 for compliance. SageMaker Model Monitor (with Clarify integration) detects bias drift in production - it monitors whether a deployed model's bias characteristics change over time as data distributions shift. However, the requirement is to detect bias before deployment as a gate in the deployment pipeline. Deploying a potentially biased model to production and waiting 24 hours for monitoring to catch problems violates the compliance requirement to prevent biased models from reaching production. If Model Monitor detects bias violations after deployment, customers have already received biased predictions, creating regulatory exposure and reputational damage. The 24-hour delay also blocks the deployment pipeline unnecessarily. The correct pattern is: use Clarify for pre-deployment bias detection (preventing bad models from deploying), then use Model Monitor for ongoing bias drift detection (alerting if a good model degrades over time). This answer confuses these two distinct use cases. Key Concepts: SageMaker Clarify = pre-deployment bias detection and explainability Processing job = distributed bias analysis on holdout datasets Step Functions integration = Clarify as pipeline gate before deployment Bias metrics = Disparate Impact, Demographic Parity, Equalized Odds Model Monitor = post-deployment drift detection (different use case)

refs.label: Post-training Data and Model Bias Metrics
Amazon SageMaker Clarify

questions.question: A scientific research institute is building a knowledge base using Amazon Bedrock to search through thousands of research papers. The papers vary dramatically in structure - some have short 200-word sections while others have dense 2,000-word methodology descriptions. When using standard fixed-size chunking (512 tokens), the system arbitrarily splits lengthy methodology sections in the middle of experimental procedures, while combining multiple unrelated short sections into single chunks. Researchers report that retrieved chunks either contain incomplete experimental procedures or mix multiple unrelated topics, making it difficult to understand complete research findings. The institute needs a chunking strategy that respects natural topic boundaries and keeps related content together regardless of length variations.Which chunking strategy addresses these requirements MOST effectively?
options: Semantic chunking - analyzes document content to identify natural topic boundaries and creates variable-size chunks based on semantic coherence, ensuring complete topics stay together regardless of length.
Standard chunking - uses fixed token limits with configurable overlap between chunks to ensure that content split across boundaries is captured in multiple chunks for redundancy.
Hierarchical chunking - creates parent chunks for document sections and child chunks for subsections, maintaining structural relationships between different levels of content.
Multimodal content chunking - processes documents containing mixed content types (text, tables, images) and creates separate chunks for each modality with cross-references.
questions.explain: CORRECT: Semantic chunking. Semantic chunking uses natural language processing to identify topic boundaries and semantic coherence within documents. Instead of splitting at arbitrary token counts, it analyzes content to detect when topics shift and creates variable-size chunks that keep semantically related content together. For research papers where a methodology section might be 2,000 words (needs to stay together) and a conclusion might be 200 words (shouldn't be merged with unrelated content), semantic chunking ensures complete topics are preserved. This prevents splitting experimental procedures mid-explanation and avoids mixing unrelated sections, directly solving the retrieval quality issues researchers face. INCORRECT: Standard chunking. Standard chunking is exactly the current failing approach - it uses fixed token limits (512 tokens) that arbitrarily split content without understanding semantic boundaries. While overlap provides some redundancy, it doesn't solve the core problems: lengthy methodology sections are still broken mid-procedure, and short unrelated sections are still combined. For documents with highly variable section lengths and semantic complexity, standard chunking creates the fragmented and mixed results that researchers are complaining about. INCORRECT: Hierarchical chunking. Hierarchical chunking is designed for documents with consistent nested structures (contracts with sections/subsections, manuals with chapters/topics). Research papers don't have uniform hierarchical structures - some use deep nesting, others are flat, and structure varies widely across papers from different journals. Hierarchical chunking wouldn't know how to handle a 2,000-word methodology section without predefined structural markers, and it doesn't address the semantic coherence requirement. This strategy assumes structural consistency that research papers lack. INCORRECT: Multimodal content chunking. Multimodal chunking is designed for documents containing different content types (text mixed with images, tables, diagrams) and creates specialized chunks per modality. The scenario describes a problem with text segmentation and topic boundaries, not processing different content modalities. While research papers contain figures and tables, the primary challenge is keeping semantically related text content together, not separating different modalities. This doesn't address the semantic coherence requirement. Key Concepts: Semantic Chunking = Variable-size chunks based on topic boundaries and content coherence Standard Chunking = Fixed-size token-based splitting with optional overlap Hierarchical Chunking = Parent-child chunk relationships preserving document structure Multimodal Chunking = Separate processing for text, tables, images Topic Boundaries = Natural transition points where content shifts to new subjects

refs.label: How content chunking works for knowledge bases

questions.question: A financial services company deployed a RAG application using Amazon Bedrock Knowledge Base to answer customer queries about investment products. The application is live in production, but the team has no visibility into which queries are failing or performing poorly. They need to track query patterns, identify retrieval issues, and analyze response quality metrics.Which solution provides comprehensive monitoring of Knowledge Base operations?
options: Enable Amazon CloudWatch Logs as the destination for Knowledge Base logging. Configure the logs to capture query text, retrieved chunks, confidence scores, and response metadata for analysis.
Create an Amazon EventBridge rule to capture Knowledge Base API calls. Stream the events to Amazon S3 and use Amazon Athena to query the execution logs.
Implement custom application logging in the Lambda function that calls the Knowledge Base. Write query details and results to a DynamoDB table for tracking.
Enable AWS X-Ray tracing on the Knowledge Base API calls. Use the service map to visualize request flows and identify performance bottlenecks.
questions.explain: CORRECT: Enable Amazon CloudWatch Logs as the destination for Knowledge Base logging. Configure the logs to capture query text, retrieved chunks, confidence scores, and response metadata for analysis. Amazon Bedrock Knowledge Base provides native integration with CloudWatch Logs specifically designed for this purpose. When enabled, it automatically captures detailed query information including the user query text, retrieved document chunks, relevance scores, source attributions, and response metadata. This provides complete observability without custom code and enables analysis of retrieval quality, query patterns, and system performance. INCORRECT: Create an Amazon EventBridge rule to capture Knowledge Base API calls. EventBridge captures API-level events but doesn't provide the detailed retrieval-specific data (chunk content, relevance scores, source attribution) needed for RAG performance analysis. It's useful for workflow automation but insufficient for monitoring retrieval quality. INCORRECT: Implement custom application logging in the Lambda function. This creates unnecessary overhead and maintenance burden. The native CloudWatch Logs integration for Knowledge Base already provides this functionality without custom code. Building a custom solution duplicates built-in features. INCORRECT: Enable AWS X-Ray tracing on the Knowledge Base API calls. X-Ray is excellent for distributed tracing and latency analysis but doesn't capture RAG-specific metrics like retrieval relevance, chunk content, or semantic search quality. It shows the request flow but not retrieval effectiveness. Key Concepts: CloudWatch Logs Integration = Native monitoring solution for Bedrock Knowledge Base Retrieval Metrics = Query text, chunks, confidence scores, source attribution Observability = Essential for identifying and fixing RAG performance issues

refs.label: Monitor knowledge bases using CloudWatch Logs

questions.question: A healthcare technology company is building a patient support chatbot using Amazon Bedrock. The chatbot will be accessed by doctors through a third-party medical records application. The company must integrate authentication with their existing corporate identity provider, ensure all access uses temporary credentials instead of API keys, and maintain detailed audit trails for compliance.Which solutions meet these security requirements?(Choose two)
options: Configure Amazon Cognito user pool with OpenID Connect (OIDC) integration to the corporate identity provider. Use Cognito identity pool to exchange authenticated tokens for temporary AWS credentials with IAM roles that grant Bedrock access.

Implement AWS IAM Identity Center with SAML 2.0 federation to the corporate identity provider. Configure permission sets with Bedrock access policies and enable CloudTrail logging for comprehensive audit trails.

Create IAM users for each doctor with programmatic access keys. Store the credentials in AWS Secrets Manager with automatic rotation enabled. Configure IAM policies to restrict Bedrock API access by IP address.

Deploy Amazon API Gateway with Lambda authorizer in front of Bedrock. Implement custom JWT token validation logic in the Lambda function to verify tokens from the identity provider before allowing API calls.

Use AWS STS AssumeRole with web identity federation. Configure the third-party application to directly call STS with tokens from the identity provider to obtain temporary credentials for Bedrock access.
questions.explain: CORRECT: Configure Amazon Cognito user pool with OpenID Connect (OIDC) integration to the corporate identity provider. Use Cognito identity pool to exchange authenticated tokens for temporary AWS credentials with IAM roles that grant Bedrock access. Amazon Cognito provides seamless integration with external identity providers via OIDC. The identity pool can exchange authenticated OIDC tokens for temporary AWS credentials with specific IAM role permissions for Bedrock. This eliminates long-lived credentials, integrates with existing IdP, and all API calls are logged via CloudTrail. CORRECT: Implement AWS IAM Identity Center with SAML 2.0 federation to the corporate identity provider. Configure permission sets with Bedrock access policies and enable CloudTrail logging for comprehensive audit trails. IAM Identity Center (formerly AWS SSO) is purpose-built for enterprise identity federation. It supports SAML integration with corporate IdPs, provides temporary credentials through permission sets, and includes comprehensive audit logging. This is the recommended AWS solution for enterprise SSO scenarios. INCORRECT: Create IAM users for each doctor with programmatic access keys. This violates the requirement to eliminate long-lived credentials. Access keys are static credentials that don't expire automatically. Even with Secrets Manager rotation, they remain long-lived credentials rather than the required temporary session tokens. INCORRECT: Deploy Amazon API Gateway with Lambda authorizer in front of Bedrock. While technically possible, this adds unnecessary complexity and custom code maintenance. Lambda authorizers require building custom authentication logic when AWS native services (Cognito, IAM Identity Center) already provide this functionality with better security, audit capabilities, and less operational overhead. INCORRECT: Use AWS STS AssumeRole with web identity federation directly. While STS AssumeRoleWithWebIdentity provides temporary credentials, it requires the third-party application to implement AWS SDK integration and token exchange logic. Cognito and IAM Identity Center abstract this complexity and provide additional features like user management, MFA, and centralized audit logging. Key Concepts: Temporary Credentials = Session tokens that expire (vs. long-lived access keys) Identity Federation = Integrating external IdP with AWS using OIDC or SAML Amazon Cognito = Managed service for user authentication and AWS credential vending IAM Identity Center = Enterprise SSO solution with SAML federation CloudTrail = Audit logging for all AWS API calls including authentication events

What is IAM Identity Center?

questions.question: An e-commerce company deployed a custom fine-tuned LLM on Amazon SageMaker for product description generation. The model runs on GPU instances with 6 GPUs each using DJL serving container with continuous batching enabled. Production metrics show that the company needs to provision many instances to handle peak traffic, driving up infrastructure costs. Performance analysis reveals that actual customer requests use sequences averaging 400 tokens, while the model is configured for 4,000 token maximum length. GPU memory profiling indicates the model weights and activations require only 3 GPUs worth of memory. Current batch concurrency per instance is surprisingly low.Which optimizations will improve GPU utilization and reduce the number of required instances? (Choose two)
options: Configure tensor parallelism with degree of 3 to split the model across 3 GPUs. This enables running two independent model replicas per 6-GPU instance, doubling throughput capacity.

Adjust the maximum sequence length parameter to 400 tokens to match actual request patterns. This reduces memory overhead per request and allows the continuous batching engine to process larger batches per GPU.
Enable pipeline parallelism to distribute model layers across all 6 GPUs. Configure the pipeline stages to process multiple requests simultaneously through different transformer layers.
Increase the instance size to instances with 12 GPUs each. Configure model replication across GPU pairs to achieve higher parallel processing capacity per instance.
Switch from GPU instances to AWS Inferentia2 instances. Migrate the model to use AWS Neuron SDK for optimized inference on purpose-built AI accelerators with better cost-per-inference ratios.
questions.explain: CORRECT: Configure tensor parallelism with degree of 3 to split the model across 3 GPUs. This enables running two independent model replicas per 6-GPU instance, doubling throughput capacity. Since profiling shows the model fits in 3 GPUs, using tensor parallelism degree of 3 allows deploying two complete model replicas on a 6-GPU instance (3 GPUs per replica). This doubles the request handling capacity per instance, improving utilization and reducing the total number of instances needed. Tensor parallelism splits individual model layers across GPUs for models too large for a single GPU. CORRECT: Adjust the maximum sequence length parameter to 400 tokens to match actual request patterns. This reduces memory overhead per request and allows the continuous batching engine to process larger batches per GPU. The model is configured for 10x longer sequences than production requests actually use (4,000 vs 400 tokens). Reducing max sequence length dramatically decreases memory allocation per request in the KV cache. This allows continuous batching to fit more concurrent requests per GPU, increasing throughput without adding hardware. INCORRECT: Enable pipeline parallelism to distribute model layers across all 6 GPUs. Pipeline parallelism distributes sequential layers across devices but introduces pipeline bubbles and latency. Since the model already fits in 3 GPUs, spreading it across 6 GPUs would waste resources rather than enable running multiple replicas. Pipeline parallelism is useful for very large models that don't fit on fewer devices. INCORRECT: Increase the instance size to instances with 12 GPUs each. This increases costs rather than optimizing existing resources. The problem is underutilization of current 6-GPU instances, not insufficient GPU count. Adding more GPUs per instance without addressing the root causes (sequence length, parallelism configuration) wastes resources. INCORRECT: Switch from GPU instances to AWS Inferentia2 instances. While Inferentia2 offers cost advantages, migration requires model compilation with Neuron SDK, testing, and potential model architecture changes. This is a larger architectural change rather than an optimization of the current deployment. The scenario asks for improving resource utilization of the existing setup. Key Concepts: Tensor Parallelism = Splitting model layers across multiple GPUs for large models Model Replication = Running multiple independent copies for higher throughput Continuous Batching = Dynamic batching that processes requests as they arrive Sequence Length = Directly impacts KV cache memory requirements GPU Memory Profiling = Essential for right-sizing parallelism strategies
